articles.title: Engagement Optimization in Large-Scale Video Platforms: A Systems Review of Metrics, Ranking, Experimentation, Latency, and Feedback Loops
articles.authors: Manoja Chebrolu
articles.abstract: Large-scale video platforms increasingly depend on engagement optimization to determine what users see, how content is ranked, and how platform value is measured. However, engagement is difficult to optimize because platforms rely on imperfect proxy signals such as click-through rate, watch time, completion rate, likes, and session continuation rather than directly observed user satisfaction. This paper presents a systems-oriented review of video engagement optimization across five interconnected layers: engagement metrics, multi-stage ranking architectures, online experimentation, latency-sensitive serving, and personalization feedback loops. The review synthesizes foundational and recent work on large-scale recommendation systems, controlled experimentation, tail latency, short-video engagement metrics, and feed auditing. The paper argues that engagement should be understood as an end-to-end systems property rather than only a modeling objective. It further highlights that ranking quality, serving responsiveness, metric design, and user feedback dynamics jointly shape observed engagement outcomes. The review identifies open challenges in long-term user-value measurement, performance-aware evaluation, feedback-loop mitigation, and commerce-specific video usefulness.

articles.title: "YES! YES! I absolutely love this insight!" Affirmative Narration as Interactional Strategy in Dialogues with LLM Chatbots
articles.authors: Hanna-Riikka Roine; Anne Sigrid Refsum; Jill Walker Rettberg
articles.abstract: This article analyses narrative mechanisms that are common in dialogues with LLM chatbots. In combination, these mechanisms produce an interactional strategy for maximising user engagement, which we call affirmative narration. Affirmative narration serves to convince users of the chatbot’s utility. We analyse three narrative mechanisms that support affirmative narration in human-LLM dialogues: firstly, guiding the user to view chatbot as an intelligent and reliable character; secondly, activating masterplots, culturally significant and recurring story templates; and thirdly, using characters and masterplots not only to affirm, but also to isolate the user. The case studies range from a journalist's unsettling chatbot experiment to cases where users have experienced delusions or even killed themselves after lengthy interactions with a chatbot. The analyses illustrate the worrying sides of affirmative narration, and the article thus concludes with the discussion of LLMs as a genre of fictional narrative media which requires a new type of literacy.

articles.title: Production of public quality television in the streaming era: Controversy, popularity, and social relevance at the Norwegian Public Broadcaster NRK
articles.authors: Maud Ceuterick
articles.abstract:
This article looks at how the streaming era transforms the notion of quality television and
high-end drama in the public and local setting public service media. It takes as a case study
the Norwegian broadcaster NRK’s extremely popular TV series Exit (2019-2023). I argue
that social relevance and edgy controversial narrative become two sides of the same coin
when looking at public broadcasters’ production or commissioning of high-end or quality
drama in the age of streaming. The article questions the concept of quality TV in a public
context through an integrated multi-method approach that combines textual analysis,
production studies through expert interviews with industry professionals, and a brief review
of cultural policy and press articles.

articles.title: The Moralization of Artificial Intelligence
articles.authors: Victoria Oldemburgo de Mello; Éloïse Côté; Reem Ayad; Yoel Inbar; Jason Plaks; Michael Inzlicht
articles.abstract: Resistance to artificial intelligence (AI) is widespread and persists even when AI is shown to be instrumentally advantageous. What explains this persistent aversion? Across four studies, we investigate whether moral reactions to AI—beliefs rooted in deeply held notions of right and wrong—explain resistance beyond pragmatic concerns. In Study 1, we analyze moral language in AI-related news headlines from a major US media corpus (COCA, 2018–2024). AI was moralized at levels comparable to GMOs and vaccines, technologies whose moral opposition has received considerable attention. In Studies 2a, 2b, and 3, representative samples of Americans reported attitudes toward several AI applications. Although few participants opposed AI outright (6–44%), most opponents indicated consequence insensitivity, quantity insensitivity, and universalism, suggesting moral motives for opposing AI. Structural equation models revealed that AI moralization was best captured by a single latent factor, indicating a generalized moral sentiment rather than domain-specific risk–benefit appraisals. Qualitative analyses further showed that moralizers typically invoked pragmatic language to justify their opposition. In Study 4, participants from Studies 2b and 3 were re-recruited 75 and 573 days later to complete a behavioral task. Earlier moralization scores predicted greater reluctance to use AI even when instrumentally beneficial—a one standard deviation increase in moralization corresponded to a 42% decrease in AI usage. Together, these findings demonstrate that AI resistance is partly moral in nature, suggesting that reaping the benefits of AI may require addressing moral concerns rather than relying solely on pragmatic arguments.

articles.title: Bayesian Networks for Benchmark Validity in Score Reporting: An ePIRLS Demonstration
articles.authors: Xuejun Ryan Ji
articles.abstract: Large-scale assessments often report achievement through ordered benchmark categories. In PIRLS and ePIRLS, benchmark cut points are derived from the achievement scale and interpreted through scale anchoring. This paper proposes a supplementary Bayesian network (BN) framework for examining whether reported benchmark classifications are supported by local item-response evidence. The framework is not intended to replace item response theory scaling, scale anchoring, or official benchmark procedures. Instead, it treats the benchmark category as an observed reporting classification and evaluates four forms of evidence derived from BN conditional and posterior probabilities: ordering, adjacent-level separation, subgroup transportability, and posterior support for assigned benchmark classifications. Using an ePIRLS 2016 Canadian reading-comprehension subtest with 3,006 students and 17 binary-scored items, the demonstration shows that item-response probabilities were broadly ordered across five benchmark levels, but that evidence varied by item, item type, process of comprehension, benchmark boundary, and language version. Retrieval and Evaluation items showed perfect mean ordering, constructed-response items showed stronger ordering and adjacent separation than multiple-choice items, Integration items showed the smallest average English-French gaps, and posterior support was strongest for middle benchmark levels and weaker at the extremes. The results illustrate how BNs can make benchmark evidence inspectable at the item, item-type, process-of-comprehension, boundary, and subgroup levels. The proposed framework is best understood as a diagnostic validity tool for benchmark interpretation and as a basis for more transparent score-reporting evidence.

articles.title: Disrupting the Mad Genius Myth: Emotional Instability Undermines Creative Self- Beliefs Through Lower Subjective Well-Being in Professional Artists
articles.authors: Florence Khoriaty; Arielle Bonneville-Roussy; Pier Luc de Chantal
articles.abstract: The relationship between emotional instability and creativity in professional artists has long been debated and is often framed through the “mad genius” narrative. Although emotional instability has been associated with creativity, it has also been linked to lower life satisfaction and greater psychological distress, suggesting that it may simultaneously support and undermine creativity-related self-perceptions. This study examined whether life satisfaction and psychological distress mediate the relationship between emotional instability and creative self-beliefs among professional performing artists. A sample of 201 professional performing artists completed the Satisfaction With Life Scale (SWLS), questions from the Generalized Anxiety Disorder Scale (anxiety) and the Patient Health Questionnaire (depression) to measure psychological distress, the Temperament Evaluation of Memphis, Pisa, Paris, and San Diego (TEMPS-A, for emotional instability), and a validated measure of creative self-beliefs. Confirmatory factor analyses and structural equation modelling were conducted. Results indicated that emotional instability positively predicted psychological distress and negatively predicted life satisfaction. Life satisfaction positively predicted creative self-beliefs, whereas psychological distress was not significantly associated with creative self-beliefs. The association between emotional instability and creative self-beliefs was fully mediated by life satisfaction. These findings challenge the “mad genius” narrative by suggesting that life satisfaction is the principal mechanism linking emotional instability to creative self-beliefs.

articles.title: Sex Differences in Overhand Throwing are Completely Due to Socialization: A Blank Slate “Hypothesis” Unconstrained by Evidence
articles.authors: Robert Deaner; Michael P. Lombardo
articles.abstract: Contemporary evolutionary biology–including evolutionary psychology–assumes an interactionist framework, recognizing the importance of both biological (nature) and environmental (nurture) influences. Despite this scientific consensus, within women’s studies and gender studies, socialization-only explanations of sex differences in behavior persist. This commentary evaluates a recent prominent example: Anne Fausto-Sterling’s 2025 critique of our 2018 Quarterly Review of Biology thesis that the large human sex difference in overhand throwing partly reflects an evolved male adaptation. Fausto-Sterling (FS) argues that our ancestral selection scenarios are unrealistic and that, based on a qualitative study of ten infants, childhood throwing differences are fully explained by early socialization. We demonstrate that FS’s critique fails in several ways. First, FS’s objections about our evolutionary scenarios rely on flawed characterizations of the hunter-gatherer literature, ignoring our primary focus on ancestral combat and discounting cross-cultural data showing far greater male involvement in hunting. Second, FS erroneously implies that childhood throwing performance is our sole evidence, ignoring extensive data presented on elite athletic performance, cross-cultural consistency, prenatal hormone exposure, and distinct skeletal sexual dimorphisms. Finally, FS treats evolutionary and developmental processes as mutually exclusive, failing to recognize that evolutionary theory predicts that socialization will often mediate and amplify evolved behavioral predispositions. We conclude that the persistence of FS’s socialization-only, Blank Slate perspective can only be maintained by ignoring relevant evidence.

articles.title: Does Teacher Praise Make Children Feel Less Smart? Two Preregistered Virtual Reality Experiments
articles.authors: Lena-Emilia Schenker; Jellie Sierksma; Milica Nikolic; Eddie Brummelman
articles.abstract: Does teacher praise make children feel less smart? When two students perform equally, teachers might praise the student they believe worked harder, implicitly conveying to that student that they have lower ability. We tested this hypothesis through two preregistered experiments in a novel Virtual Reality classroom with children aged 7-13 (Study 1: N = 212, Study 2: N = 225). After the child and a virtual classmate performed equally on a task, the teacher praised the child, the classmate, both, or neither. Contrary to hypotheses, there was no evidence that praise made children feel less smart, think that the teacher believed they were less smart, or experience elevated physiological arousal (heart rate, heart rate variability, blushing, skin conductance). Study 2 suggests that children instead viewed praise as a sign of being liked by the teacher. Our studies offer robust evidence that teacher praise may not make children feel less smart.

articles.title: Motives of social media use are differentially associated with mental health outcomes in adolescents
articles.authors: James Lian; Morgane Künzi; Jack L Andrews; John D Gallacher; Sarah Bauermeister
articles.abstract:
Background: Social media use is ubiquitous among adolescents, yet its relationship with mental health may depend on why it is used. This study distinguishes between avoidant-escapist and social-recreational motives for social media use and examines their associations with mental health, wellbeing, and resilience in a large cohort of UK schoolchildren.
Methods: Cross-sectional data were drawn from the BrainWaves study (N = 6820, Mage = 16.72). Social media motives were assessed using 10 items and derived using exploratory factor analysis (EFA) into two latent factors: avoidant-escapist (boredom, addiction, distraction, procrastination) and social-recreational (socialising, communication, entertainment, observation) use. Confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) was used to estimate factor scores, which were examined in linear regression models. Structural equation modelling (SEM) was then applied to estimate associations with anxiety, depression, wellbeing, and resilience, adjusting for gender and deprivation.
Results: In linear regression analyses, avoidant-escapist social media use was associated with higher anxiety and depressive symptoms, whereas social-recreational use was associated with lower levels of both outcomes. No interaction between gender and social media motives was observed. The SEM was a good fit to the data and showed that avoidant-escapist use was associated with higher anxiety and depressive symptoms, and lower wellbeing and resilience. In contrast, social-recreational use was associated with lower anxiety and depressive symptoms, and higher wellbeing and resilience. 
Conclusions: The associations between social media use and adolescent mental health differ by underlying motives. Avoidant-escapist use is linked to poorer outcomes, whereas social-recreational use is associated with more favourable outcomes. Focusing on why adolescents engage with social media, rather than overall use alone, is necessary for understanding risk and informing interventions. However, given the cross-sectional design, causality cannot be inferred, and associations may be bidirectional.

articles.title: A Conversation Guide to Support Color-Conscious Racial Socialization among White Parent-Child Dyads
articles.authors: Sylvia Perry; Jamie L Abaied; Allison Louise Skinner; Deborah Jean Wu; Sara Waters; Meghan George; Lauryn Hoard
articles.abstract: Family discussions may be an important context for shaping White children’s developing racial attitudes, yet the majority of White parents in the U.S. report that they either de-emphasize or avoid discussions of race and racism with their White children. The current study presents a qualitative analysis of racial messages communicated during a guided discussion task designed to scaffold color-conscious conversations about vignettes depicting interpersonal racism. Participants, recruited from a Midwestern U.S. city between 2018 and 2020, included 87 self-identified White parents (91.5% women, 8.5% men; Mage = 42.57 years, SD = 5.14) and their parent-identified White children (ages 8–12; 53% girls, 47% boys; Mage = 8.95 years, SD = 1.16). Most parents were highly educated, middle-class or upper-middle-class, married, and liberal or liberal-leaning. Findings revealed that many parents and children used racial language and endorsed color-conscious messages, such as acknowledging that racial prejudice had occurred in the vignettes. However, some dyads redirected the conversation away from race or made external attributions for racist behavior rather than attributing responsibility to White perpetrators. Together, these findings suggest that structured scaffolding may support more explicit conversations about racism in White families while also highlighting variability in how parents and children interpret and discuss racist behavior.

articles.title: What is it to be aware? A Pragmatic Approach to Comparing Animal Awareness
articles.authors: Nadine Meertens
articles.abstract: Efforts to understand animal abilities face longstanding conceptual and methodological challenges, especially regarding cognition and consciousness. Awareness has largely suffered a similar fate, having been subsumed into these discussions rather than examined on its own merit. This paper asks what it means to be aware. The proposal is to understand it as an organism’s abilities to selectively process, store, and use information in the service of goal-directed action. A pragmatic approach to awareness for comparative research is developed and briefly illustrated through an example of spatial awareness in desert ants.

articles.title: The Accommodation Trap: An Attractor Model of Plasticity-Driven Persistence in Delusion-Like Fixation
articles.authors: Muhammed Joghatayi
articles.abstract:
Delusional disorder is characterized by fixed, encapsulated beliefs embedded in a largely functional cognitive system. Existing computational accounts often emphasize aberrant precision weighting, anomalous salience, or atypical belief updating. Less specified is how a belief-like configuration persists after the initiating condition resolves.
We introduce attractor accommodation, an attractor network account of plasticity-driven persistence. When a belief-like element is held fixed, Hebbian-like plasticity may reorganize surrounding connections, so configurations incorporating it become lower-energy states. What begins as an externally maintained contradiction can become an internally supported attractor.
We implemented this in a Hopfield network with one clamped node and bounded Hebbian-like plasticity and report three findings. (1) Bounded plasticity creates persistent post-clamp fixation, with a sharp threshold (η_critical≈0.008 at α=100) above which all seeds remain trapped. (2) A weight-swap control isolates persistence to the plastic weight matrix: from the same accommodated state, the original matrix produced 100% recovery, the plastic matrix 0% (n=30). (3) The accommodated basin’s resistance to perturbation scales with η×α, and the weight changes building it spare high-connectivity nodes (r=-0.80); the state remains a single-node (1-2/N) deviation across N∈{50,100,200}. Findings replicated under weight-decay normalization and generalized across loads (P/N=0.06"-" 0.14); recovery depended more on accommodation depth and sustained focal duration than on perturbation structure.
The model is a hypothesis-generating prototype demonstrating that bounded Hebbian-like plasticity can produce attractor configurations that persist after their initiating conditions are removed, relevant to delusional encapsulation and treatment-resistant fixation.

articles.title: Predicting and Interpreting Psychological Distress and Mental Health Treatment Experience from Life Experiences and Everyday Behaviors
articles.authors: Takumi Kitahara; Masashi Mizuno
articles.abstract: The aim of this study was to examine whether psychological distress and treatment experience among university students can be predicted using machine learning models based on daily experiences and behaviors. Participants were university students who completed a web-based survey assessing psychological distress (K10 scores), treatment experience, and various life and behavioral factors. To reduce self-report bias, behaviorally specific and concrete variables were selected as predictors. Multiple models, including linear/logistic regression, Random Forest, and CatBoost, were trained and evaluated using nested five-fold cross-validation. The models explained approximately 30% of the variance in K10 scores, and classification performance reached an AUC of .77. Across both studies, key predictors consistently included seeking mental health information, persistent rumination over minor issues, and perceived lack of parental respect. Additional predictors included satisfaction with university entrance exam results and drinking frequency for distress, and experiences of bullying and family history of mental illness for treatment experience. These findings suggest that everyday experiences and behaviors may serve as useful indicators of psychological distress and help-seeking among university students.

articles.title: No evidence for predicting from conflict: A reverse cueing study reveals the limits of congruency-stimulus learning
articles.authors: Nicola Vasta; David Gallego; Luc Vermeylen; Elger Abrahamse; luis jimenez; Senne Braem
articles.abstract: The interplay between cognitive control, memory, and learning is as relevant as it is elusive. Despite broad interest in the notion that people learn to adjust control in response to cues, consensus on control learning is lacking. Previous research struggled to find evidence for cross-trial control learning when different levels of control were cued by stimulus features of the previous trial. We examined whether these inconsistencies arise from a general inability to prepare for control, or from an inability to encode control information within cross-trial associations. To test this, we turned around the cue-control paradigm by using a Stroop task where congruency levels (i.e., control demands) predicted target colors (i.e., stimulus features). Four independent experiments (n = 50; 125; 50; 56) were conducted. We found that people struggle to learn to benefit from congruency-color associations, with no clear evidence for faster reaction times on trials preceded by predictive congruency levels. Post-hoc analyses suggest that participants may have integrated individual congruency-color sequences into predictor-outcome episodes and retrieved these episodes when encountering a given congruency value, only facilitating performance when the same congruency-color sequence was repeated. Overall, our findings suggest that people are largely unable to use control levels as cues to predict task-relevant features across trials, indicating that task-generated conflict may not function effectively as a cue. However, we also offer a novel interpretative framework for researchers interested in investigating episodic retrieval in control-learning paradigms.

articles.title: The relationship between social media use and social isolation in young adults: a systematic review
articles.authors: Yiting Liu; Sojen Pradhan; Xin Wang
articles.abstract: Social isolation has become a growing public health concern, with increasing attention to the potential role of social media in shaping social connection and disconnection. This systematic review synthesises 21 empirical studies published between 2000 and 2024 that examined associations between social media use and social isolation among young adults. Following the PRISMA guidelines, studies were selected from five databases: PubMed, Web of Science, Scopus, EBSCOhost, and Google Scholar. Thematic analysis identified three major categories: (1) motivations and patterns of social media use, (2) Effects of Text-based and Visual Communication Channels, and (3) No Association Between Social Media and Social Isolation. While 15 studies reported a positive association between certain patterns of social media use and increased feelings of isolation, four identified potential benefits, and two found no significant relationships. Specifically, excessive or passive use without interaction was more commonly linked to negative outcomes, whereas purposeful and interactive use appeared to support social connection. Differences in platform specifics (e.g., text-based vs. image-based) and contextual variables such as the COVID-19 pandemic contributed to the variability of results. Most studies adopted cross-sectional designs and applied varied measurement tools, which may have influenced the consistency and comparability of results.

articles.title: Beyond Individual Resilience: Institutional Stressors, Burnout, and Coping among Baptist Pastors in Urban Ghana
articles.authors: McFaustine Kofi Mahama; Yaw Adu-Gyamfi; Gabriel Osei Forkuo
articles.abstract:
Background: Baptist pastors in Ghana face intense urban pressures, yet empirical studies on their occupational wellbeing are scarce. This research investigates burnout and institutional conflict, utilizing a novel socio ecological framework to uniquely link macro environmental pressures with denominational policy.
Methods: A cross sectional survey instrument was administered to 200 ordained GBC pastors in Kumasi. Using Python, descriptive statistics, Pearson and Spearman correlations, Cronbach alpha reliability, Kruskal Wallis, and chi square analyses were computed alongside qualitative content analysis. A novel four level conceptual framework mapped distal context, proximal stressors, and coping strategies.
Results: Church conflict was highest rated (M = 3.51, SD = 1.00; 71.5% agreement), followed by training gaps (64.5%) and work life imbalance (63.5%). Qualitative findings highlighted financial pressure, burnout, and family disruption. Mid career pastors (11-20 years) exhibited highest burnout, which did not differ significantly by location. Sabbaticals (55.5%), training (53.5%), prayer and fasting (52.0%), peer support (50.5%), and professional counselling (44.5%) were common coping strategies.
Conclusions: The novel framework reveals that pastoral distress is a systemic crisis driven by institutional deficits rather than individual vulnerability. Distal urban pressures interact with proximal stressors to generate distributed distress, which is only partially moderated by coping resources. By conceptualizing these dynamic, nested connections, the framework provides a comprehensive roadmap for GBC policy interventions, demonstrating that the denomination must establish funded mental health systems, reform mandatory transfer policies, and implement structured continuing education pathways to proactively address early career vulnerability and ensure long term ministerial health across sub Saharan Africa.

articles.title: The Experimental Study of Habitual Behavior in Humans: A Review of the Validity of Current Protocols
articles.authors: Pablo Martínez-López; Francisco Garre-Frutos; Miguel A. Vadillo; David Luque
articles.abstract: Although habits are widely recognized as playing a key role across psychological science, current laboratory methods may limit our understanding of these behaviors. Within the dual‑system framework, habits are defined as context-triggered, outcome‑insensitive responses that emerge gradually with repeated instrumental training. Many human experimental paradigms have been developed in an attempt to test this characterization of habits. However, in most cases, they have not provided clear evidence of habit formation, suggesting a gap between habit theory and existing methodologies. In the present study, we systematically review evidence on the construct validity of fourteen paradigms in which the amount of training was manipulated. We also reanalyze publicly available datasets from studies using these paradigms to assess the psychometric reliability of their main measures, thereby extending the available validity evidence. Paradigms that most closely mirror classical approaches derived from habit theory have consistently failed to induce habits, whereas more recent approaches have revealed training effects. However, these novel paradigms often rely on broader operationalizations that substantially diverge from traditional definitions of habit. Among the paradigms reviewed, the forced‑response task stands out as capable of inducing habitual behavior while providing a narrowly operationalized measure with acceptable reliability. Finally, we argue that strengthening the credibility of the field will require greater attention to the psychometric properties of habit measures, including precise operationalization, reliability, and systematic validity testing.

articles.title: Situational and longitudinal associations between motivational beliefs, anxiety, and learning during retrieval practice
articles.authors: Elisabeth Graf; Jakob Schwerter; Daria Katharina Benden; Thomas Dimpfl; Fani Lauermann
articles.abstract: Motivational and emotional processes are presumed to play a key role in shaping who engages in retrieval practice. Furthermore, students' success or struggle with retrieval practice may shape their subsequent motivations and emotions, yielding “feedback loops” in which these processes are mutually linked. Building upon situated expectancy-value theory and control-value theory, the present study examined a) concurrent intraindividual associations between control and value beliefs, anxiety, and engagement and achievement during retrieval practice, b) their reciprocal intraindividual associations over time, and c) whether students' aggregated motivational and emotional experiences predicted their end-of-term achievement and anxiety. In addition to an begin- and end-of-term questionnaire, students (N=636) participated and reported their motivational experiences weekly during retrieval practice sessions across a semester. Dynamic structural equation models revealed that control, intrinsic value, and utility value were negatively related to anxiety, while cost was positively related to students' anxiety during retrieval practice. Anxiety was negatively associated with engagement and achievement. Additionally, reciprocal within-person associations indicative of feedback loops emerged, with engagement and partly achievement relating positively to subsequent control and value. Students' aggregated achievement during retrieval practice positively predicted their end-of-semester achievement, whereas trait- and aggregated state motivational and emotional experiences showed no incremental predictive effects. However, higher aggregated state anxiety was associated with higher end-of-semester test anxiety. Overall, these findings suggest that retrieval practice exercises can contribute to self-reinforcing motivational-emotional cycles across the semester, pointing to a pathway through which momentary experiences during retrieval practice may accumulate into end-of-term differences in test anxiety and academic achievement.

articles.title: Modeling Decision Bias in dockerHDDM: A Systematic Comparison and Evaluation
articles.authors: Siyu Wu; Wanke Pan; Hu Chuan-Peng
articles.abstract: Decision bias refers to a systematic tendency to approach or avoid a particular option in two-alternative forced-choice (2AFC) tasks. With the advent of easy-to-use tools, diffusion-decision model (DDM) is widely used to distinguish two types of latent cognitive processes, bias in starting point or in efficiency, providing a powerful tool for understanding a variety of psychological phenomena across different fields. However, correctly estimating these two types of bias requires specifying the model to match the type of bias being investigated. So far, no study has systematically evaluated the different model configurations for modelling decision bias in HDDM, the most widely used python package for DDM. Here we evaluated nine model specifications using the three main modeling modules in HDDM across both simulated and empirical data. Our results show that the default function, which uses accuracy coding of data without flipping parameters, fails to recover either the starting point bias or the drift rate bias. Only model specifications that directly estimate the drift bias parameter, drift criterion (dc), or apply appropriate parameter flipping recover true bias parameters. By applying optimal specification to empirical data, we uncovered a novel psychological effect that went undetected in the original study. We provide concrete, evidence based recommendations for model specification and fully reproducible code. This work ensures accurate parameter estimation and the correct interpretation of parameter when modeling decision biases.

articles.title: Good Kitty, Bad Bank? Rescoring Miscalibrated CATs Improves Accuracy
articles.authors: NA; Benjamin Domingue; Nilam Ram; Michael C. Frank
articles.abstract: Computerized adaptive tests (CAT) depend on accurately calibrated item banks, but in practice, the item parameters governing deployment of the item banks are often based on small pilot samples, transported across populations, or assigned placeholder values pending proper calibration. We decompose the impact of miscalibration on subsequent ability estimates into two components: item selection error and scoring error. While selection error is not recoverable, rescoring using better calibrations, and without any additional testing burden, can potentially provide a post-hoc fix to scoring errors.  In a simulation study, we find that rescoring substantially reduces root mean squared error (RMSE), with scoring error accounting for 86 to 96% of miscalibration-attributable error. In Study 2, we apply this framework to data from 2,657 children (ages 5 to 12) assessed across three pilot sites in the LEVANTE project. Using identical scoring methods with old versus new item parameters, we found that rescoring shifted scores substantially and reordered children's relative standing; external validation confirms the rescored estimates are better, not just different. Low-ability and younger children are disproportionately affected by rescoring, underscoring the equity implications. These results highlight the utility of rescoring for any CAT using early-stage, transported, or AI-generated item parameters, and how rescoring can enable rapid deployment of assessments to new populations.

articles.title: The Entangled Loop: Emotion, quantum-like interference and hierarchy in the architecture of consciousness
articles.authors: Morten L. Kringelbach; Fernando Rosas; Ruben Eero Laukkonen; Shamil Chandaria; Yonatan Sanz Perl; Gustavo Deco
articles.abstract: We propose three entangled characteristics that explain the functional properties of consciousness: Emotion, quantum-like interference and hierarchy. The argument proceeds from thermodynamic first principles. Biological systems must stay far from equilibrium at minimal energy cost, and emotion is one of evolution’s heuristic solutions to this constraint. Valenced summary statistics compress the intractable decision space of survival into rapidly evaluable approximations. The same thermodynamic pressure selects for greater representational power at lower energy cost by promoting quantum-like inference – through interference in coupled oscillators across spectral gaps in structural connectivity – and hierarchical integration through a core group of orchestrating regions. Here we first derive emotion as the thermodynamic compression of adaptive decision-making and review the direct causal tractability of the distributed hedonic network through deep brain stimulation and optogenetic intervention. Second, we show how quantum-like inference follows from the same thermodynamic pressure demanding hierarchical orchestration, and we distinguish the physical mechanism of interference from the computational function of inference it makes possible. Third, we ask whether emotion is architectural rather than a weighted parameter within an otherwise neutral model. Support comes from recent mechanistic interpretability research on large language models, recovering the three features of two-dimensional geometry of mammalian emotion, quantum-like representations and synergistic hierarchy in human linguistic output. We argue that these signatures are the echo of the consciousness-generating architecture in the language it produces, not evidence that language models are themselves conscious. We draw out some of the consequences of this perspective for theories of consciousness, for the hard problem, for falsifiable empirical prediction and for the clinical restoration of flourishing. This convergent evidence supports our account of the Entangled Loop: A perspective on consciousness in which emotion is architecturally primary, quantum-like inference binds conscious content, and where the orchestrating regions coordinate both. This offers a principled route to rebalancing the affective architecture in neuropsychiatric disorders and to flourishing understood as meaningful pleasure achievable in the moment.

articles.title: From Retreat to Reality: A Qualitative Study of How Adolescents Apply Mindfulness One Month After a Weeklong Retreat
articles.authors: Jiaxiong Li; Brian Galla; Michael James Tumminia
articles.abstract:
Objectives 
Mindfulness programs are increasingly delivered to adolescents to help them navigate emotional and social challenges, yet little is known about how they apply these skills after program completion. While most research has focused on short-term, school-based programs and intra-personal outcomes, this qualitative study explored how adolescents integrated mindfulness into their lives one month after participating in an intensive, weeklong retreat. 
Methods
Fifteen adolescents (Mage = 16.74 years, SD = 1.58 years; 73% girls, 27% boys; 53.3% White, 6.7% African American, 6.7% East Asian, and 33.3% Multiracial) completed interviews one month after a six-day mindfulness retreat. Participants reflected on retreat takeaways and described how they applied mindfulness to personal and social challenges. Data were analyzed using a three-cycle coding process to identify patterns in intra- and inter-personal applications.
Results	
Adolescents described intra-personal applications, such as self-awareness and regulation (100% of adolescents; e.g., regulating thoughts and emotions), practicing self-compassion and acceptance (73%; e.g., being less self-critical), and gaining a broader perspective (60%; e.g., focusing on what matters). They also reported inter-personal applications, including navigating social anxiety and building connections (73%; e.g., using mindfulness to reduce social apprehension), engaging in compassionate communication (67%; e.g., active listening and providing emotional support), and greater social awareness and attunement (53%; e.g., practicing empathy and perspective-taking). All adolescents described experiences in both domains.
Conclusions
This study offers insights into how adolescents integrate mindfulness into daily life after an immersive retreat and suggests such experiences foster transferable skills supporting developmental needs. Implications for contemplative science are discussed.

articles.title: Defining Wellness: Conceptions of Ideal Mental Health in Ancient Yogic Texts and Contemporary Indian Views
articles.authors: Kriti Gupta; Jyotsna Agrawal
articles.abstract:
The prevailing modern operationalisations of positive mental health have been criticised for their inherent focus on sense-based, egotistical happiness. Addressing this concern, many contemporary mental health professionals have turned to indigenous ideas and classical philosophical texts to gain insights into optimal well-being. With the same aim, a cross-sectional, mixed-methods exploratory research was designed to uncover the meanings of ideal mental health among 240 urban adults (Male =136, Female = 104; mean age, 38.17 years) from North India, along with exploring themes of ideal mental health in selected classical texts from Indian psychology, i.e., The Bhagavad Gita (700 verses) and Patanjali’s Yogasutras (196 verses). Their content analysis revealed five broad themes: Sthitaprajñā (Steady wisdom), Anasakti (Non-attachment), Svadharma (Personal duty),  Shraddha (Faith), Ishwar Pranidhana (Surrender to the Divine/God). From the thematic analysis of subjective meanings, two major themes emerged: Describing ideal mental state as a positive mental state and Dismissive attitude towards ideal mental state. A notable similarity was found between the traditional, non-egotistical understanding of mental health and contemporary community aspirations in India. However, there were certain critical differences in opinions regarding the attainability of such goals. This research provides important contextual evidence that can help expand the framework of well-being.
Keywords: Ideal mental health, Yogic scriptures, well-being, Indian psychology

articles.title: Does Teacher Praise Make Children Feel Less Smart? Two Preregistered Virtual Reality Experiments
articles.authors: Lena-Emilia Schenker; Jellie Sierksma; Milica Nikolic; Eddie Brummelman
articles.abstract: Does teacher praise make children feel less smart? When two students perform equally, teachers might praise the student they believe worked harder, implicitly conveying to that student that they have lower ability. We tested this hypothesis through two preregistered experiments in a novel Virtual Reality classroom with children aged 7-13 (Study 1: N = 212, Study 2: N = 225). After the child and a virtual classmate performed equally on a task, the teacher praised the child, the classmate, both, or neither. Contrary to hypotheses, there was no evidence that praise made children feel less smart, think that the teacher believed they were less smart, or experience elevated physiological arousal (heart rate, heart rate variability, blushing, skin conductance). Study 2 suggests that children instead viewed praise as a sign of being liked by the teacher. Our studies offer robust evidence that teacher praise may not make children feel less smart.

articles.title: Adolescents value learning about a newly acquainted peer in a face-to-face interaction
articles.authors: Kathryn A. McNaughton; Katherine Rice Warnell; Sarah Nelson; Eva H. Telzer; Elizabeth Redcay
articles.abstract: Adolescence is a developmental period characterized by heightened importance of peers. Adolescents actively seek out opportunities to learn about peers, while discriminating between different peers in the learning process. Previous work has predominantly focused on how youth value learning about close friends, popular kids, or majority opinions, and has not yet investigated how adolescents may value learning about other sources of peer information, such as a peer they have just met. Here, we present findings from a novel behavioral task in which adolescents 11-15 years old had the opportunity to earn money as they made choices to learn information about a newly acquainted peer interaction partner (‘interaction peer’), the most common response of other adolescents their age (‘majority peer’), or trivia facts. Adolescents valued learning about the interaction peer over the majority peer and trivia facts, giving up nearly a penny for each opportunity to learn about the interaction peer. They also valued learning about the majority peer over trivia. Age and gender significantly interacted to predict heterogeneity in youth’s choices: with increasing age, male adolescents were increasingly motivated to learn about the majority peer, while female adolescents were decreasingly motivated to learn about the majority peer. Together, these results highlight gender differences in how peer information is valued across adolescent development. Characterizing how youth value learning about peers informs understanding of the way peers impact adolescents’ social worlds through relationship development and social influence.

articles.title: Distinct Neural Representations across Discrete and Continuous Non-symbolic Ratios
articles.authors: Rebekka Lagacé-Cusiac; Jessica Adrienne Grahn; Daniel Ansari
articles.abstract: Relative magnitudes, such as ratios and proportions, are crucial to everyday life. According to the ratio processing system (RPS) theory, ratios across different formats share a common neural code. Prior neuroimaging studies have shown that ratios across different visual formats are processed by overlapping brain areas, thus providing support for this hypothesis. However, overlapping activation found from univariate analyses does not necessarily imply common representations. The aim of this study was to probe the key prediction of the RPS theory by investigating the neural representation of ratios depicted in discrete and continuous magnitudes using multivariate fMRI analyses. Thirty participants completed a delayed ratio comparison task on discrete (sets of dots) and continuous (line lengths) magnitudes while in the MRI scanner. Using representational similarity analysis (RSA), we examined the similarity between ratio representations across high-level variations, such as magnitude type (length and numerosity), and low-level variations, such as variations in line orientation and dot size, throughout the brain, notably in parietal areas. Results did not support a common ratio representation across discrete and continuous magnitudes. Instead, ratios in numerosity and length seemed to be encoded in qualitatively distinct ways. Altogether, results suggest that ratios are not represented in a format-independent way in the brain, thus challenging the RPS theory.

articles.title: Beyond the Western Canon: Broadening the Foundations of Psychological Science
articles.authors: Leher Singh; Megan Pham
articles.abstract: Modern psychology has attended closely to the limits of WEIRD samples. At the same time, the field has paid far less attention to the foundational narrative which preceded and contributed to a WEIRD sampling bias. This paper connects skews sampling to a WEIRD philosophy of mind, expressed through two interconnected canons: an institutional canon that centers Euro-American laboratory psychology and a philosophical canon that incorrectly represents questions of mind as emerging primarily from a Western intellectual lineage. Drawing on transregional histories of psychological science and philosophies of mind, the paper formulates a broader institutional and philosophical canon and discusses the contributions of scholars, traditions, and intellectual lineages often left outside psychology’s standard narrative. The aim of this paper is to broaden the historical and conceptual foundations of psychological science and thus expand areas of inquiry and explanatory frameworks available to the field.

articles.title: Rated E for Emotional: Children's Understanding of Death, Conversations About Death, and Parental Predictors
articles.authors: Henrique Santos; David Menendez
articles.abstract: Understanding death involves integrating biological, spiritual, and affective concepts, which are shaped by watching media and interacting with parents. However, parents sometimes shield children from information about death because they believe the children are too young to understand it. We examined the alignment between children’s actual understanding of death and their parents’ predictions of their understanding. We also had dyads watch and discuss a short animated film depicting death to observe their conversations about death. We recruited 68 4- to 6-year-olds (31M, 36F, 1NB; 56% White) and their parents from the United States from April 2024 to May 2025. We found that children’s biological understanding increases with age, whereas spiritual and affective understanding remained stable across this age period. Parents tended to overestimate their children’s understanding of several biological and affective aspects of death, suggesting that parents’ shielding beliefs are not related to children’s actual understanding of death. Although most families mentioned death, their conversations were brief, showing that death conversations are more common and less in-depth than prior research suggests. Parents had an active role in the conversation and focused predominantly on affective aspects of death. These findings highlight that promoting in-depth conversations about death might require supporting parents.

articles.title: Optimizing Care for Older Transgender and Non-Binary Adults: A Co-created Agenda for Future Research
articles.authors: Mara Pieri; Nessa Millet; Martijn Anthonio; Alex Bakker; Maya Chew; Elia Covolan; Zowie Davy; Tim de Jong; Joost Derwig; Koen Dreijerink
articles.abstract:
Background
Despite a growing population of older transgender and non-binary (TNB) adults, evidence-based guidance for their care remains limited. There is a pressing need for a comprehensive research agenda developed in collaboration with, rather than solely about, this population.
Aim
This study aimed to co-create key areas and priorities for future research to improve quality of care for older TNB adults.
Methods
A four-day workshop was held with a group of 19 TNB individuals, clinicians, and academic researchers, representing diverse lived experiences, ages, occupations, and cultural backgrounds. A series of lived experience and scientific presentations were given and co-creation sessions were held to collaboratively explore the study’s aim. Concept mapping and design thinking principles were employed to generate and thematically organize insights during co-creation sessions. Data transcripts were then further analyzed using template analysis in consultation with co-creators.
Results
Three overarching themes were constructed. The first theme (reframing research best practice) offers guidance on future research conduct, emphasizing the importance of adopting participatory approaches, inclusivity, and the expansion of methodological perspectives to better capture complexity. The second theme (addressing knowledge gaps) highlights gaps in our understanding of gender-affirming medical care in older age and biopsychosocial influences on health. The third theme (practice-based research and knowledge mobilization) focuses on support for known challenges such as limited social support, best practice in elderly care, recognition and navigation of systemic power structures, and effective translation and dissemination of knowledge.
Discussion
This co-created agenda is intended to catalyze more responsive, inclusive, and equitable research and practice, ensuring that the voices and needs of older TNB adults remain central to efforts aimed at promoting healthy aging and quality of life in this population.

articles.title: A selection of environmental sounds for behavioural and neuroimaging research: Introducing the EnviSounds dataset
articles.authors: Magdalena Kachlicka; Jasper J.F. van den Bosch; Jian Kang; Frederic Dick
articles.abstract: With growing interest in ecologically valid stimuli and the proliferation of recognition and classification algorithms, environmental sounds are increasingly a topic of interest and study. However, there is a lack of consensus as to which sound categories should be selected, and which exemplars should represent these categories. Indeed, many existing datasets include only one exemplar per sound class. This is not characteristic of the actual acoustic environment and can lead to brittle and unrepresentative computational solutions and experimental results. Importantly, the existing literature provides relatively little information about how human listeners perceive the recognizability, similarity and representativeness of acoustically and perceptually varying sounds. Here, we introduce the Environmental Sounds (EnviSounds) dataset. It provides data from over 1000 human listeners performing different tasks (recognition, similarity judgement, goodness of fit) on 53 commonly encountered environmental sound categories of human, natural and man-made sounds, each represented by 10 different exemplars. We provide behavioural indices of within-class similarity and goodness of exemplar for all samples. Furthermore, we include normative data for recognition and identification latency, accuracy, and imageability, enabling researchers to select items for experiments based on pre-defined criteria across these dimensions. With this dataset, environmental sounds can be considered at various levels of complexity: sounds produced by the same sources varying in their acoustics (within-class acoustic variability) and acoustically similar sounds produced by different sources (between-category confusion). The dataset can be used for specifying training sets with individual sound sources, defining the categorical boundaries, or providing additional variables to building prediction models.

articles.title: Environmental Dynamics and Exam Stress among Nigerian  Adolescents: The Mitigating Role of Regional Community Support Structures
articles.authors: Ifunanyachukwu Daniella Ajaero
articles.abstract:
This study investigates the structural relationship between external environmental 
stressors, local community support networks, and examination-induced anxiety among 
secondary school seniors preparing for high-stakes standardized assessments in Nigeria. 
Using a descriptive quantitative survey layout framework, data was collected from a 
cross-sectional sample of N=95 Senior Secondary School 3 (SS3) students at Rochas 
Foundation College (Owerri, Imo State). Structural metrics focused on evaluating the 
mitigating capacity of multi-tier support structures, specifically comparing localized 
family communication channels and informal peer group interaction dynamics against 
standardized stress markers. Quantitative analysis revealed that peer support 
environments served as the primary self-reported buffer against exam panic, yielding a
dominant mean structural preference ranking of 1.38, followed closely by familial 
support networks at 1.69. Crucially, candidates lacking robust peer group integration 
exhibited extreme examination anxiety indices averaging 18.63 out of a 20-point max 
scale, whereas students with high peer-tier positioning dropped significantly to a mean 
stress index of 15.10, representing an empirical 18.91% buffer in psychological stress 
resilience. Lived-experience frameworks were integrated utilizing existential coping
metrics to evaluate individual cognitive adaptation under pressure. The findings suggest 
that structural implementation of peer-led learning circles within secondary academic 
institutions offers a low-cost, high-impact behavioral intervention to optimize student 
mental health during high-pressure educational transitions.

articles.title: Behavioral Emotion Regulation in Internalizing Disorders: A Transdiagnostic Psychometric Evaluation of the German Behavioral Emotion Regulation Questionnaire (BERQ)
articles.authors: Sarah Aline Wellan; Sarah Wyka; Till Langhammer; Gina-Isabelle Henze; Lars Schulze; Lydia Fehm; Norbert Kathmann; Babette Renneberg; Shuyan Liu; Susanne Erk
articles.abstract:
Background
Behavioral emotion regulation (ER) is conceptually distinct from cognitive ER, yet dedicated instruments to assess behavioral strategies are limited and rarely validated in clinical samples. Both ER domains relate to of the psychopathology of internalizing disorders, with potentially different contributions across diagnoses. 

Aims
This study evaluates the psychometric properties of the German version of the Behavioral Emotion Regulation Questionnaire (BERQ), including its factorial structure, reliability, construct validity, and measurement invariance across gender and three diagnostic groups: unipolar depression, anxiety disorders, and obsessive-compulsive disorder.

Methods
Baseline data from N = 462 outpatients with internalizing disorders prior to cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) were analyzed. Confirmatory factor analyses with subsample cross-validation, as well as measurement invariance testing and correlational and regression analyses, were conducted to evaluate the BERQ and its associations with clinical measures and other ER measures.

Results
The five-factor structure of the BERQ was supported, with good internal consistency and measurement invariance across gender and diagnostic groups. Several behavioral strategies were significantly associated with depressive symptoms and global symptom severity, with withdrawal showing the most pronounced effects. Behavioral ER explained additional variance in symptom severity beyond cognitive ER strategies. Latent mean differences indicated clinically relevant differences across diagnostic groups. 

Conclusions
The findings support the German BERQ as a reliable and valid instrument for assessing behavioral ER in clinical populations and highlight the relevance of behavioral strategies for understanding the psychopathology of internalizing disorders. Improved measurement of behavioral ER may therefore contribute to advances in targeted interventions and clinical research.

articles.title: A motor basis for a foreign accent?  Linguistic context constrains the generalization of second language speech motor plans
articles.authors: Lindsay Heyland; Samantha Palatinus; Douglas M Shiller; Daniel Robert Lametti
articles.abstract: Most second-language learners never achieve native-like speech, even after years of immersion. Contemporary models of second language speech attribute a foreign accent to interference between first and second language speech-sound representations. But difficulties in second language speech may also relate to limits on the adaptability of second language speech motor plans. Here, we test this hypothesis using sensorimotor adaptation to altered auditory feedback. Forty-five first-language English, second-language French bilinguals adapted their vowel production to compensate for a real-time formant alteration during the fluid production of English and French sentences. Critically, the formant alteration was equal in magnitude but opposite in direction between languages; to compensate for the opposing alterations, bilinguals acquired distinct sensorimotor adaptations in each language. We then examined the extent to which these language-specific adaptations might generalize to new linguistic contexts that systematically varied in their degree of syntactic similarity with the adapted sentences. Despite equivalent initial adaptation in English and French, second language (French) motor adaptations showed less generalization to new syntactic contexts than their first language (English) counterparts. The results reveal a motor constraint on second language speech. Compared to speech in one’s first language, second language speech motor representations generalize poorly beyond the linguistic context in which they are acquired—a deficit in motor control that may contribute to the persistence of a foreign accent.

articles.title: Metacontingency Analysis of Practices Associated to Mental Health
articles.authors: Blanca Patricia Ballesteros de Valderrama; Monica Novoa Gomez
articles.abstract:
This article proposes an analysis of mental health and psychotherapy as a result of cultural practices, based on contextual behavioral science, which combines behavioral analysis and cultural analysis. Similarly, it proposes complementing the analysis of public policy and other regulatory instruments with analytical tools from this scientific perspective, such as metacontingencies and macrocontingencies, which allow us to understand a policy or any regulatory instrument as a metacontingency system capable of constructing cultural practices related to mental health and facilitate the analysis of molar and molecular factors in psychotherapy. Reference is made to the case of Colombia as a country with particular conditions that could be addressed with an integrated model. Finally, methodological challenges and implications are addressed.
Keywords: metacontingencies; macrocontingencies; cultural practices; psychotherapy; public policy and mental health.

articles.title: Mind Wandering During Rest, Evoked by Preferred Music, Does Not Impede Short-Term Memory Consolidation
articles.authors: Juan Felipe Ariza; Juan David Leongómez
articles.abstract: Research has shown that post-learning rest periods are beneficial for memory consolidation. These same periods of rest are also prone to evoke mind wandering (MW) episodes which, when prevalent during learning, have been observed to prevent consolidation. However, although research has focused on characterising the role of MW during encoding, less is known about its role on consolidation when occurring during post-learning rest periods. Using the caught probe paradigm, we measured the effect of the prevalence of MW episodes during two conditions of post-learning rest, in silence and with preferred music, over performance in a recognition and classification task. We found that MW episodes during neither type of rest are predictive of performance in the classification task, even though participants are predicted to perform above chance. We additionally found that participants are predicted to perform above chance in the recognition task after rest with music, but no performance benefit is predicted from MW episodes occurring during rest with music; and that the prevalence of MW episodes interacts with rest in silence to predict performance in the recognition task, whereby a higher number of MW episodes predicted higher performance.  Together, these results offer empirical support to recent theoretical proposals that attribute MW episodes, and spontaneous thought more broadly, a relevant role on memory consolidation.

articles.title: Social media and smartphone use are associated with blunted striatal reward response in early adolescence
articles.authors: Amrita Lamba; Matt R. Nassar; Oriel FeldmanHall
articles.abstract: Adolescents spend unprecedented time on smartphones, raising concerns that screen time, and social media use in particular, may negatively impact developing reward circuitry in the brain—the very system critical for motivation and flexible learning. We tested whether social media and overall smartphone use are associated with altered risk-taking, neural responsivity, and mental health outcomes in early adolescence. Twenty-nine participants (ages 13-15) completed a reinforcement learning gambling task while undergoing fMRI. We fit a computational model to estimate trial-wise reward prediction errors (RPEs) during a gambling task and quantified striatal RPE sensitivity, a canonical neural signature of dopaminergic reward learning. We also measured time spent on social media applications and total daily smartphone use. Adolescents averaged nearly 6 hours of screen time per day and exhibited strong preferences for the social media apps TikTok and Snapchat. Greater social media use predicted increased gambling behavior, indicating increased reward-seeking behavior. Increased screen time and social media use were associated with blunted striatal activity, suggesting a reduced neural responsiveness to rewards. App-specific analyses indicate that these diminished striatal responses were largely driven by TikTok use. Finally, smartphone use was associated with greater anxiety, depression, and intolerance of uncertainty, which were also related to reduced striatal sensitivity. These findings provide evidence consistent with an emerging maladaptive profile that links technology exposure, mental health, and reward circuit function.

articles.title: Object perception encodes mutually exclusive possibilities
articles.authors: Gabor Brody; Peter Mazalik; Roman Feiman
articles.abstract: Human adults represent and reason about possibilities: multiple, mutually exclusive, non-actual ways the world could be. It is debated whether this is a late-developing, human-specific capacity and whether it requires natural language. We propose that instead of a unitary high-level capacity, multiple cognitive capacities might independently represent possibilities in different ways and with different properties. Our case study is object perception, a capacity shared with infants and non-human animals. Using methods that tap specifically into visual processes, across four experiments (N = 160), we found possibility representations in vision tasks that do not involve high-level reasoning or language. In Experiments 1 and 2, we found evidence for the visual encoding of possible object identities: When there were two possible ways that features might map onto visual objects, both possible mappings were primed. In Experiment 3, we found that visual feature-binding is a necessary precondition for such priming, ruling out low-level associative and high-level reasoning-based alternatives. Finally, in Experiment 4, we discovered that the visual system cannot encode one visual object possibly being at two possible locations. This pattern helps to explain a host of previous findings from the comparative and developmental literature and suggests that the unit of analysis for possibility representations should be the cognitive capacity rather than the agent.

articles.title: Common Humanity and Resilience: A Scoping Review
articles.authors: Sarah McAllister; Milan Le; Kiera Young; Patrick R. Labelle; Jess Whitley
articles.abstract:
Objectives: Social support and self-compassion are well-established predictors of resilience across diverse populations and contexts. While the associations between resilience and both social support and self-compassion are well documented in the literature, common humanity—a subcomponent of self-compassion related to social support that involves recognizing the universality of painful experiences—has received comparatively little attention in resilience research. Accordingly, a scoping review was conducted to map the empirical literature on common humanity and resilience, synthesize their associations, and examine definitions and conceptualizations of both constructs.
Methods: To identify studies, a search strategy was developed and executed across seven databases. The titles and abstracts as well as the full texts of emerging publications were independently screened by three reviewers. Data from 40 studies that met the inclusion criteria by consensus were extracted and summarized.
Results: Among the 4,353 references identified, findings from 40 included studies indicate a consistent positive relationship between common humanity and resilience, with higher common humanity associated with more effective coping, adaptation, and recovery from adversity. Regardless of the definitions and conceptualizations of both common humanity and resilience—as well as differences in geographic location, research type, study population, sample size, or form of adversity under investigation—common humanity consistently evidenced a robust association with resilience. Nevertheless, this relationship varied depending on the constructs examined, the form of adversity studied, the underlying mechanisms, and the conceptualizations of common humanity. These results elucidate the association between common humanity and resilience while identifying the factors that shape this relationship.
Conclusions: This scoping review advances knowledge in the field by identifying conceptual and methodological gaps, offering a foundation for future research, and informing the development of interventions that draw on common humanity to enhance resilience.
Preregistration A review protocol was developed a priori and registered on the Open Science Framework (https://osf.io/rby8e/).
Keywords: Common humanity, resilience, adaptation, adversity, scoping review

articles.title: Planning paths not taken: False memories for unexecuted actions
articles.authors: Maia Juliet Armstrong; Daniel Yon; Silvia Seghezzi
articles.abstract: Action planning involves representing multiple possible actions, including alternatives that are ultimately not taken. While such representations support flexible behaviour, they may also bias memory for one's own actions. Participants (N = 24) performed a goal-directed maze navigation task in which they planned routes with multiple alternatives before executing a chosen path, followed by a recognition memory test. On some trials, an available path was blocked after planning, forcing participants to revise their plan. False memories were higher for paths that were blocked than for unchosen but still-available alternatives, and this effect was strongest when the blocked path was the shortest available route. This pattern suggests that the depth to which a route is incorporated into an action plan determines the strength of its subsequent mnemonic trace. A second experiment (N = 33) confirmed that the effect requires prior plannability and cannot be explained by the visual salience of the block alone. Together, these findings reveal a direct link between action planning and memory distortion for one's own actions.

articles.title: SCEIMA: Social Coordination Evaluation through Integrated Model Analysis
articles.authors: Bavo Van Kerrebroeck; Caroline Palmer; Guillaume Dumas; Alexander Pantelis Demos
articles.abstract: Computational models are increasingly used as interactive partners in studies of human coordination, yet it remains unclear whether observed differences in human behavior reflect properties of the models themselves, changes in human behavior elicited by such artificial partners, or both. We introduce SCEIMA (Social Coordination Evaluation through Integrated Model Analysis), a two-stage framework designed to disentangle human-specific, model-specific, and interaction-driven contributions to coordination in human–machine interaction paradigms. In the empirical stage, human participants perform a coordination task with both human partners and computational models, establishing reference human–human and human–model interaction patterns. In the analytical stage, the same models are paired with one another and optimized through simulations to reproduce empirical coordination metrics. Comparing human–human, human–model, and simulated model–model interactions reveals whether coordination differences arise from intrinsic model dynamics, from human adaptation to artificial partners, or from their interaction. SCEIMA treats computational models as contrastive instruments whose capacity to elicit and reproduce human behavior can be systematically evaluated. We illustrate the framework with two distinct case-studies, a sensorimotor synchronization task and a conversational turn-taking task, showing how distinct outcome patterns diagnose the sources of coordination differences. By providing a principled methodological framework for evaluating interactive computational models, SCEIMA improves interpretability in human–machine interaction research and informs the design of artificial agents that coordinate with humans more naturally and responsively.

articles.title: Silent and Underestimated Climate Change Concern Without Behavioural Backlash: Findings From a Representative Dutch Survey on Climate Change Communication
articles.authors: Sandra Klaperski-van der Wal; Erin Rose Newman-Grigg; Esther K. Papies
articles.abstract:
Notwithstanding the scientific evidence and widespread concern about climate change, insufficient action is being taken to mitigate it. The misperceived social norm that others do not care about climate change—conceptualized as pluralistic ignorance—and a lack of interpersonal communication about topics related to climate change are seen as possible inhibitors of climate action and pro-environmental behavior. However, empirical evidence on the relationship between pluralistic ignorance, climate change communication, and pro-environmental behavior is sparse.
We surveyed a representative Dutch sample (N = 926). Most participants talked about climate change-related topics with friends and family less than once per month or “occasionally”. Using one-sample t-tests we found empirical support for pluralistic ignorance, with participants underestimating levels of climate change importance, worry, and felt individual responsibility. Regression analyses showed that hearing others talk about climate change negatively predicted pluralistic ignorance, while talking more often with people who share one’s opinion positively predicted pluralistic ignorance. Contrary to our expectations, participants reporting higher levels of pluralistic ignorance engaged in more pro-environmental behavior. An additional investigation of a small sample of climate change communication experts (N = 56) furthermore demonstrated the existence of pluralistic ignorance in individuals talking frequently about climate change. 
Our findings suggest that most adults in the Netherlands talk about climate change infrequently and that engaging in these conversations is not necessarily linked to a more accurate picture of climate change concern levels. At the same time, pluralistic ignorance did not seem to be linked to the expected problematic collective bystander effect.

articles.title: Effectiveness and uptake of ROOM to grow: Results of a randomized controlled trial
articles.authors: Tajda Laure; Marilisa Boffo; Stefan Konigorski; Djameela Dulloo; Rutger Engels; Danielle Remmerswaal
articles.abstract:
Background
One in four university students face mental health challenges, underscoring the need for effective support systems. Within Erasmus University Rotterdam student wellbeing initiative, we developed a mobile intervention providing universal and targeted preventive support, focusing on emotion regulation to address common issues like depression and anxiety. This study reports the results of a superiority Randomized Controlled Trial testing the intervention impact and uptake.
Methods
Students (N = 202) were randomized to immediate ROOM app access (n = 101) or a 3-month waitlist control (n = 101); 171 (85%) completed the trial. Questionnaires were completed at baseline and weeks 3, 8, and 12, alongside continuous in-app activity logs. Primary outcomes were perceived stress and emotion regulation; secondary outcomes included depressive and anxiety symptoms, resilience, and objective and subjective engagement. Analyses followed an intent-to-treat approach, with exploratory subgroup analyses assessing differences by engagement level.
Results
The intervention showed no statistically significant impact on any outcomes. Subgroup analyses suggested that highly engaged users experienced reduced stress, while moderately engaged users showed improved resilience but elevated depressive symptoms. Engagement was initially high but declined sharply after week 1, stabilizing by week 6. More than half of participants still used the intervention at day 30. Breathing and relaxation exercises were favoured, whereas transfer-elements and self-assessments were used less than expected.
Conclusions
 ROOM showed limited overall impact but yielded benefits for highly engaged users. Future refinement should focus on identifying how much and what type of engagement is needed for the app to effectively support students.

articles.title: Transitions in Teachers’ Technology Utilization: Evidence from a Large-Scale, Longitudinal, and International Panel Study
articles.authors: Evi Konstantinidou; Ronny Scherer
articles.abstract: Teachers’ technology utilization varies substantially within and across educational systems, yet its longitudinal dynamics remain poorly understood. Using data from the International Computer and Information Literacy Study (ICILS) 2018-2020 Teacher Panel Study covering Denmark, Finland, and Uruguay, this study examined latent profiles of teachers’ ICT utilization, their stability and change over time, and associations with teacher characteristics. Profiles ranged from minimally engaged to comprehensive integrators, with intermediate profiles reflecting selective or functionally embedded ICT use. Two theoretically important profiles exhibited a paradoxical combination of positive ICT attitudes and low classroom practice, consistent with second-order barriers to technology integration. Profile stability varied cross-nationally, with Finland showing the greatest stability and Uruguay exhibiting a consolidation of profiles between occasions. ICT self-efficacy and collaborative ICT use were the most consistent predictors of lower-engagement profile membership. Our findings highlight cross-national heterogeneity in ICT utilization profiles and a persistent attitude-practice gap across educational systems. Effective professional development should target barriers preventing attitudinally committed teachers from enacting ICT in practice, with collaborative teaching cultures as a key enabling condition.

articles.title: Measurement of American adolescents’ experiences of online harms: A nationally representative survey
articles.authors: Angela Yuson Lee; Carmen Hui Jing Lim; Xun Liu; Kang-Xing Jin; Anneke Buffone; Ravi Iyer; Zachary M Rausch; Jonathan Haidt; Nathanael Fast; Jeffrey Hancock
articles.abstract:
Importance: Concerns about the risks adolescents face online are rising, but existing research on online harms typically examines limited outcomes, rarely allows for platform-specific comparison, and lacks practical standardized measurement that would be suitable for public health monitoring.
Objective: We introduce and implement a measurement approach estimating the prevalence of negative experiences among US adolescents online, offline, and by platform.
Design, Setting, and Participants: Cross-sectional, nationally representative, probability-based survey of US adolescents aged 13-17 conducted February-December 2025 through the Understanding America Study panel. Self-reported use of 20 online platforms (e.g. Facebook, Instagram, Snapchat, TikTok, YouTube, Facetime, email etc) was assessed.
Main Outcomes and Measures: Self-reported prevalence of major risks and harms experienced in past 4 weeks, including harassment, sexual solicitation, and exposure to violent or self-harm content, as well as sleep disruption (measured online, offline, and by platform). Survey-weighted logistic regression identified associated factors.
Results: Weighted sample included 482 adolescents (mean age 15 years; 47.1% female; 65.1% White) from 47 US states. Across online and offline contexts, 44.3% witnessed harassment and 29.6% had experienced it directly in the past 4 weeks. Overall prevalence varied by gender and age: girls were more likely than boys to feel worse about themselves due to others' posts, messages, or communications (29.1% vs 15.5%), and older adolescents (ages 15-17) were more likely than younger adolescents (ages 13-14) to have witnessed discrimination against others (48.6% vs 38.5%). Online exposure exceeded offline exposure for numerous harms, including exposure to violent imagery (10.5% vs 1.6%), unwanted sexual images (13.5% vs 2.0%), and content encouraging self-harm (9.6% vs 5.1%). More than half (51.7%) reported sleep disruption due to technology use. Parental education, income, and parental controls were not significantly associated with online harm exposure (all P > .05). Platform differences in exposure to some harms were substantial, e.g., 42.8% of TikTok users and 28.3% of Instagram users attributed disrupted sleep to those platforms, compared with 11.3% of text-messaging users and 0.6% of email users.
Conclusions and Relevance: Negative online experiences are common among US adolescents and vary by platform, gender, and age.
