Establishing rigorous, multi-layered measurement approaches with platform-by-platform, social media versus other digital environments, and online versus offline comparisons is essential for transforming fragmented evidence into actionable data that can guide effective decisions by youth, parents, platforms and
regulators.

articles.title: Object perception encodes mutually exclusive possibilities
articles.authors: Gabor Brody; Peter Mazalik; Roman Feiman
articles.abstract: Human adults represent and reason about possibilities: multiple, mutually exclusive, non-actual ways the world could be. It is debated whether this is a late-developing, human-specific capacity and whether it requires natural language. We propose that instead of a unitary high-level capacity, multiple cognitive capacities might independently represent possibilities in different ways and with different properties. Our case study is object perception, a capacity shared with infants and non-human animals. Using methods that tap specifically into visual processes, across four experiments (N = 160), we found possibility representations in vision tasks that do not involve high-level reasoning or language. In Experiments 1 and 2, we found evidence for the visual encoding of possible object identities: When there were two possible ways that features might map onto visual objects, both possible mappings were primed. In Experiment 3, we found that visual feature-binding is a necessary precondition for such priming, ruling out low-level associative and high-level reasoning-based alternatives. Finally, in Experiment 4, we discovered that the visual system cannot encode one visual object possibly being at two possible locations. This pattern helps to explain a host of previous findings from the comparative and developmental literature and suggests that the unit of analysis for possibility representations should be the cognitive capacity rather than the agent.

articles.title: A test of the digital Give-a-Number task
articles.authors: Khuyen N. Le; Jingyi Liu; Daniel C. Hyde; David Barner
articles.abstract: For over 50 years, the Give-a-Number task has been used as a measure of children’s understanding of number words. Also, the knower level framework that results from the task has also featured prominently in theoretical proposals about children’s number acquisition and the development of number concepts. However, because different labs test children with different objects, trial structures, and numbers, the field lacks a standardized version of the task that can be used as a stable predictor across studies. In this study, we took a step toward standardization by asking whether a digital version of the task could replace the standard version. When individual children were tested with both a physical act-out version and a digital version of Give-a-Number, we found moderate to high concordance between the two versions. However, we also found that children tended to perform more accurately on the physical version, though this difference had little effect on knower level classification. We conclude that the digital version may replace the physical act-out task in many contexts, with minimal impact on classification validity.

articles.title: Humor: The Amusing Side of the Psychology of Art and Aesthetics
articles.authors: Paul Joseph Silvia; Vi Bui
articles.abstract: Humor was an interest of Berlyne’s new experimental aesthetics movement, but the psychology of aesthetics and humor have since drifted apart. To reunite them, we apply an aesthetics lens to some classic and modern findings in the psychology of humor, drawing parallels between humor and the broader psychology of aesthetics and the arts for aspects of the product (e.g., words, puns, and jokes), the experience (e.g., feeling amused, irritated, or embarrassed by jokes), and the audience (e.g., the massive individual differences in what people find funny). Its intrinsic interest aside, the psychology of humor reminds us of the sheer ubiquity of comic creative work and highlights the importance of amusing, playful, and lighthearted experiences of the arts.

articles.title: The Emperor's New Diagnoses: Psychedelics, Psychosis, and the Limits of Psychiatric Distinctions
articles.authors: Maja Willard
articles.abstract: Psychedelic and psychotic experiences are institutionally treated as belonging to different orders — yet in phenomenology and in cultural interpretation, they often converge. The present paper examines that gap. It sets out the similarities and differences between the two — chiefly the predominance of visual rather than auditory hallucinations in psychedelic experience, the role of insight, the influence of context in both, and the asymmetry in clinical response. It does not adjudicate the causal question of whether psychedelics can induce psychosis; the clinical, population, and naturalistic literature answer different questions, and none of them closes the case. I argue, rather, that psychosis research has much to gain from attending to features long central in psychedelic science: the significance of subjective experience and meaning-making, the influence of set and setting, and the moral commitment to impartial care. The aim is narrow — to mark what the available comparison does and does not warrant — but the implications, I suggest, bear on how psychiatry frames, studies, and responds to altered states more broadly.

articles.title: Executive Functioning as a Developmental Scaffold for Emotion Regulation: Longitudinal Neural and Behavioral Associations Across Adolescence
articles.authors: Kayleigh Esparza; Robin Aupperle; Martin P Paulus
articles.abstract: Adolescence is a critical developmental period marked by rapid changes in limbic and prefrontal regions, alongside gains in executive functioning and the development of emotion regulation strategies. Prior work suggests reciprocal relationships between executive function and emotion regulation, with each being important for supporting optimal developmental outcomes. However, further understanding the longitudinal relationships and underlying neural mechanisms is important for informing the timing and targets of intervention approaches. The present study utilized longitudinal data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study (N = 11,868) to examine these relationships from approximately ages 9–16. Exploratory factor analyses were conducted using fMRI data from the emotional N-back task, identifying three factors each for the 2-vs.-0-back and Faces-vs.-Places contrasts, corresponding to Sensorimotor-Insular, Central Executive, and Visual-Occipital networks. Cross-lagged panel models were used to test prospective associations amongst these network factors, scores on the emotion regulation questionnaire, and an NIH Toolbox executive function factor score across time. Results indicated that the Central Executive factor of the 2-vs.-0-back contrast predicted subsequent executive functioning across all time points, with some evidence of bidirectional associations in preadolescence. Moreover, executive functioning at ages 13–14 positively predicted both cognitive reappraisal and emotion suppression at ages 14–15, while neither emotion regulation strategy predicted later executive functioning. These findings highlight the dynamic and reciprocal nature of neural and behavioral development across adolescence and support the importance of executive network development for supporting cognitive function, and for executive functioning as an important developmental scaffold for emotion regulation strategy use.

articles.title: Exploratory Validation of the Black Female Athlete Anxiety Scale
articles.authors: Luka Ojemaye; Joyce Olushola Ogunrinde; Billy Hawkins; Micheal Cottingham; Jie Zhang
articles.abstract:
Black female collegiate athletes (BFCAs) navigate a distinct set of stressors at the intersection of race, gender, and athletic participation that may contribute to elevated levels of anxiety. Although awareness of mental health concerns among collegiate athletes has increased, existing anxiety assessment tools often lack sensitivity to the intersectional experiences shaping the lives of BFCAs. Consequently, these instruments may fail to capture the culturally specific stressors that influence anxiety within this population. The present study addressed this gap by conducting a preliminary validation of the Black Female Athlete Anxiety Scale (BFAAS), a culturally responsive instrument designed to assess anxiety-inducing stressors experienced by BFCAs.
A total of 116 NCAA Division I BFCAs completed the newly developed 21-item Black Female Athlete Anxiety Scale (BFAAS). Exploratory factor analysis (EFA) was conducted to examine the instrument’s underlying factor structure. Results identified a three-factor model, with factors labeled Navigating Marginalization, Performance Pressure, and Athletic Time Constraints. Additional analyses, including analysis of variance (ANOVA) and independent-samples t tests, were conducted to explore group-level differences in anxiety-related stressors within the sample. Internal consistency estimates demonstrated strong reliability across factors (Cronbach’s α > .80).
These findings provided preliminary evidence supporting the BFAAS as a reliable and culturally responsive measure of anxiety-inducing stressors among BFCAs. The scale offers practical utility for improving mental health assessment in collegiate sport contexts and may inform culturally responsive interventions, practitioner training, and policy initiatives aimed at supporting the anxiety and well-being of BFCAs.

articles.title: Polarity and the speed of scalar implicature processing
articles.authors: Michael Vrazitulis; J E Loy; Vera Demberg
articles.abstract: Scalar implicatures (SIs) such as the inference from ‘some’ to ‘some but not all’ are known to vary in processing cost. According to the Polarity Hypothesis (van Tiel et al., 2019; van Tiel & Pankratz, 2021), this cost depends on the polarity of the scalar term: SIs from positively polar expressions (e.g., ‘some’ implicating ‘some but not all’) are expected to be slower to compute than literal interpretations, while the reverse should hold for SIs from negatively polar expressions, sometimes referred to as indirect SIs (e.g., ‘not all’ implicating ‘not all but some’). We tested this prediction in a sentence–picture verification experiment with 538 English-speaking participants. Each participant judged whether sentences containing weak or strong scalar terms matched a corresponding picture, allowing us to measure response times as an index of processing effort. Our results showed no clear evidence for the Polarity Hypothesis; specifically, we found it to not generalise across the different scales included in our experiment. We additionally conducted a meta-analysis synthesising all existing evidence so far—this meta-analysis shows that, overall, the Polarity Hypothesis can be vindicated. Surprisingly, our results do also not clearly validate the traditional assumption of a B&N effect (Bott & Noveck, 2004), i.e., a general polarity-independent processing slowdown under SIs: a subsequent analysis including trial order revealed that the B&N effect was present in early trials of our study, whereas later trials do not show the effect. Overall, we conclude that polarity may influence the speed of scalar implicature processing, but that this effect is not robust across all types of scales.

articles.title: Can Multi-Task Eye-Movement Profiles Identify People Across Recording Blocks?
articles.authors: Marian Sauter
articles.abstract:
Eye-movement biometrics are often evaluated in narrowly defined tasks, leaving open the
question of whether a broader behavioral profile can identify people more robustly across
time. We address this question by combining features from multiple eye-tracking tasks,
including grid-based viewing, smooth pursuit, microsaccades, blinks, free viewing, and
pupil responses, into one oculomotor profile per participant and recording block. We then
test whether a held-out block can be matched back to the correct participant. All three
models we evaluated identified people far above chance: a family-weighted cosine fusion
model, which learns cross-validated weights for each task family in the cross-session
benchmark, reached the numerically highest top-1 accuracy in every cohort (0.88 in the
primary Neon-only cohort and 0.79 in the EyeLink dual-setup cohort), but a subject-level
paired bootstrap showed that its advantage over a simpler unweighted cosine baseline ($0.80$
Neon; 0.71 EyeLink) was not statistically distinguishable. Feature analyses showed that
useful identity information was distributed across multiple task families---most strongly
grid and smooth-pursuit features---rather than being driven by a single measure. Because the
Neon-only and dual-setup Neon profiles come from different participant samples, a cohort
classifier separated them almost perfectly (AUC = 0.99), reflecting a combination of
recording-context and between-sample differences. These findings indicate that multi-task
oculomotor fingerprinting is feasible, but that careful attention to acquisition conditions
and to the limits of small-sample model comparisons is essential when interpreting biometric
performance.

articles.title: The Arousal-Space Metaphor: Evidence from Spatial Mapping in a 3D Volumetric Environment
articles.authors: Josef Kundrát; Karel Rečka; Eric Hiris; Radim Peša; Fernando Marmolejo-Ramos; Aleš Neusar; Tereza Benešová
articles.abstract: Spatial metaphors linking valence to physical location—such as feeling up or being down—have been extensively documented. However, valence and arousal co-vary systematically, leaving open the question of whether previously reported vertical-space effects reflect valence, arousal, or both. The present work addresses this question by having participants positioned Czech affective adjectives in a three-dimensional virtual space. Stimuli were selected via Shannon entropy to orthogonally represent valence and arousal, minimizing their usual correlation. Across three studies (data collected in 2025 and 2026), we examined spatial placement (Studies 1–2) and perceptual validation of adjective location (Study 3). Linear mixed-effects models revealed that both valence and arousal independently predicted vertical placement (Y-axis), with positive valence or high arousal being placed higher. Valence consistently predicted depth placement (Z-axis), with positive adjectives placed closer to the body and negative adjectives further away, consistent with approach-avoidance frameworks. No consistent effects emerged on the horizontal axis. Study 3 confirmed perceptual validity: congruent pairings of valence and arousal the strongest agreement judgments (e.g., OR = 122.41 for positive, high-arousal adjectives placed high), while incongruent pairings of valence and arousal (e.g., positive, low-arousal adjectives) produced uncertainty about the correct location of these adjectives. These findings demonstrate that arousal constitutes an independent contributor to vertical spatial-affective mapping—a dimension largely overlooked in prior work due to its natural covariation with valence—and that the depth axis operates primarily as a valence domain. Substantial individual differences in the application of affective-spatial metaphors were observed across all studies.

articles.title: Assessing the efficacy of crowdsourced fact-checking for TikTok videos
articles.authors: Sze Yuh Nina Wang; Jennifer Nancy Lee Allen; Hause Lin; Cameron Martel; David Gertler Rand; Gordon Pennycook
articles.abstract: Social media companies are increasingly shifting towards crowdsourced fact-checking approaches, which are more scalable than solely relying on professional fact-checkers. While past work suggests these approaches are effective for news headlines, it is unclear whether they are effective for short-form video content like TikToks, which are increasingly prevalent as a source of news/information. We compare the accuracy ratings of three professional fact-checkers to those of layperson crowds on three politicized topics: the Ukraine/Russia conflict, climate change, and abortion. Professional fact-checkers showed reasonable agreement on TikToks about the Ukraine/Russia conflict and abortion, but poor agreement on TikToks about climate change. Poorer performance by Republican laypeople meant that politically-balanced crowds performed as well as professional fact-checkers on Ukraine/Russia TikToks, while only Democrat-only crowds performed as well as professionals on abortion TikToks. A classifier using layperson ratings to predict professionals’ ratings performed well on TikToks where professionals’ ratings were unanimous, suggesting that crowdsourcing may be effective for unambiguously true or false TikToks.

articles.title: A Two-Stage Scaffolding Model of Color and Luminance Development: Retinal Waves, Global Impoverishment, and Luminance-Grounded Object Representations
articles.authors: David Henry Peterzell
articles.abstract: Vogelsang et al. (2024) reported that individuals who gain sight late in life after congenital blindness from dense bilateral cataracts rely disproportionately on color cues for object recognition. They proposed that this reflects the adaptive value of neonatal color immaturity: because typical infants experience a period of degraded chromatic input, they build object representations grounded in luminance rather than color. The companion paper (Peterzell, 2026) showed this color-specific account is untenable, because the same photoreceptor immaturity that degrades chromatic input equally degrades luminance and spatial processing, leaving no developmental window in which luminance-first representations can form while color is specifically suppressed. The present paper develops a positive alternative. I propose a two-stage scaffolding model: a prenatal stage in which retinal waves, achromatic by physical necessity, establish the foundational spatial and luminance architecture of the visual system before any visual experience occurs; and a postnatal stage in which the globally degraded neonatal visual signal forces the first object representations to be built on luminance structure, the only dimension with sufficient fidelity under maximal photoreceptor immaturity. The late-sighted Prakash patients retain the prenatal structural scaffold (their intact retinas should have generated normal retinal waves) but lack the postnatal experiential scaffold (the luminance-grounded representational architecture normally built through years of globally impoverished visual experience). The accounts make different predictions: whereas Vogelsang et al. predict color-specific fragility, the scaffolding account predicts generalized fragility to any form of image degradation, a distinction testable on the existing Vogelsang et al. computational architecture without new patient data.

articles.title: Mismatch Between Routine Care and Personalized Selection of Low-Intensity Psychological Interventions Using Causal Machine Learning
articles.authors: Christian A. Webb; Jaime Delgadillo
articles.abstract:
Importance: Low-intensity interventions can help to widen access to treatment for common mental disorders.

Objective: To develop a data-driven model for personalized assignment to one of three available low intensity interventions, and to evaluate the clinical outcomes of model-matched cases.

Design: Analysis of electronic health records for an observational cohort. A multi-arm causal forest estimated conditional treatment effects in a training sample of early cases (~70%) and evaluated the potential benefit of personalized treatment selection in a hold-out test sample of cases treated at a later date (~30%).

Setting: Sixteen NHS Talking Therapies services offering low intensity psychological interventions in England.

Participants: N= 89,467 patients receiving one of three interventions.

Interventions: cCBT (computerized CBT), iGSH (individual guided self-help), and gGSH (group guided self-help).

Main Outcomes and Measures: Reliable recovery on psychometric measures of depression (PHQ-9) and anxiety (GAD-7). Personalized intervention benefit (PIB) indexed recommendation strength as the predicted advantage of the recommended treatment over alternatives.

Results: Observed assignment in the test sample was highly concentrated in iGSH (87.9%), with 9.0% receiving gGSH and 3.1% cCBT. The causal forest recommended cCBT for 64.1%, iGSH for 33.5%, and gGSH for 2.4%. Only 30.7% received their model-recommended treatment. Overall reliable recovery was 41.1%. Receiving the recommended treatment was associated with higher reliable recovery, with the benefit increasing across PIB quartiles: Q1 = 0.5%, Q2 = 2.5%, Q3 = 6.8%, Q4 = 11.3% (p < .001). Predictors of observed assignment and model-recommended treatment modestly overlapped (Spearman ρ = 0.36).

Conclusions and Relevance: Causal forest recommendations diverged substantially from observed care, with current practice markedly underutilizing cCBT. Personalized treatment selection could improve reliable recovery rates by up to 11%.

articles.title: Bayesian Evidence Synthesis under Heterogeneity
articles.authors: Björn Alfons Edmar; Duco Veen; Irene Klugkist
articles.abstract: Bayesian evidence synthesis (BES) provides a way of synthesising research without pooling effects, allowing for quantitative synthesise procedures when the more traditional common-effects and random-effects models cannot be used. BES can be applied through the multiplication of independent Bayes factors assessing a common theory, quantifying the evidence for same informative hypothesis given all observed studies. This paper evaluates the behaviour of BES under varying levels of heterogeneity, and extends the methodological framework to incorporate model space inference (MSI) for more nuanced synthesis results. We do this through simulation of a standard mean difference scenario, examining how various hypothesis evaluations complement one another and provide independent information that can mitigate the potential for false positive inferences. Our results depict the high sensitivity and specificity of BES, and illustrate how heterogeneity affects BES. Our simulation results provide guidance for real world application of BES, discussing what aspects of the method to keep in mind when synthesising evidence comparing two means. In addition to this, we present an empirical example further detailing the application of the method.

articles.title: Ethnoracial and Linguistic Diversity in Treatment Research for Primary Progressive Aphasia: A Systematic Review
articles.authors: Rylee Catherine Manning; Wenfu Bao; Maria Fernanda Quiñones Vieta; Lucas Ansín Gómez; Jo-Ali Acosta; Jessica de Leon; Júlia Filella Mercè; Estefanía García Hernandez; Karla P. Gutiérrez Sánchez; Jan C. Holst Chaires
articles.abstract:
Purpose: This systematic review sought to characterize the ethnoracial and linguistic representation of participants in the nonpharmacological intervention literature for primary progressive aphasia (PPA) and primary progressive apraxia of speech (PPAOS). In addition, we aimed to identify which social determinants of health (SDOH) factors are most frequently reported across studies. 

Method: We performed a comprehensive search across six electronic databases: OVID Medline, OVID PsycINFO, EBSCO Medline, EBSCO CINAHL Plus, Embase, and Web of Science, as well as unpublished literature. We identified 149 original studies that administered neuromodulation and/or speech-language interventions to individuals with PPA or PPAOS and reported communication outcomes relative to a baseline or control group. From these studies, we extracted data on participants’ race, ethnicity, spoken languages, occupation, years of education, and other relevant sociodemographic variables.

Results: Ethnoracial and linguistic diversity in the current PPA literature is limited and underrepresented. Among studies that reported the extracted variables, the majority of participants were identified as non-Hispanic White, and most studies were conducted in the United States. English was the most commonly reported spoken language and was frequently used as an inclusion criterion. Regarding SDOH, participants tend to have medium-high education and hold higher skill-level occupations. Very few studies reported retirement status and whether retirement was due to symptom onset.

Conclusions: Current intervention research for PPA and PPAOS lacks ethnoracial and linguistic diversity.  This underrepresentation is further compounded by limited reporting of factors related to SDOH. The concentration of research in homogenous populations limits the generalizability of current findings and restricts their relevance for managing PPA and PPAOS across diverse demographic, cultural, linguistic, and socioeconomic contexts. Future research on treatment should not only ensure comprehensive reporting of participant characteristics but also actively expand the inclusivity of recruitment practices.

articles.title: Intersecting Identities and Campus Well-Being: Mental Health, Substance Use, and Service Barriers Among Racial/Ethnic and Sexual/Gender Minoritized Students at a Canadian University
articles.authors: Victoria Doan; Erin Courtice; Tara Raessi; Sarah Dermody
articles.abstract: Sexual and gender minoritized (SGM) and racial and ethnic minoritized (REM) and university students face unique minority stressors that increase risk of mental health problems and substance use. Yet, little research examines how intersecting identities influence barriers to accessing campus services. We investigated associations between these identities - including their intersections - and mental health, substance use, and obstacles to utilizing campus-based services. We conducted an online survey with 1,009 undergraduate students (Mage= 20.6, SD= 5.1) at a Canadian university about mental health symptoms and substance use, as well as experiences with and barriers to accessing campus mental health and substance use services. Regression analyses revealed few differences in mental health severity and barriers to accessing campus services between most REM subgroups and white students. Most REM subgroups reported lower rates of substance use, greater awareness of campus services, and found services more helpful relative to white students. Sexually minoritized students reported greater severity of mental health symptoms and more barriers to accessing campus services relative to heterosexual students. Interactions between SGM and REM identities did not correspond with differing outcomes. Our findings highlight the need for tailored campus services that address unique challenges faced by diverse minoritized student populations.

articles.title: Bidirectional associations between executive function and social behavior across childhood: A registered report using a nationally representative sample.
articles.authors: Blake Glatley; Andrew David Ribner; Sammy F. Ahmed
articles.abstract: Executive function (EF) refers to a set of cognitive skills involved in deliberate, goal-oriented behavior. Although research has established links between children’s EF and aspects of their social skills, it remains unclear how EF skills are related to specific social behaviors, such as prosociality and social aggression. Additionally, the co-development of EF and social behavior during childhood is less understood. Therefore, the current registered report aimed to examine bidirectional associations between two EF skills - working memory and cognitive flexibility - and children's prosocial behavior and social aggression using the Early Childhood Longitudinal Study - Kindergarten Class of 2010-11 (ECLS-K:2011; N = 18,174). Based on Sameroff’s Transactional Model of Human Development, we expected that EF and social behavior would be bidirectionally related from third-to-fifth grade (8 to 11 years old). We hypothesized that both working memory and cognitive flexibility would be positively associated with later prosocial behavior and negatively associated with later social aggression. Furthermore, we hypothesized that prosocial behavior would be positively associated with later EF skills and that social aggression would be negatively associated with later EF skills. Our results indicated that, at the between-person level, EF skills were positively associated with prosocial behavior and only working memory was negatively associated with social aggression. At the within-person level, we found no bidirectional associations between EF skills and social behaviors. However, we observed a small number of concurrent correlations between EF skills and social behaviors, along with a limited set of cross-lagged associations. Findings are discussed in the context of children’s social development.

articles.title: Signatures proposed to index perceptual effects emerge in a purely cognitive task
articles.authors: Sixing Chen; Michaela Bocheva; Dobromir Rahnev
articles.abstract: A central question in many studies on perception and consciousness is whether the effects of a given manipulation are perceptual or cognitive. Typically, studies seek to find evidence that the raw sensory experience has been altered—that is, a manipulation produces perceptual rather than cognitive effects. Here, we test two specific proposals for behavioral signatures that could allow this type of inference. First, a long-standing proposition is that different parameters of the drift–diffusion model (DDM) map onto different effects, such that a drift-rate bias indicates a perceptual effect, whereas a starting point shift indicates a cognitive effect. Second, a newer proposal holds that, when plotted as a function of a sensory feature, changes in the peak of the distributions for confidence and reaction time (RT) imply that a given manipulation’s effects are perceptual. Here, we test both proposals. We designed a purely cognitive task where no effects can be perceptual and examined whether the proposed signatures of perceptual effects occur in this task. Participants viewed numbers generated by two distributions and indicated which distribution the numbers likely came from. In separate conditions we manipulated prior beliefs and rewards related to each distribution. We found that both manipulations elicited the same signatures previously proposed as indicative of purely perceptual effects. Our results demonstrate that individual DDM parameters and the peaks of confidence and RT distributions in isolation are insufficient to distinguish between perceptual and cognitive effects.

articles.title: Shared neural representations of barriers to agents’ actions and objects’ motions
articles.authors: Minjae J. Kim; Samuel Maione; Shari Liu
articles.abstract: In the human brain, distinct regions preferentially respond to the actions and interactions of animate agents, and the motions and interactions of inanimate objects. Yet, evidence across the cognitive sciences suggests that we process animate agents fundamentally as physical bodies (Liu et al., 2025). This suggestion predicts that processing of agents and processing of objects depend in part on common neural substrates. Here, we used functional brain imaging to test this prediction. Our case study concerned a fundamental, developmentally early-emerging intuition that because agents and objects are solid bodies, their motions are constrained by physical barriers (Gergely & Csibra, 2003; Saxe et al., 2006; Spelke et al., 1992). Participants (N = 28) were scanned using functional magnetic resonance imaging while they watched videos of agents navigating or objects moving in environments containing physical barriers, and videos containing similar physical features that did not block agents’ or objects’ paths. We report a domain-invariant code for barriers to agents’ actions and objects’ motions in regions associated with physical reasoning and multiple demand (superior parietal lobule), object processing (lateral occipital complex), and scene processing (occipital place area, parahippocampal place area, and retrosplenial cortex). Our results generalized across two distinct tasks, and are not explained by differences in luminance, motion energy, or the number of objects in the stimuli. These results suggest that brain regions specialized for reasoning about physical dynamics and spatial layouts support our capacity to make sense of agents, raising questions about interactions more broadly between social, physical and spatial cognition.

articles.title: ManyBabies 6: A Multi-Lab Investigation of Neonatal and Early Imitation
articles.authors: Andrew N. Meltzoff; Sumeet Farwaha; Virginia Slaughter; Elizabeth Ann Simpson; Rechele Brooks; Mark Nielsen; Heidi A Baumgartner; Laurie Bayet; Silvia Benavides-Varela; Veronica Boyce
articles.abstract: Classic theory in developmental science holds that the imitation of facial gestures is a late developing capacity in human infancy. Meltzoff and Moore (1977) reported that 12- to 21-day-old infants imitate facial and manual gestures, theorizing that young infants can recognize cross-modal correspondences between observed and performed actions and suggesting that early imitation plays a role in social learning from other people. Subsequent studies yielded mixed findings and alternative interpretations have been proposed. Thus, whether young infants can imitate adult gestures remains a debated question. As part of the ManyBabies consortium, a large-scale collaborative network in developmental science, we tested whether very young infants (14–28 and 40–54 days old) demonstrate facial imitation using a protocol that was standardized for use across multiple independent laboratories. In a within-subjects design, infants saw mouth-opening and tongue-protrusion gestures in randomized order, presented by the same experimenter at the same distance and rate; responses were videorecorded for later behavioral scoring. [Insert study results and interpretations here.]

articles.title: Graded gender perceptions in personal names predict difficulty in reference resolution
articles.authors: Hassan Khan; Yoonjung Kang; Dave Kush
articles.abstract: We conducted two experiments to better understand how comprehenders use probabilistic knowledge of the associations between genders and personal names when inferring the gender of new referents during reading. Personal names are, surprisingly, under-investigated in studies on gender inference compared to role nouns (e.g., nurse) despite being strongly connected to gender. In a self-paced reading task involving variably gender biased names and gendered reflexives, we observe a gender mismatch effect proportional to the incongruence between a name’s perceived gender and that of the reflexive. In a novel variant of the Maze task involving forced ambiguity resolution between two gendered reflexives, we find decisions track the perceived gender of names and decisions take longer with more ambiguous names. We interpret the results to support a model in which comprehenders make graded, not categorical, inferences about gender during incremental comprehension, contrary to previous proposals.

articles.title: Controlled real-world object generation for visual memory experimentation and theory-building
articles.authors: Jiachen Zou; Chi Zhang; Deng Haotian; Quanying Liu; Chen Wei; Maria Martinovna Robinson
articles.abstract:
Real-world objects are visually rich and semantically structured, but due to this complexity, theories of visual memory are generally tested with more tractable stimulus spaces, such as color. To address the challenge of studying real-world objects with high experimental control,
we used a novel generative artificial intelligence (AI) Concept-based Controllable Generation (CoCoG) model, grounded in human similarity judgments, to create systematically structured object spaces. We then combined CoCoG-derived embeddings with a computational model of memory to predict memory error distributions. Across seven online visual-working-memory studies (N = 1,019), we found that these stimuli can be used to investigate established phenomena, such object-state versus -exemplar memory, and new phenomena, specifically memory for novel object morphs. These results build on recent work that bridges generative AI with cognitive modeling and demonstrate how architectures like CoCoG can be used as a methodological and theory-constraining tool for systematically studying real-world visual cognition.

articles.title: Neurodiversity and learning in context: Development and validation of a self-report measure of engagement with learning activities (MELA)
articles.authors: Julie Bailey; Sara Baker
articles.abstract: While neurodivergent students’ learning engagement is recognised as important, there is not yet a suitable measure of engagement with learning activities to support research into inclusive teaching. This paper outlines the development and validation of a new self-report instrument (MELA: Measure of Engagement with Learning Activities) intended for exploring neurodivergent students’ engagement. In Phase 1, existing measures were adapted and presented to autistic undergraduates (n = 33) and experts (n = 5). The measure was then refined following interviews with autistic students (n = 7) and presented in Phase 2 to autistic (n = 53) and non-autistic (n = 121) undergraduates to test for convergent validity through comparison against exiting measures. In Phase 3, the measure’s reliability and performance were examined. MELA demonstrated strong validity and therefore provides a robust research instrument for exploring students’ learning engagement, making it valuable for inclusion research supporting improvements to teaching in higher education.

articles.title: Choose your own PAS? Quantitative and Qualitative Validity Evidence for the Perceptual Awareness Scale
articles.authors: Alicia Franco-Martínez; Alicia Ferrer-Mendieta; Carmen Peiro Lanchares; Miguel A. Vadillo; Francisco Garre-Frutos
articles.abstract: The most preferred visibility measure in unconscious processing studies is the Perceptual Awareness Scale (PAS). Since it was originally proposed as a methodological approach, no PAS labels, instructions or display are currently official. Authors tend to adapt this scale to their experimental tasks often without specifying their PAS properties or without providing adequate justification for the modifications. In this work, we tested how “choosing your own PAS” affects response-process validity both quantitatively and qualitatively, in the same  unconscious working memory (WM) task as in Franco-Martínez et al. (in press) replication. In a preregistered 2×2×2 between-subjects design (N = 176), we manipulated: PAS Version (Original vs New labels, updated based on pilot participants’ reports), Examples (With brief demonstrations of PAS = 4 and PAS = 1 trials vs Without them), and Display (Scale&Text shown onscreen trial-by-trial vs ScaleOnly). In a post-experiment PAS questionnaire participants were asked to qualitatively report how they recalled and interpreted the scale. The unconscious WM effect and its reliability were robust under the Version and Examples manipulations, but presenting the New Version and/or With Examples increased the proportion of valid trials, enhancing statistical power. Although the unconscious WM effect increased in the ScaleOnly Display, the qualitative evidence suggests participants in this condition tend to forget the PAS labels and ultimately use them differently from intended. Finally, we provide practical recommendations for the use of PAS and a post-experiment PAS questionnaire to evaluate its validity.

articles.title: Partisans update their contentious political views in the face of contradictory facts and evidence
articles.authors: Sze Yuh Nina Wang; Thomas H Costello; David Gertler Rand; Gordon Pennycook
articles.abstract: It is commonly held that politically motivated reasoning causes partisans to reject information that counters their views, driving political polarization and public disagreement about the facts. We challenge this view across three experiments (N=2,921), where Large Language Models are used to provide partisans with strong evidence-based arguments that directly contradict their previously stated positions about democracy, corruption, and government funding. In contrast to predictions from theories of motivated reasoning, we found that these conversations were effective at shifting Republicans’ opinions about whether President Trump is undermining democracy (Study 1, d=.39), engaging in corrupt actions (Study 2, d=.71), or defunding programs that people support (Study 3, d=.70). These conversations also decreased overall support for Trump (d’s=0.37-0.46). Partisans updated their views despite being directly confronted about having contradictory positions on political topics.

articles.title: Adaptive search promotes asymmetrical learning
articles.authors: Samuel David Jones; Paul Rauwolf
articles.abstract: Recent theoretical work has argued that learning trajectories are shaped by adaptive search and satisficing. The central idea of this work is that the value of an information source (i.e., its utility or valence) modulates engagement, sampling density, and encoding fidelity, providing a behavioural pathway to variation in learning outcomes. The current manuscript reports the first explicit empirical test of this idea in adults using a modified version of a multi-step decision-making paradigm known as 'Mouselab'. Across two experiments and six sub-tasks, N=101 participants searched a decision tree at a cost until settling on and executing a high-value policy. While Experiment 1 involved a non-symbolic decision space, Experiment 2 incorporated symbolic cues that allowed us to determine the extent to which associative learning is sensitive to underlying value structure. We found that value-sensitive adaptive search promoted rank-dependent learning asymmetries at test – estimates for the value of the best symbol were near veridical, while those for low-value symbols were at chance. In addition, we found that (i) informative symbolic cues shifted search from balanced exploration (breadth ≈ depth) towards depth-first exploitation; (ii) cue-guided search developed rapidly, with high-value symbol exploitation emerging within a handful of trials; (iii) search evidenced a win-stay tendency and was sensitive to cue valence, with individuals quickly pruning negatively valued branches; (iv) raising cost constrained search volume but preserved its depth-first, cue-sensitive character; and (v) observed individual differences in search behaviour were not predicted by common measures of cognitive function. These results provide new insight into how adaptive search and satisficing shape learning, supporting an emerging theory of the behavioural origins of individual differences in human development.

articles.title: Community Notes as debunking intervention: Correction design and topic context determine effectiveness
articles.authors: Nicole Antes; Martina Gierlich; Anastasia Kozyreva; Nadia Said
articles.abstract: Crowdsourced on-platform corrections, such as Community Notes, have gained popularity as a scalable response to misinformation. Yet it remains unclear whether they function as effective debunking interventions. This study investigates whether corrections presented in the forms of Community Notes reduce continued influence of misinformation and whether their effectiveness varies across neutral and socially divisive content. In an online experiment, 542 adults from a quota-based German sample viewed simulated social media posts containing misinformation. The misinformation was shown again with a Community Note in one of three forms: no correction, a simple retraction, or a retraction with an alternative explanation. For neutral content, both corrective notes reduced reliance on misinformation, with explanatory notes performing best. For socially divisive content, Community Notes reduced continued influence only when providing an alternative explanation. These findings suggest that Community Notes can serve as an effective debunking intervention, but only under specific design and context conditions.

articles.title: Polarity and the speed of scalar implicature processing
articles.authors: Michael Vrazitulis; J E Loy; Vera Demberg
articles.abstract: Scalar implicatures (SIs) such as the inference from ‘some’ to ‘some but not all’ are known to vary in processing cost. According to the Polarity Hypothesis (van Tiel et al., 2019; van Tiel & Pankratz, 2021), this cost depends on the polarity of the scalar term: SIs from positively polar expressions (e.g., ‘some’ implicating ‘some but not all’) are expected to be slower to compute than literal interpretations, while the reverse should hold for SIs from negatively polar expressions, sometimes referred to as indirect SIs (e.g., ‘not all’ implicating ‘not all but some’). We tested this prediction in a sentence–picture verification experiment with 538 English-speaking participants. Each participant judged whether sentences containing weak or strong scalar terms matched a corresponding picture, allowing us to measure response times as an index of processing effort. Our results showed no clear evidence for the Polarity Hypothesis; specifically, we found it to not generalise across the different scales included in our experiment. We additionally conducted a meta-analysis synthesising all existing evidence so far—this meta-analysis shows that, overall, the Polarity Hypothesis can be vindicated. Surprisingly, our results do also not clearly validate the traditional assumption of a B&N effect (Bott & Noveck, 2004), i.e., a general polarity-independent processing slowdown under SIs: a subsequent analysis including trial order revealed that the B&N effect was present in early trials of our study, whereas later trials do not show the effect. Overall, we conclude that polarity may influence the speed of scalar implicature processing, but that this effect is not robust across all types of scales.

articles.title: The mental health emergency of heatwaves: Implications for Australian policy, clinical practice and social support
articles.authors: Hasini Gunasiri; Sunny N Nguyen; Caroline X. Gao; Jana Menssink; David Baker; Cybele Dey; Rebecca Patrick; Neerja Singh; Samantha Julia Legaspi Eala; Fiona Charlson
articles.abstract: As climate change drives more frequent and severe heatwaves, its impacts on population mental health are becoming increasingly evident. Extreme heat is associated with increased psychological distress, aggression, suicide, and mental health-related service use. The risks are further heightened among people with pre-existing mental illness, due to physiological, social, and medication-related factors. Drawing on a rapid survey of Australian state and national heat-health policies, this perspective highlights the gap in integrating mental health into heatwave responses and outlines actionable recommendations to embed mental health within preparedness and response policies, clinical care, and community-based strategies in a warming climate.

articles.title: Mental Well-Being and Stress in Farming Communities in the UK and Canada: The Roles of Personal Vulnerability and Event Load
articles.authors: Sarah Nyczaj Kyle; Emma Barkus; Andria Jones; Stephen Dunne
articles.abstract:
Objective: Farmers, their families, and employees face daily stressors that adversely affect their mental well-being. To improve understanding of stress and mental well-being in farming populations, this study examines this relationship in the United Kingdom (UK) and Canada. 
Method: Using an online cross-sectional survey, 569 adults from farming communities (37% male, 63% female; Mage = 47.57, SD = 12.55) completed measures of stress event load (EL), personal vulnerability to stress (PV), and subjective mental well-being. Data were analysed using analysis of variance and hierarchical multiple regression, with simple-slopes analysis. 
Results: Farming community members who reported higher PV reported significantly lower well-being, regardless of whether EL was low or high. In regression, when considered together, PV eliminated the predictive power of EL, highlighting the importance of PV. Although a significant interaction was observed, after Bonferroni correction, significance remained only for those with high PV, where well-being among farming community members was substantially lower than at low or average PV, across all levels of EL. While UK participants reported significantly lower mental well-being than Canadian participants, mental well-being was low in both countries at levels that necessitate support. 
Conclusion: While studies have traditionally focused on farming-specific causes of stress, personal vulnerability was a stronger determinant of mental well-being than stress event load across the two countries. Interventions targeting the psychological mechanisms that underpin stress appraisal will therefore help protect mental well-being in farming communities. Future research must utilise longitudinal measurements to explore the psychological resources of farming community members worldwide.

articles.title: People Overestimate Support for “Cancellation”
articles.authors: Jakob Kasper; Randy T. Lee; Jabin Binnendyk; Gordon Pennycook; Ovul Sezer
articles.abstract: Social exclusion has long served as a mechanism of norm enforcement. However, in contemporary discourse, there is a common narrative about “cancel culture” that implies an overextension of social exclusion – often driven by social media. Given the ambiguity surrounding what cancellation entails and how often it occurs, people may struggle to accurately judge their own likelihood of being cancelled for expressing controversial views. Across two preregistered studies (NS1 = 753, NS2 = 753), we examined judgments of cancellation using naturalistic statements that participants (N  = 49) in a pretest believed could get them “cancelled.” We found that people think they are more likely to get cancelled for making statements than is warranted, both when compared with other people’s judgment of the same statement and with others’ expressed willingness to impose such sanctions. Their estimates are also only weakly related to how others judged the same statement. Although participants generally judged cancellation as similarly likely whether they imagined themselves or another person making a statement, this self-other asymmetry emerged once cancellation was framed explicitly as taking place on social media: participants then perceived a greater likelihood of cancellation when simply imagining themselves, rather than another person, making the same statement. Together, these findings suggest that people systematically overestimate the risk of being cancelled and raise the possibility that concerns about cancellation are fueled by inaccurate beliefs about others’ willingness to impose social sanctions.

articles.title: The recoverability, reliability and generalisability of reward processing parameters and relation to mental health symptoms
articles.authors: Alexandra Claire Pike; Rowan Board; Eoin Travers; Megan Croal; Yeuk Ching Lam; Vincent Valton; Oliver Joe Robinson; Jonathan Paul Roiser
articles.abstract: Theory-driven computational psychiatry attempts to use cognitive models of computation to understand how mental health problems might relate to (or be caused by) changes in cognitive processes such as learning and decision-making. However, the potential applications and relevance of this approach are contingent on several (often implicit) assumptions, including that computational parameters 1) are recoverable, 2) are reliable over time, 3) reflect conceptually similar processes across different tasks, and 4) relate to symptoms. To illustrate and test these assumptions for a selection of commonly used tasks, we recruited a large online sample of participants (n=548), who completed seven mental health questionnaires and five tasks. A subset of n=115 was re-invited to complete the five tasks 14 days later. For each task, five models (including a null model, or model of no interest) were fit and the winning model was selected through Bayesian model comparison. The parameters from the winning models showed good recovery (mean r=0.853, sd=0.135), and good to excellent levels of test-retest reliability (mean ICC=0.635, sd=0.30). Parameters that were theoretically or mathematically similar to each other were, however, generally not related, with relationships only found within the class of ‘inverse temperature’-like parameters (significant r values ranged from 0.11 to 0.23): indicating that in general, parameters do not generalise across task contexts. Finally, only parameters from two of the five tasks (four-armed bandit and cognitive effort) related to symptoms, and these relationships were weak (maximum absolute r values ~0.10). To conclude, it seems that at least some of the implicit assumptions when advocating for the use of computational models in psychiatry are not met for these popular tasks and computational models. This limits the clinical and translational utility of this approach. Computational psychiatry researchers should carefully assess the assumptions and psychometric properties of their tasks and models as part of their early-phase development to ensure robustness of the field going forward.

articles.title: Color cues, developmental coupling, and the limits of the late-sighted model: A commentary on Vogelsang et al. (2024)
articles.authors: David Henry Peterzell
articles.abstract:
Vogelsang et al. (2024) report in Science that congenitally blind individuals who gain sight late in life rely disproportionately on color cues for object recognition, unlike normally sighted controls. They propose that this pattern reflects the adaptive value of neonatal color immaturity: because typical infants experience a period of degraded chromatic input, they develop object representations grounded in luminance rather than color.
The theoretical account, however, rests on an incomplete engagement with the developmental literature. Decades of research indicate that color and luminance sensitivity are developmentally coupled through shared photoreceptor immaturity: the same front-end bottleneck that degrades chromatic input in neonates simultaneously degrades luminance and spatial processing. If this coupling is real, there is no luminance-first window, because the bottleneck lifts for both dimensions together. The account's central premise is therefore undermined not by arguing independence, but by showing that color and luminance do not develop in the sequence the account requires.
I further identify methodological concerns: a small and unusual sample, a fixed order confound in the primary behavioral task, a computational model implementing a binary switch rather than the gradual chromatic cascade of normal development, and a color sensitivity test using non-equiluminant stimuli, leaving open the possibility that luminance rather than chromatic contrast sensitivity drove performance.
Together, these issues require qualification of the conclusions of Vogelsang et al. and reconciliation of the proposed account with classical findings in infant visual development. This broader question remains important, but the answer is unlikely to hinge on color alone.

articles.title: PARADOx: A Randomized Controlled Trial Examining Short- and Long-Term, Family-Specific, and Side Effects of a Digital Parenting Intervention
articles.authors: Rick van Logchem; Anne Bülow; Crystal R. Smit; Savannah Boele; Bart Soenens; Loes Keijsers
articles.abstract: Few digital parenting interventions for preventing adolescent mental health problems exist and little is known about their effectiveness. This preregistered Randomized Controlled Trial examined short-term (1 month post-intervention) and longer-term (6 months) effects, side-effects, and family-specific effects of a novel digital parenting intervention. Dutch adolescents (N = 547, 53% female, Mage = 14.1) and one of their parents (N = 541; 81% mothers, Mage = 46.7) were assigned to a control or intervention group. The intervention group received a parenting feedback report and 18 parenting tips across six weeks, in the second half of a 100-day diary study. No explicit intervention effects were identified, but no harmful side-effects were identified either. At 6-month follow-up, parent- and adolescent-reported overprotection decreased and longer-term adolescent well-being improved. Furthermore, improvements in overprotection and adolescent well-being were correlated and changes in overparenting were generally stronger when parents were exposed to more tips, tentatively suggesting a benefit of 100 days of journaling. The trial was retrospectively registered (CCMO; registration NL-009451) on 19-02-2025.

articles.title: Manipulating Causal Priors Affects the Outcome-Density Bias
articles.authors: Simon Stephan; Michael R. Waldmann
articles.abstract: Non-contingent learning data can lead to the (seemingly) illusory perception of causality if the potential cause and effect often co-occur – an effect known as “outcome-density bias.” Bayesian models of causal induction explain this effect as the result of a rational learning process in which the data do not fully override non-zero causal priors. Convincing evidence for this rational explanation requires the demonstration of an experimentally manipulated effect of causal priors, which has been lacking. We successfully manipulated participants’ (𝑁 = 300) causal priors through visually conveyed causal mechanism information. This manipulation influenced the outcome-density effect in the predicted way: in a condition inducing low causal priors, the effect disappeared. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of visual mechanism information in manipulating priors and strengthen the Bayesian view of causal induction.

articles.title: An Experimental Paradigm for Evaluating Shared Agency in Humans and LLMs
articles.authors: Anita Braga; Ty Hayes; Simon Myers; Paolo Turrini; Nick Chater
articles.abstract: Coordination is central to social life, yet LLMs are poor coordinators. We introduce the Hangman game as a novel experimental paradigm to study why. Our focus is on shared agency, a precondition for coordination, defined as the ability to form shared goals, intentions, and plans. Success in Hangman requires agents to coordinate on word choices across rounds. Human teams reliably develop shared strategies, coordinating on mutually predictable word patterns. LLMs fail to do so. Across Human-Human, Human-LLM, and LLM-LLM teams, humans outperform all tested state-of-the-art LLMs, and humans paired with LLMs perform worse than when paired with other humans. As LLMs are increasingly deployed as collaborative agents, coordination failures undermine their usefulness. Hangman offers a flexible, controlled method for measuring and tracking coordination skills as models develop.

articles.title: An Evolution of the UCLA Isolation Scales: Distinguishing Feelings of Loneliness from Social Disconnection in UK and US Online Adult Samples
articles.authors: Paddy Maher; Yavor Dragostinov; Ross David Stewart; Andrew John Cooper
articles.abstract: Loneliness measurement has faced serious challenges in recent years, and recent reviews have cited indeterminate factor structures and poor construct validity as the principal causes. The field's most widely used instrument, the Revised UCLA Loneliness Scale (R-UCLA), has come under particular scrutiny. We address these challenges, firstly by clarifying the structure of the UCLA, and then by redeveloping it through a scale purification process (N = 21,589) in which we prune cross-loading items to create the UCLA-V (“UCLA-Five”). We confirm that the new scale behaves consistently across gender, geographic regions and age groups, and that it is functionally and structurally equivalent to its predecessor, but with a clear structure and improved model fit. We also derive the UCLA-V-short, the first true three-item representation of the UCLA (rparent = .92). We validate these scales in a multi-rater follow-up study (N = 910), finding that the UCLA is an isolation scale with three dimensions, one of which is loneliness.

articles.title: Cognitive drivers of feedback-seeking decisions differ between socially fearful and reassurance-seeking individuals
articles.authors: Yukta Thyagaraj; Caroline J Charpentier
articles.abstract: Seeking feedback about oneself from others is an important aspect of social communication, and its dysfunctions can be characteristic of underlying psychopathology, especially social anxiety. However, the factors driving active feedback-seeking decisions and how they vary along social anxiety and related internalizing symptoms, remain poorly understood. To address this, in this study, we developed a novel online task that allowed us to quantify the effects of expected feedback valence, confidence about performance, feedback directness, and perceived feedback instrumentality on decisions to seek or avoid feedback. Across two independent datasets (final N=344), our findings show that individuals are more likely to seek feedback when they expect it to be positive and when they perceive it as helpful. Symptom-based clustering analyses reveal differences in the drivers of feedback-seeking based on the relative levels of different social anxiety symptoms (social fear-avoidance v/s reassurance-seeking), suggesting a dissociation between these constructs. Our results contribute to understanding the cognitive mechanisms underlying feedback-seeking decisions and their variation along social anxiety, potentially informing cognitive-based interventions for subtypes of social anxiety.

articles.title: Who reciprocates whom? Mutual influence in emoticon use and perceived responsiveness between chat workers and young people seeking help
articles.authors: Gerard Chung; Andrew Zi Han Yee; Nataliya Thell
articles.abstract:
Research on computer-mediated communication has shown that reciprocal emoticon and emoji
use predicts how warm, responsive, and engaging a text exchange is perceived to be. This work,
however, has been conducted almost entirely in symmetric dyads, such as peers, friends, dating
partners, or colleagues, in which both parties enter the exchange on equal terms. We extend this
literature to a role-asymmetric helping dyad, examining emoticon reciprocity between trained chat
workers and help-seeking young people in a Singapore youth chatline. Drawing on the Interpersonal
Process Model of Intimacy and Communication Accommodation Theory, we analysed 86,424 lagged
turns nested within 1,435 sessions using the Actor–Partner Interdependence Model, operationalising
emoticon use in both binary (presence) and count (intensity) forms. Three findings emerged. First,
emoticons were a routine feature of the channel (87% of sessions), with chat workers using more and a
narrower affirming repertoire than users. Second, positive actor and partner effects were present in
both directions, but the partner effect from chat worker to user was substantially larger than the
reverse, indicating asymmetric reciprocity tracking the role and power asymmetry of the dyad. Third,
partner effects were stronger in sessions rated higher on worker responsiveness and session
satisfaction; actor effects were also moderated by both ratings, though in opposing directions
depending on operationalisation. What matters in text-based help, our results suggest, is not either
party's emoticon use considered alone but the reciprocal exchange between them.

articles.title: Distinct neural representations of perceptual and numerical absence in the human brain
articles.authors: Benjy Barnett; Stephen M Fleming
articles.abstract: The ability to represent absences is fundamental to human perception and cognition. Sensitivity to perceptual absences depends on inferring a lack of sensory evidence, enabling the recognition of a lack of environmental resources or other objects. In turn, encoding absence as a concept – in the form of the number zero – has enabled a multitude of technological and mathematical advances. Whether perceptual and conceptual absences rely on overlapping or distinct neural representations in the human brain, however, remains unknown. We employed three different tasks together with magnetoencephalography (MEG) to characterise neural representations of absence evoked within perceptual and numerical domains. Using multivariate decoding and Bayes factor analyses, we replicate previous findings of shared representations of numerical absence across symbolic and non-symbolic formats. In contrast, we show that these representations of numerical absence are distinct to those evoked by perceptual absences. This result held even after controlling for stimulus-level confounds which generate surface-level similarities between perceptual absences and non-symbolic empty sets. Our results extend a boundary between perception and cognition to the domain of absence and provide insight into the different mechanisms employed by the human brain to both perceive and conceive of absences.

articles.title: People Overestimate Support for “Cancellation”
articles.authors: Jakob Kasper; Randy T. Lee; Jabin Binnendyk; Gordon Pennycook; Ovul Sezer
articles.abstract: Social exclusion has long served as a mechanism of norm enforcement. However, in contemporary discourse, there is a common narrative about “cancel culture” that implies an overextension of social exclusion – often driven by social media. Given the ambiguity surrounding what cancellation entails and how often it occurs, people may struggle to accurately judge their own likelihood of being cancelled for expressing controversial views. Across two preregistered studies (NS1 = 753, NS2 = 753), we examined judgments of cancellation using naturalistic statements that participants (N  = 49) in a pretest believed could get them “cancelled.” We found that people think they are more likely to get cancelled for making statements than is warranted, both when compared with other people’s judgment of the same statement and with others’ expressed willingness to impose such sanctions. Their estimates are also only weakly related to how others judged the same statement. Although participants generally judged cancellation as similarly likely whether they imagined themselves or another person making a statement, this self-other asymmetry emerged once cancellation was framed explicitly as taking place on social media: participants then perceived a greater likelihood of cancellation when simply imagining themselves, rather than another person, making the same statement. Together, these findings suggest that people systematically overestimate the risk of being cancelled and raise the possibility that concerns about cancellation are fueled by inaccurate beliefs about others’ willingness to impose social sanctions.

articles.title: Artificial intelligence as work design: A scoping review of motivational and demotivational mechanisms in professional work contexts
articles.authors: Samira Hamouche; Josette M. P. Gevers; Jan C. Fransoo; Toni Waefler
articles.abstract:
Work design has long been recognized as a central determinant of intrinsic motivation, well-being, and performance. As artificial intelligence (AI) increasingly enters professional work, it should not be understood merely as a technical tool, but as a force that directly reshapes working conditions by altering how tasks are structured, decisions are made, feedback is delivered, and workers interact with their work and with one another. This makes work design and motivational theories especially important for the design and implementation of AI systems. To clarify this fragmented field within human-AI interaction research, including closely related work on human-AI collaboration and human-AI teaming, we conducted a scoping review of AI in professional, knowledge-intensive work contexts, focusing on how different AI configurations relate to motivation and demotivation. Following PRISMA-ScR, we reviewed 31 empirical and non-empirical sources published between 2009 and 2025.
Only three sources proposed explicit motivational AI design frameworks, suggesting that motivation is still more often treated as an outcome of AI use than as a direct design objective. Across the corpus, AI was described as motivationally supportive when it preserved meaningful human influence, provided developmental feedback, or reduced routine burden without hollowing out meaningful work. Demotivational risks were described when AI expanded surveillance, narrowed self-organization, prescribed work too tightly, or translated social relations into comparison and performance signals. These risks were especially prominent in conceptual discussions of managerial or algorithmic-management configurations, whereas empirical evidence was more concentrated in assistive AI settings.

articles.title: The Medusa Effect Revisited
articles.authors: Kaito Takashima; Takahiro Kawabe
articles.abstract: The Medusa effect refers to the cognitive phenomenon in which individuals depicted directly in a photograph (Level 1; L1) are judged as possessing more mind than individuals depicted within a photograph embedded inside another photograph (Level 2; L2). Although the Medusa effect has been replicated in several studies, a number of issues still remain to be addressed. One concerns the measurement scale used for mind perception, and another concerns the size of the face and body of the person depicted in the stimulus images. The present study addressed these unresolved issues across three experiments. Experiment 1 examined whether the Medusa effect could be replicated using the 18 concrete mental capacity items derived from Gray et al. (2007). Results showed that L1 targets were judged as possessing greater mental capacities than L2 targets across several capacities, particularly Agency-related capacities. Experiment 2 tested whether this effect would persist when L1 and L2 targets were presented at equal displayed sizes. Under size-controlled conditions, the Medusa effect was not observed. Experiment 3 independently manipulated target size and hierarchical frame cues. Presentation size showed significant main effects across multiple mental capacity judgments, whereas frame presence showed no reliable main effects. Together, these findings suggest that stimulus size contributes substantially to previously reported Medusa effect when mind perception is assessed using concrete mental capacity items.

articles.title: How Schemas Shape Memory for Everyday Activities: The Roles of Surprise and Prediction  Uncertainty
articles.authors: Tan Nguyen; Ken McRae; Jeffrey M. Zacks
articles.abstract:
Schemas provide structured knowledge that guides comprehension and memory of 
everyday experience. Schema violations have powerful effects on memory, but sometimes 
in apparently opposing directions—schema consistency can apparently boost or impair 
memory. We used narrative texts describing everyday activities to try to resolve the 
apparently conflicting effects. We investigated three fundamental issues: how schema 
consistency and violation affect memory formation, what dimensions of schema violation 
contribute to subjective surprise, and how prediction uncertainty influences memory 
encoding. In Study 1, gist but not detail memory was monotonically better for more 
surprising events. This is consistent with the proposal that schema violations trigger 
additional processing, but not with the proposal that schema-consistent information 
garners any advantageous processing. Schema-consistent information was not forgotten 
more slowly over 24 hours. Study 2 investigated factors leading to schema-driven surprise. 
Surprise was driven primarily by an event's appropriateness (contextual fit) rather than its 
relevance to activity goals. Critically, inappropriate events elicited stronger surprise when 
they occurred during goal-relevant actions, consistent with predictive processing theories, 
suggesting that humans develop more precise predictions about functionally important 
aspects of activities. In Study 3, gist memory was better for high-uncertainty information, 
supporting proposals that additional attention is allocated to moments of uncertainty. 
Together, these findings suggest that gist memory is enhanced when events are surprising 
or uncertain, and that surprise arises from contextual inappropriateness modulated by goal 
relevance.

articles.title: Non-linear Sensitivity to Binaural Interaural Correlation Changes: The Impact of Correlation Change and Interaural Delay
articles.authors: Yizhen Yang; Liang Li
articles.abstract:
Human listeners’ ability to detect a binaural interaural correlation (BIAC) change is crucial for auditory scene analysis. The present study investigated whether human listeners can detect a binaurally correlation-changed arbitrary-noise fragment [a ‘break’ in correlation, break in interaural correlation (BIAC)] embedded in binaurally steady-noise markers, and further examined how this detection sensitivity is modulated by different levels of correlation change. The longest interaural delay was used to index the BIAC detection sensitivity.
In Experiment 1, twenty-three normal-hearing participants were tested for the longest interaural delay remained to detect a binaurally correlation-changed arbitrary-noise fragment embedded in identical arbitrary-noise (BIAC fragment correlations: 0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8). In Experiment 2, another twenty normal-hearing participants were tested for the longest interaural delay remained to detect a binaurally uncorrelated arbitrary-noise fragment in steady-noise (the background steady-noise correlations: 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1). 
As the BIAC correlation level increased, the longest interaural delay remained to detect the BIAC in identical noises decreased significantly (Exp 1); conversely, as the background steady-noise correlation level increased, the longest interaural delay remained to detect the uncorrelated BIAC increased significantly (Exp 2). Results showed that detection sensitivity is non-linearly modulated in both cases. Specifically, thresholds followed exponential decay (Exp 1) and growth (Exp 2) models, revealing a "mutation" in sensitivity as correlation contrast decreased. These findings suggest that people could detect a BIAC in steady-noise markers with binaural delay whether the correlation of BIAC fragment or background decrease, and this sensitivity was nonlinearly modulated by the magnitude of correlation change.

articles.title: Disrupted Geometric Constraints on Connectome Wiring in Children and Adolescents with ADHD
articles.authors: Nanfang Pan; Francis Normand; Arshiya Sangchooli; Christopher Adamson; Priscila Levi; Victor Barnes; Isaac Z. Pope; Stuart Oldham; Alexander Holmes; Trang Cao
articles.abstract: Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) is thought to result from aberrant neurodevelopmental wiring of connectome architecture, yet the precise mechanisms underpinning this miswiring remain unclear. Here, we used a generative mathematical model to understand how physical and geometric constraints on brain network development might be altered in the disorder. Leveraging multisite diffusion magnetic resonance imaging (dMRI) datasets comprising 713 ADHD children and 304 neurotypical controls (aged 6-18 years), we fitted the recently developed geometric eigenmode model (GEM) to individual data describing connectivity between each pair of cortical regions, as quantified using probabilistic tractography. Replicating past work, the GEM was able to recover both topological and topographical properties of the empirical connectomes, but reconstruction accuracy was lower in ADHD children, suggesting a relative untethering of connectome architecture from geometric constraints. This untethering was particularly salient for long-range connections, which were disproportionately affected in the ADHD cohort. Regional variations in the degree to which connectivity was untethered from geometry were correlated with spatial maps of cortical developmental expansion, myelination, and transcriptomic organization, consistent with a neurodevelopmental basis for geometric untethering that is spatially patterned along the cortical hierarchy. Together, these findings suggest that aberrant neurodevelopment in ADHD may untether connectome development from typical geometric and physical constraints, resulting in subtle disruptions of long-range inter-regional connectivity.

articles.title: Can conversational AI influence confidence in pre-existing political beliefs?
articles.authors: Semra Sevi; Can Serif Mekik
articles.abstract: Conversational artificial intelligence (AI) may influence political polarization by shaping the strength with which political attitudes are held. Generative AI systems are increasingly capable of conducting adaptive, human-like conversations, raising questions about their ability to strengthen or weaken belief certainty. We report results from a preregistered, two-wave randomized experiment (N = 1,968) testing whether GPT-4 can strengthen or weaken individuals' attitudes on salient, evolving political issues. Participants engaged in personalized, multi-turn conversations in which the AI was instructed to either strengthen or weaken their stated views, or to engage in non-political discussion. Across issues including housing, immigration, and economic policy, AI-mediated conversations consistently reduced attitude strength. Specifically, belief confidence, while showing no evidence of strengthening. These effects persisted for up to 21 days. Our findings indicate that generative AI can durably influence political attitudes, primarily by weakening rather than strengthening belief certainty. By examining both pro and counter-attitudinal persuasion among individuals with existing views in dynamic political contexts, this study provides a demanding test of AI’s persuasive capacity and clarifies its implications for political polarization and democratic discourse.

articles.title: Innovations in Practice: A Pilot Randomised Trial on Smartphone and Social Media Abstinence: Effects on Sleep Quality and Psychological Well-being in Adolescents
articles.authors: Emma Caitlin Sullivan; Scott Cairney; Lisa Henderson
articles.abstract: There is growing concern that smartphones and social media (S/SM) disrupt adolescent sleep, wellbeing and cognition, yet causal evidence from robust interventions is limited. To assess the acceptability of a total 21-day S/SM detox we conducted a preregistered (https://osf.io/79zd2) pilot randomised control trial with eighty-two adolescents aged 13-18 years from UK schools. Participants were randomly assigned to a total S/SM detox (N=26), total S/SM detox with access to a basic “brick” phone (N=26) or a business-as-usual control (N=30). Pre-and post-intervention measures of sleep, wellbeing, and cognition were collected using surveys, wearables, and cognitive tasks.  Retention was high, with 13% attrition across groups.
