At 21-days, one or both detox groups showed tentative improvements in sleep duration, daytime sleepiness, depressive symptoms, perceived stress, and working memory relative to the controls, however these changes were not maintained at a two-month follow-up. Open-ended post-detox reflections reflected a range of both positive and negative experiences, with reported challenges including boredom, functional reliance on smartphones, and fear of missing out.  Nevertheless, 83% endorsed the need for government action. Digital detoxes appear feasible for generating causal evidence; however, they can also pose functional challenges to young people. Fully powered trials should address these challenges, potentially via more targeted intervention approaches (e.g. switching off the internet on smartphones, particularly at bed-time).

articles.title: Chunking improves working memory via compression, not direct retrieval from long-term memory
articles.authors: Juan Manuel Chau Delgado; Joel Shebioba; Anna Pitt; Matias J. Ison; Nicholas E. Myers
articles.abstract: Working memory (WM) capacity is severely limited. Chunking, the process of grouping related items in WM, circumvents this limit and drastically improves performance. Despite decades of research, the mechanism underlying this benefit remains debated. One account proposes that statistical regularities allow multiple items to be compressed into structured WM representations, whereas another argues that structured associations are stored in long‑term memory (LTM) and reinstated at recall, effectively offloading storage demands. These accounts imply fundamentally different interactions between WM and long-term knowledge, yet in most paradigms, they make indistinguishable behavioural predictions. Here, we dissociate them by introducing small, continuous trial‑to‑trial variations in the hues of learned colour pairs, allowing us to test whether chunking benefits arise from compressed memory for the current hue (WM) or retrieval of long‑run feature means (LTM). Across two delayed colour report experiments, chunking reliably improved recall relative to unchunkable arrays. Participants remained sensitive to trial‑specific feature variations, indicating that responses were not driven by retrieval of feature means from LTM templates. At the same time, responses were systematically biased toward each item’s long‑run mean, especially on chunkable trials. To explain this bias, we fit psychophysical models derived from the competing compression and LTM‑retrieval accounts. The compression-based model, in which compression produces graded biases toward learned means within WM, outperformed the LTM-retrieval and baseline models. In a second experiment, we examined whether these biases arise during encoding/maintenance or at the recall stage. Cues in the maintenance period indicating which item would be probed improved recall accuracy but did not reduce bias toward learned structure, indicating that the bias arose during encoding or early maintenance rather than retrieval. These findings show that chunking enhances WM by integrating learned structure into WM representations through compression, rather than by offloading to LTM.

articles.title: Motivation Science in Digital and AI-Supported Learning Environments
articles.authors: Lisa Bardach; Mladen Rakovic; Rimantas Vosylis; Rasa Erentaite
articles.abstract: In this conceptual paper, we argue that motivation science provides a valuable yet underutilized framework for understanding and designing digital and AI-supported learning environments. To illustrate this, we first review studies included in a recent meta-analysis on generative AI and motivation to examine the extent to which motivation science informs current research. We show that the vast majority of studies do not use motivational theories to explain how such systems may support student motivation. Next, drawing primarily on achievement goal theory and the TARGET (task, autonomy, grouping, recognition, time) framework, we outline key motivational principles for structuring learning environments and discuss how these principles may be enacted in digital and AI-supported contexts. We also examine risks and tensions associated with these environments, acknowledging that digital and AI systems may create novel motivational challenges not fully addressed by existing motivational theories developed in more traditional educational settings. Overall, the paper aims to stimulate further research on rethinking digital and AI-supported learning environments through the lens of motivation science while also reconsidering motivation science in light of these emerging environments.

articles.title: Can callous-unemotional (CU) traits be acquired? Tests in a longitudinal sample spanning late childhood through adolescence
articles.authors: Emily C. Kemp; Meghan Dontha; Zeying Du; Herry Patel; William E. Pelham
articles.abstract: Background. Callous-unemotional (CU) traits (i.e., “Limited Prosocial Emotions”) characterize a particularly severe group of antisocial youth. “Secondary” CU traits, as opposed to “primary,” are distinguished by co-occurring anxiety and are theorized to reflect an acquired phenotype that develops through an interaction between heightened emotional sensitivity and prolonged adversity exposure. The aim of this study was to test this pathway in a large, diverse sample spanning late childhood (age 9-10) to middle adolescence. Methods. Data were drawn from the Adolescent Brain and Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, with youth (N = 11, 880; Mage = 10; 48% female) assessed annually over five timepoints. Retrospective and prospective analyses examined the effects of emotional sensitivity and different forms of adversity on the development of CU traits. Results. Retrospective analyses showed no main or interactive effects of emotional sensitivity and adversity during late childhood in predicting secondary CU traits by adolescence. Instead, a significant interaction (B = .21, SE = .06, p < .001, RRR = 1.24) was found such that higher overall adversity and emotional sensitivity together increased risk for primary, not secondary, CU traits. Prospective analyses among emotionally sensitive (≥90th percentile) youth with no CU traits (n = 365, age ~10) similarly showed no forms of cumulative adversity predicted increases in or the onset of “elevated” CU traits after four years. Conclusions. Findings did not support a distinct adversity-linked pathway to secondary CU traits via emotional sensitivity. Instead, results were consistent with alternative models in which secondary CU traits reflect a comorbid profile marked by broader clinical severity or overlapping liabilities (e.g., negative affectivity, internalizing risk).

articles.title: Semantigories: A large-scale investigation of form-meaning interactions and category-level differences in semantic memory search
articles.authors: Abhilasha Ashok Kumar; Channing Everidge Hambric; Larry Zhang; Michael N. Jones
articles.abstract: Humans routinely search for information within their mental lexicon, and this process involves navigating across clusters of related items within a complex lexical landscape. However, concepts can be related in several ways, and prior work has examined semantic memory search primarily within taxonomic categories (e.g., Animals) and underemphasized interactions between lexical sources that guide search and retrieval. The present work examined memory search via the verbal fluency task across different semantic categories as an explore-exploit trade-off mediated by semantic, phonological, and frequency-based information within the lexicon. This account was tested on a large-scale compilation of semantic fluency data (N=1245) across six diverse categorical domains (Animals, Occupations, Things at the Beach, Things in a Classroom, Things to Rescue from a Burning Home and Excuses for Being Late), being made openly available for future research via https://semantigories.research.bowdoin.edu/. Search behavior was consistent with a strategic foraging account within the taxonomic category Animals, whereas search in categories with less rigid organization was relatively impaired, highly idiosyncratic, and relied more on short-range transitions indicating a strong tendency to select the most similar and accessible items. Fluency performance was enhanced for individuals who adequately leveraged form-meaning mappings within structured and functional categories, suggesting that memory search involves both automatic and strategic components. The present results thus highlight the flexibility of both semantic representations and search processes in human cognition.

articles.title: Look at Me Being Good – Connecting Moral Grandstanding with Affective Polarization and Civic Engagement
articles.authors: Nadine Knab; Emina Labeau; Norina Emechafor
articles.abstract: In polarized societies where public moral discourse is often performed in front of broad audiences, understanding the psychological dynamics of moral grandstanding has become crucial for explaining both social division and civic engagement. Moral grandstanding, defined as the use of moral and political discourse to seek social status, has been linked to affective polarization, yet less is known about how this moralized self-presentation relates to civic engagement and helping behavior. Across four studies (N total = 1720), we examine how moral grandstanding is associated with affective polarization and different forms of helping in the context of refugee support and intergroup relations. Study 1 (N = 503) replicates previous findings showing that moral grandstanding positively predicts affective polarization, even when controlling for intellectual humility. Study 2 (N = 774) demonstrates that dichotomous thinking is statistically implicated in the association between moral grandstanding and affective polarization, including in a more right-leaning sample. Study 3 (N = 212) shows that moral grandstanding is positively related to both dependency-oriented and autonomy-oriented helping intentions, as well as to public and private forms of intergroup civic engagement; these associations are partially mediated by affective polarization. Study 4 focuses on a sample of self-identified activists (N = 231) and reveals that moral grandstanding is not only positively associated with reported intentions to help – both in dependency- and autonomy-oriented ways – but also with self-reported past helping behavior.

articles.title: The Development of Superstitions in Uncontrollable Environment
articles.authors: Haoyang Lu; Tianyuan Teng; Hang Zhang
articles.abstract: Superstitions are false beliefs about causality and illusory control over outcomes. Although previous research has explored factors that influence superstitious beliefs, the cognitive processes underlying their formation remain unclear. We designed a task environment allowing free exploration of a vast number of actions with uncontrollable outcomes to understand the development of superstitions over experience. Participants (N=281) played a game where they attempted to produce rewarding keypress sequences across 100 trials with randomly assigned reward contingencies, reporting perceived reward probability and controllability. We found that perceived controllability increased non-linearly with reward rates, plateauing after 50%. Reward predictions and controllability showed a bidirectional feedback loop, reinforcing each other. Personality traits such as superstition proneness, locus of control, and schizotypy also influenced perceived controllability. Our results delineate how superstitious beliefs emerge from an interplay between environmental reward statistics, exploratory tendencies, and psychological traits, involving distorted perceptions of causality.

articles.title: The Affective Nature of Affective Polarization: Evidence from Physiological and Self-Reported Responses to U.S. Politicians
articles.authors: Kevin Arceneaux; Bert N. Bakker
articles.abstract: What is the nature of affective partisan polarization? We answer this question in a preregistered laboratory study conducted in the United States measuring partisans' affective reactions tostatic images of U.S. politicians with self-reports and physiological indicators of valence and arousal. Consistent with prior research, participants reported substantially more negative evaluations of out-party than in-party politicians. However, we do not observe reliable physiological differentiation between in-party and out-party elites under these conditions. This disconnect between self-reported negative affect and concurrent physiological responses suggests that, in the context of minimal elite exposure, affective polarization can be expressed without detectable visceral activation. These findings are consistent with constructivist and attitude-retrieval accounts and constrain strong versions of the hot cognition thesis, though they do not logically exclude all possible roles for visceral affect under different conditions.

articles.title: Exploring the Influence of Music’s Semantic Associations on Autobiographical Memories
articles.authors: Fruzsina Lilla Szücs; Kelly Jakubowski; Tuomas Eerola
articles.abstract:
While the effect of music on autobiographical memories has been examined in the past, it has not 
yet been studied how semantic associations with music cues influence the content and 
phenomenology of autobiographical memories. In a repeated-measures design experiment with 
53 participants, we tested how 14 musical cues depicting semantic associations, in either a more 
concrete (e.g., “birds”) or abstract (e.g., “French café ambience”) manner, influenced the recall 
of autobiographical memories and the phenomenological characteristics of these. Some music 
excerpts evoked a high proportion of memories containing our expected semantic association 
(e.g. “Loss” and “Latin-American ambience”). Others were less successful in evoking the 
expected semantic association, but nevertheless evoked memories with a high degree of shared 
content (e.g., “Calmness”). There were no statistically significant differences found in the 
phenomenological characteristics of the memories evoked by more concrete versus abstract 
music cues. These results indicate that music can convey shared semantic meaning across 
listeners that shapes the content of associated memories, although this capacity varies 
substantially across different musical cues. Findings from this study have implications for how 
music may be used as a memory cue in comparison to other cue types in both clinical and 
everyday settings.

articles.title: Perinatal Engagement With a Purpose-Built AI for Mental Health (Ash): Emotional Expression, Help-Seeking, and Estimated Depression Trajectories Across Pregnancy and Postpartum
articles.authors: Shannon E Cusack; Ting Su; Thomas Hull; Caitlin A. Stamatis
articles.abstract:
Objective: Perinatal mood disorders are common and consequential yet underdiagnosed and undertreated. Conversational AI mental health tools may help close this gap, but real-world evidence on how perinatal individuals engage with them remains limited. We aimed to characterize naturalistic engagement with a purpose-built mental health AI tool (Ash) across the perinatal period, using a theory-informed emotion taxonomy and predicted depression-severity trajectories.

Methods & Analysis: We conducted a retrospective analysis of conversation logs (May 2025-January 2026). An LLM classifier followed by clinical psychologist review of every classifier-positive case yielded 419 confirmed perinatal users (4,087 sessions). Sessions were coded by an LLM judge against a 26-label, six-domain Perinatal Emotion Expression Taxonomy anchored in established perinatal theory. A separate model produced continuous PHQ-9 estimates over two-week windows. A date-matched cohort of 46,134 non-perinatal users served as comparison, and all high- and crisis-risk sessions received blinded clinician review of AI escalation.

Results: Perinatal users differed from the general cohort by content (parenting d = +1.34, family d = +0.72) rather than overall distress. Sessions were emotionally multidimensional (mean 5.38 labels; 77.8% endorsed ≥3 concurrent) with relational and attachment content dominant (75.9%). Distress followed a non-monotonic course with two troughs (second trimester; 6 weeks-3 months postpartum). Across the postpartum year, positive affect declined and identity/role strain and emotional exhaustion rose monotonically. Among users entering at moderate severity or above, model-estimated PHQ-9 declined by 2.41 points over a median follow-up of 8 weeks (d = -0.58; 27.1% reliable improvement). Safety-relevant content appeared in 11.0% of sessions, with no confirmed safety gaps after clinician adjudication (0/50).

Conclusions: Perinatal AI-support users present with an emotionally complex, relationally-anchored profile that does not resolve in the early postpartum window as assumed and is qualitatively distinct between pregnancy and postpartum. Findings support perinatal-specific AI design, identify the second trimester as a proactive engagement window, and bolster the case for extended postpartum screening.

articles.title: AI vs. Humans: Does AI Face Harsher Moral Judgments? A Meta-Analysis
articles.authors: Olivia Hill-Cousins; Joseph Sweetman; Cassandra J Lowe
articles.abstract:
Research on how people judge AI compared to humans remains fragmented despite its criticality
in resolving a multitude of current AI ethical debates. This pre-registered meta-analysis (OSF:
10.17605/OSF.IO/GUHPQ; PROSPERO: CRD420251237117) addresses this by systematically reviewing
experimental evidence from 50 experimental studies (363 comparisons) comparing moral judgments of
human and AI agents’ decisions in moral dilemmas (e.g. the trolley problem) which were extracted from
PubMed, Web of Science and Elicit. Standardized effect sizes (Hedge’s g) were computed for two-cell
designs (Agent: Human vs. AI). Risk of bias was assessed using a pre-registered checklist for experimental
designs. Results from a three-level random-effects meta-analysis show that AI agents’ moral decisions are
judged as less morally permissible than equivalent human decisions (g = -0.08, p = 0.035). Among several
moderation analyses, perceived mental capacity (PMC) – ascribed through proxies such as agency and
experience – significantly reduced and reversed the negative effect (g = 0.11, p = 0.007). Higher PMC
corresponded to smaller or reversed differences between moral evaluations of human and AI decisions.
Interpretability was constrained by substantial heterogeneity across designs. Together, these findings
reveal asymmetries in the moral frameworks guiding evaluations of human and AI agents and point to
psychological factors that may contribute to variability in human-AI moral expectations. No external
funding.

articles.title: Testing observation-scale linking functions between surprisal and online measures of sentence processing
articles.authors: Alexandre Menezes Barroso
articles.abstract: Contextual surprisal reliably predicts online language-processing measures, but the functional form of that relationship is difficult to interpret because observed measures are not latent cognitive costs. Eye movements and event-related potentials are outputs of measurement systems, task constraints, and response definitions; therefore, nonlinear effects of surprisal on those measures can arise either before or after the latent cost that a theory may posit. This article estimates observation-scale linking functions from surprisal to online sentence-processing measures in two existing datasets: a Dutch co-registered eye-tracking/EEG corpus and an English sentence-aligned dataset with eye-movement, self-paced reading, and ERP measures. The analysis uses power-law transformations of surprisal, selected by blocked cross-validation inside the full covariate model. Dutch analyses use matched content-word observations for eye-tracking and N400 comparisons, include SUBTLEX-NL frequency, and analyze reading times in untransformed milliseconds with Gamma models, with raw-OLS and log-OLS sensitivity analyses. English source comparisons use a common observation mask for transformer and n-gram surprisal estimates. Surprisal significantly predicted every primary Dutch measure and most English measures. However, after matched exclusions, frequency control, raw-time modeling, and one-standard-error/practical-equivalence checks, the Dutch data did not require a nonlinear exponent: ordinary surprisal was practically competitive for all primary measures. The English data showed more limited and model-dependent departures, with Gamma reading-time models favoring non-unit exponents for some eye-movement measures, while N400 and P600 analyses did not provide decisive evidence for distinct nonlinear exponents. These results support surprisal as a robust predictor while showing that observation-scale linking claims require scale-sensitive diagnostics rather than inference from point estimates alone.

articles.title: Criminal Evidence Indexes for Taxometric Investigations of Guilty-Suspect Base Rates and Classifications
articles.authors: Dario Rodriguez; David Michael Zimmerman
articles.abstract: Taxometric methods may help researchers estimate guilty-suspect base rates in the criminal justice system and develop procedures for classifying individual suspects. This program requires that criminal evidence be quantified for statistical analysis, but no appropriate indicator variables exist. We discuss the criteria for suitable taxometric indicators and outline characteristics that indexes of criminal evidence should meet. We derive quantitative indexes of four types of criminal evidence: eyewitness identifications, interrogations and confessions, forensic match evidence, and propensity evidence. We sketch how the proposed indexes could be validated within the taxometric program. Further, we illustrate how researchers can empirically probe critical assumptions underlying these indexes, quantify the sizes of departures from these idealizations, and leverage these estimates to modify and improve index calculations. The proposed indexes address an outstanding impediment to the realization of the taxometric program to study guilty-suspect base rates in the criminal justice system.

articles.title: Scoring-Condition Drift in Rater-Scored Performance Assessment: A Feature-Warrant Framework
articles.authors: Alexandre Menezes Barroso
articles.abstract: Rater-scored performance assessments support placement, certification, progress monitoring, and program evaluation only when scores retain the same meaning across administrations. This article develops a feature-warrant framework for studying scoring-condition drift: changes in the relation between observable performance features and assigned scores when prompts, rubrics, rater training, scoring interfaces, stakes, or decision rules change. The framework distinguishes uniform score shifts from changes in feature weighting, and separates rater-side drift from performer adaptation. A concrete performance-assessment ecology is used to define scoring conditions, features, and warrants before formal models are introduced. Drift is then represented through feature-by-condition interactions, transformed alignment indices, multivariate drift summaries, and decision-impact quantities. Published speaking-assessment evidence illustrates the framework: identical speech samples can support different feature weights under different rating prompts, and fluency evidence is often profile-based rather than reducible to single features. The article closes with designs for planned studies, bridge audits, small-program monitoring, and reporting decisions tied to operational consequences and score use in routine and revised assessment systems.

articles.title: When Empathy Gets Tough: Neural Responses to Conflicting Self- and Partner-Directed Feedback Correlate with Trait Emotion Regulation and Daily Support
articles.authors: Shanshan Ma; Erin L Maresh; Andrea M Coppola; Katherine E. Richard; Leonie Koban; Diego Guevara Beltran; David Sbarra; Jessica R. Andrews-Hanna
articles.abstract: Empathy lies at the core of social interactions, particularly in intimate relationships, yet few laboratory tasks are designed to capture empathy in interpersonal contexts—especially those requiring individuals to simultaneously process social information directed toward both the self and a romantic partner. In this exploratory study, 131 adults (from 71 romantic couples) completed a neuroimaging paradigm that manipulated whether positive or negative partner-directed feedback was presented alone or alongside self-directed feedback. The partner-alone feedback contrasts (partner-directed feedback alone versus a neutral control condition) robustly engaged brain regions associated with empathic responding, including the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex, anterior insula, dorsomedial prefrontal cortex and temporoparietal junction. Notably, during the incongruent feedback contrasts—simultaneous conflicting self- and partner-directed feedback versus self-directed feedback alone—participants exhibited diminished affective and brain responses but evidenced unique brain activity in frontoparietal areas frequently associated with cognitive control (e.g., dorsolateral prefrontal cortex and supramarginal gyrus). Furthermore, when responding to positive partner-directed feedback in the presence of negative self-directed feedback, enhanced activity in the dorsomedial prefrontal cortex was associated with lower self-reported difficulties in emotion regulation and more partner-rated support provided in everyday life. Taken together, these findings suggest that salient self-focused information may dampen individuals’ emotional and neural responses to their partners’ uplifting and critical feedback, that emotion regulation tendencies may be an important between-subjects moderator of this effect, and that individual differences in spontaneous neural responses during this task were associated with meaningful real-world outcomes, although further validation and replication are warranted.

articles.title: Framing effects in explanation: How win-loss framing shapes political explanations
articles.authors: Jeffrey C Zemla; Sara Emily Burke
articles.abstract: People use explanation to make sense of complex real-world events, yet little work has examined whether explanations are susceptible to framing effects. In two studies, we explored whether framing the outcome of the 2024 U.S. presidential election as a win (“Why did Donald Trump win the election?”) or a loss (“Why did Kamala Harris lose the election?”) alters how people generate and evaluate explanations. In Experiment 1 (N = 301), participants listed reasons for the election outcome. Framing influenced both the number and kind of reasons used to explain the outcome. In Experiment 2 (N = 302), participants evaluated explanations while the frame was manipulated. Endorsement of explanations again depended on framing. We discuss these results in the context of two hypotheses: a memory retrieval hypothesis, in which a candidate’s name serves as a retrieval cue for generating explanations, and a pragmatic hypothesis, in which people assess the relevance of the explanans to the explanandum. While both factors may lead to a framing effect, our results indicate a strong influence of pragmatics, especially in Experiment 2 where retrieval demands are minimal. Collectively, the results demonstrate a strong framing effect on explanation. Notably, these findings run counter to prevailing theories of explanation that propose explanations are judged by their probability since probability is not affected by framing.

articles.title: SCEIMA: Social Coordination Evaluation through Integrated Model Analysis
articles.authors: Bavo Van Kerrebroeck; Caroline Palmer; Guillaume Dumas; Alexander Pantelis Demos
articles.abstract: Computational models are increasingly used as interactive partners in studies of human coordination, yet it remains unclear whether observed differences in human behavior reflect properties of the models themselves, changes in human behavior elicited by such artificial partners, or both. We introduce SCEIMA (Social Coordination Evaluation through Integrated Model Analysis), a two-stage framework designed to disentangle human-specific, model-specific, and interaction-driven contributions to coordination in human–machine interaction paradigms. In the empirical stage, human participants perform a coordination task with both human partners and computational models, establishing reference human–human and human–model interaction patterns. In the analytical stage, the same models are paired with one another and optimized through simulations to reproduce empirical coordination metrics. Comparing human–human, human–model, and simulated model–model interactions reveals whether coordination differences arise from intrinsic model dynamics, from human adaptation to artificial partners, or from their interaction. SCEIMA treats computational models as contrastive instruments whose capacity to elicit and reproduce human behavior can be systematically evaluated. We illustrate the framework with two distinct case-studies, a sensorimotor synchronization task and a conversational turn-taking task, showing how distinct outcome patterns diagnose the sources of coordination differences. By providing a principled methodological framework for evaluating interactive computational models, SCEIMA improves interpretability in human–machine interaction research and informs the design of artificial agents that coordinate with humans more naturally and responsively.

articles.title: Partnership-Centered Audiology Service Model for Older Adults with Hearing Loss
articles.authors: Steven P Gianakas; Tatiana Lyons; Katherine Teece; Peggy B. Nelson
articles.abstract:
Purpose
	Hearing loss can affect both the individual with hearing loss and their communication partner. Perspectives on communication abilities can differ between an individual with hearing loss and their communication partner, leading to differences in perceived communication outcomes. The purpose of this mixed-methods study is to develop effective ways to involve communication partners in aural rehabilitation and measure the degree to which mismatches change following a brief virtual intervention session.
Method
	Adult clients with hearing loss and their identified communication partners completed the Social Participation Restrictions Questionnaire, Hearing Handicap Inventory for the Elderly, and Significant Other Scale of Hearing Disability questionnaires before and shortly after the virtual intervention session. During the session, participant pairs completed a listening activity, watched a brief video about hearing loss, and answered structured interview questions.
Results
Strong, positive correlations were measured for the percentage of the conversation that the client understood, but correlations were weaker for listening effort expended by the client and their willingness to continue in conversation. Measures of agreement using all three questionnaires did not significantly improve following intervention. Main themes emerging from the interviews suggested that communication strategies organically develop within successful relationships, audiology appointments can miss the mark, and understanding the emotional impacts of hearing loss is key. 
Conclusions
	Even a brief amount of aural rehabilitation may lead clients and their communication partners to greater understanding about the experience of hearing loss and strategies for improved communication success.

articles.title: A single perceptual comparison is sufficient, but not necessary, to induce lasting memory distortion
articles.authors: Joseph M. Saito; Keisuke Fukuda
articles.abstract: Humans constantly compare their visual memories with similar visual inputs to modify their behavior adaptively. However, basic and applied studies suggest that these comparisons can produce systematic distortions in observers’ memory reports that persist across time and contaminate subsequent reports. This has led to the hypothesis that perceptual comparisons, while adaptive, are both necessary and sufficient to produce persistent memory distortions. In three experiments, we tested this hypothesis by independently manipulating demands for perceptual comparisons and memory reports across a pair of memory tests separated by a 24-hour delay. At each test, observers were cued to recall colored object silhouettes that they had learned previously. At the first test, following cued recall, the silhouette was presented again in a similar color to serve as a perceptual probe to be compared with memory or to be used in a control task where comparisons were omitted and attention to the probe varied. Afterwards, observers either reported their memory of the recalled silhouette or not. Memory reports at the first test exhibited attractive biases toward the probe after comparisons and each of the control tasks. While biases persisted into the second test following comparisons, as previously demonstrated, they also persisted when the control task required processing of the probe that was commensurate with comparisons. In both cases, persistence was not contingent upon making an initial memory report. Therefore, a single perceptual comparison is indeed sufficient for memory distortions to persist, but persistence may be primarily determined by attentional engagement with similar visual inputs.

articles.title: The structure and degree of social connection is associated with the structure and idiosyncrasy of social inference
articles.authors: David Dodell-Feder; Tamar Galvin; Tara Augenstein
articles.abstract: Social connection is associated with broad aspects of social cognition. Here, we probe the nature of this association in two studies (Ns=305, 759) with college-aged samples in which we measured different aspects of social (dis)connection and performance on a measure of social inference. We find that intersubject differences in social connection mirrored intersubject differences in social inference, suggesting a shared fingerprint between the experience of one’s relationships and the perception of social information. Further, greater levels of social disconnection were associated with greater idiosyncrasy in social inference, which was largely driven by task inaccuracy. This finding suggests that social disconnection may distort internal models of mental states in a way that pulls individuals from a canonical understanding of minds. Although findings were specific to Study 2, these results provide important context to how social connection relates to social cognition and highlights social cognition as a potential intervention target.

articles.title: Identifying the relationship between Type 1 Diabetes management and symptomology of ARFID
articles.authors: Luke Wild; Emma Smillie
articles.abstract: The present study aimed to identify a link between Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus (T1D) and Avoidant-Resistant Food Intake Disorder (ARFID). Whilst previous research has identified a difference in food intake and body dissatisfaction in those with T1D, and a small amount of research identified food avoidance behaviours in those with T1D, these three factors have not been investigated together. The present study investigated food avoidance, body dissatisfaction and food frequency in those with T1D (n = 119) and without T1D (n = 119) using the Fear of Food Questionnaire (FoFQ), Body Appreciation Scale 2 (BAS-2), and the Food Frequency Questionnaire (FFQ). Three independent t-tests were conducted, which found those with T1D reported significantly higher food avoidance, significantly greater body dissatisfaction, yet no difference in food frequency, partially supporting the hypothesis of an alignment between T1D and ARFID due to the high food avoidance reported in those with T1D. However, this result is complicated due to no difference in food frequency between the two groups, suggesting the reported avoidance may be specific to certain foods. The small difference in body dissatisfaction could be explained by the increased prevalence of body dissatisfaction cited in those with T1D, and could reinforce this avoidance being due to reasons relating to ARFID rather than another disorder. Further research could investigate fear-based avoidance, with fear of hypoglycaemia (FoHo) and fear of hyperglycaemia (FoHe) resulting in this avoidance, strengthening a link between T1D management and ARFID.

articles.title: Personality Traits as Moderators of Human Trust and Reliance on Large Language Model-Powered Conversational Agents: A Scoping Review and the Personality–Trust Calibration Framework
articles.authors: Carson Conception Rodrigues; Simran Marian Rebello
articles.abstract:
Large language model (LLM)-powered conversational agents now mediate consequential decisions, yet individuals differ markedly in how far they trust and rely on them. We ask whether stable individual differences—particularly Big Five personality traits—moderate trust calibration and over-reliance in human–LLM interaction.

We conducted a scoping review following PRISMA Extension for Scoping Reviews (PRISMA-ScR) guidance, charting 60 empirical studies (22 examining contemporary generative LLMs) identified through structured multi-source searches (Semantic Scholar, OpenAlex, and arXiv) and backward citation chasing. We synthesised the evidence using a structured synthesis-without-meta-analysis (SWiM) approach with vote-counting by direction of effect.

Because much of the evidence base concerns adjacent technologies, we coded every study by AI-system type and separated findings that are LLM-specific from those extrapolated from broader human–automation research.

Three patterns are robust. First, Neuroticism and trait anxiety are the most consistent individual-difference correlates of LLM over-reliance and dependency, operating through an affect-regulation and cognitive-offloading pathway. Second, Agreeableness is associated with more positive AI attitudes and greater susceptibility to AI-generated persuasion, especially under anthropomorphic design, though not straightforwardly with problematic dependence. Third, Openness predicts AI adoption, positive attitudes, and trust, but we found no direct evidence that it elevates over-reliance.

Conscientiousness shows context-dependent and sometimes opposing effects, while Extraversion exhibits only modest effects on initial trust. Across studies, dispositional propensity-to-trust and system features—chiefly the form of explanation and uncertainty communication—are more proximal predictors of reliance than broad personality traits. Anthropomorphism shapes trust indirectly and conditionally rather than acting as a uniform amplifier.

We organise these findings within a proposed Personality–Trust Calibration (PTC) framework and extend it to agentic LLMs, where the relevant failure mode shifts from over-reliance on advice to over-delegation of action. The framework is offered as a structured research agenda rather than a validated causal model.

articles.title: Neural and Behavioural Signatures of Statistical Learning in Aging: Evidence from Fast Periodic Visual Stimulation
articles.authors: Claudia Ruzza; Davide Crepaldi
articles.abstract: Statistical learning (SL) enables the extraction of regularities from structured input and is thought to support visual word processing. Us- ing a Fast Periodic Visual Stimulation (FPVS) paradigm, we investi- gated whether sensitivity to frequency-based regularities is preserved in healthy aging and how such sensitivity is expressed at the neural and behavioural level. Fifty-one older adults and a comparison group of young adults completed EEG and behavioural tasks, alongside a classic visual statistical learning (VSL) task. Results revealed robust neural discrimination of frequency-based categories in older adults, indicating preserved sensitivity to statistical regularities, although responses were reduced in amplitude compared to young adults. Behaviourally, evidence of learning was weaker and more variable across tasks, and no reliable relationship emerged between neural and behavioural indices. In contrast, performance in the VSL task declined with age and was positively associated with behavioural discrimination performance. Together, these findings suggest a dissociation be- tween preserved neural sensitivity to regularities and their behavioural expression in healthy aging.

articles.title: Examining Relationships Between Caregiver Concerns and Family and Child Characteristics in Kindergarten Children at Risk for Language and Literacy Disorders
articles.authors: Melissa C. Phelan; Katharine Radville; Julie Wolter; Crystle Nicole Alonzo; Sarah Floyd; Jessie Ricketts; Coille A. Putman; Tiffany P. Hogan; Christie Davis
articles.abstract:
Purpose: Identification of children with language impairments often relies on caregivers raising concerns. However, caregivers may find it difficult to identify language impairment. This study examined how child and family characteristics were related to the likelihood that caregivers reported concerns about oral language and literacy to understand the possible underlying factors influencing identification. 
Method: In a larger longitudinal study, kindergarten children (N = 385) completed a battery of oral language and literacy assessments. The caregivers completed a questionnaire including demographic information (e.g., caregiver education level) and reported concerns in the areas of oral language, literacy, speech, and attention/memory. We used two multiple logistic regressions to evaluate the predictors of caregivers’ concern about (a) oral language and (b) literacy. 
Results: Among the children with language difficulties, less than 40% of caregivers reported oral language concerns. Among the children with word reading difficulty, just over half of the caregivers reported literacy concerns. Oral language scores and attention concerns were significant predictors of caregivers’ oral language concerns. Word reading scores, caregiver education, speech concerns, and attention concerns were significant predictors of literacy concern. 
Conclusions: Although interpretations of caregivers’ concerns may vary according to our provided checklist, these concerns appear to be meaningful indicators of child language and literacy difficulties. These difficulties—particularly in oral language—often go unrecognized. Our findings underscore the need for universal language screening in early education and expanded caregiver education regarding language impairment. Relying solely on caregiver concern may lead to missing children in need of services.

articles.title: First the Rules, Then the Summary: Piloting a Design for Accurate LLM-Generated Clinical Summaries
articles.authors: Yosef Sokol; Nechama Schwartz
articles.abstract: In clinical settings, mental health providers often have access to electronic health record (EHR) and other patient data of real clinical or predictive value. However, they rarely have the time or resources to pull it together and use it well. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) suggest they could fill this gap, but in mental-health applications LLMs often produce statements that do not match the source data. We developed a two-part synthesis pipeline. A rules-based Python component reads each patient’s scores from twelve validated questionnaires, labels them by published severity levels, and computes how they change over time. A language model then writes a prose narrative summary of the patient’s data. Our data came from a large longitudinal study of recovery after a suicidal episode, in which participants completed a battery of psychological assessments every two months. We tested the design across both open-weights and proprietary models, then evaluated it on a 25-subject cohort. For each subject we compared the constrained pipeline’s output to the same model given the same data and prompted to summarize it with no rules. Evaluation included an automated check of every numerical and categorical statement by the “harness”, which generated 2,067 checks across the three models, an independent review of the narrative prose by two raters working separately, and a clinician review (four completed out of a target of seven) whose preliminary ratings ran above the acceptability anchor on all five dimensions. Across the three models (Gemma 4 31B, Gemini 3 Flash, GPT-4.1), the harness identified one substantive content error, meaning one that would change a clinician’s reading, for a rate of 0.05%, against an unconstrained baseline in which about two in five checkable numerical statements were wrong. Every constrained brief also carried a traceable evidence-ID on each statement, as the design requires, while every unconstrained baseline brief contained at least one statement with no traceable basis in the source data. Two raters independently coded the narrative prose and found no hard errors (factual contradictions of the data) and soft errors in 8 of 25 briefs (32%), where information was combined across waves or measures, which the harness does not check. The constrained pipeline generalizes across the three models we tested and meets a strict accuracy standard. Its main open challenge is the LLM’s tendency to combine information across time points and measures.

articles.title: Polarity and the speed of scalar implicature processing
articles.authors: Michael Vrazitulis; J E Loy; Vera Demberg
articles.abstract: Scalar implicatures (SIs) such as the inference from ‘some’ to ‘some but not all’ are known to vary in processing cost. According to the Polarity Hypothesis (van Tiel et al., 2019; van Tiel and Pankratz, 2021), this cost depends on the polarity of the scalar term: SIs from positively polar expressions (e.g., ‘some’ implicating ‘some but not all’) are expected to be slower to compute than literal interpretations, while the reverse should hold for SIs from negatively polar expressions, sometimes referred to as indirect SIs (e.g., ‘not all’ implicating ‘not all but some’). We tested this prediction in a sentence–picture verification experiment with 538 English-speaking participants. Each participant judged whether sentences containing weak or strong scalar terms matched a corresponding picture, allowing us to measure response times as an index of processing effort. Our results showed no clear evidence for the Polarity Hypothesis; specifically, we found it to not generalise across the different scales included in our experiment. We additionally conducted a meta-analysis synthesising all existing evidence so far—this meta-analysis shows that, overall, the Polarity Hypothesis can be vindicated. Surprisingly, our results do also not clearly validate the traditional assumption of a B&N effect (Bott & Noveck, 2004), i.e., a general polarity-independent processing slowdown under SIs: a subsequent analysis including trial order revealed that the B&N effect was present in early trials of our study, whereas later trials do not show the effect. Overall, we conclude that polarity may influence the speed of scalar implicature processing, but that this effect is not robust across all types of scales.

articles.title: Collective Memory Modeling: Utilizing Individualized Representations to Drive Processing Models
articles.authors: Brendan Johns
articles.abstract: This article advances a new framework for computational memory modeling that integrates individual variability in semantic representation with computational models of memory processes. Although existing approaches to computational memory modeling have provided detailed accounts of encoding, retrieval, and decision dynamics, they typically assume fixed representations, overlooking individual differences in linguistic experience. Focusing on false memory in the Deese–Roediger–McDermott (DRM; Roediger & McDermott, 1995) paradigm, individualized distributional semantic models were constructed from social media sources and embedded within the Minerva 2 model of recognition memory while holding process parameters constant. This design isolates the contribution of representational variability to memory performance. Across simulations of 2,000 individual models, substantial variability emerged in item-level predictions. When using an optimized subset of individuals models, aggregated predictions produced strong correspondence with item-level data and exhibited a robust wisdom of the crowd effect (Steyvers & Miller, 2015), where the aggregated item-level data exceeded the performance of the best performing individual model. Additional analyses demonstrated that aggregation stabilizes behavioral predictions while preserving meaningful variability arising from differences in semantic structure. These findings provide an existence proof that representational diversity alone can account for systematic variation in memory behavior and improve predictive accuracy of a proposed model. More broadly, the results highlight the importance of incorporating individualized semantic knowledge into computational models, offering a unified and ecologically valid framework that bridges representation and process in the study of human memory.

articles.title: AI vs. Humans: Does AI Face Harsher Moral Judgments? A Meta-Analysis
articles.authors: Olivia Hill-Cousins; Joseph Sweetman; Cassandra J Lowe
articles.abstract:
Research on how people judge AI compared to humans remains fragmented despite its criticality
in resolving a multitude of current AI ethical debates. This pre-registered meta-analysis (OSF:
10.17605/OSF.IO/GUHPQ; PROSPERO: CRD420251237117) addresses this by systematically reviewing
experimental evidence from 50 experimental studies (363 comparisons) comparing moral judgments of
human and AI agents’ decisions in moral dilemmas (e.g. the trolley problem) which were extracted from
PubMed, Web of Science and Elicit. Standardized effect sizes (Hedge’s g) were computed for two-cell
designs (Agent: Human vs. AI). Risk of bias was assessed using a pre-registered checklist for experimental
designs. Results from a three-level random-effects meta-analysis show that AI agents’ moral decisions are
judged as less morally permissible than equivalent human decisions (g = -0.08, p = 0.035). Among several
moderation analyses, perceived mental capacity (PMC) – ascribed through proxies such as agency and
experience – significantly reduced and reversed the negative effect (g = 0.11, p = 0.007). Higher PMC
corresponded to smaller or reversed differences between moral evaluations of human and AI decisions.
Interpretability was constrained by substantial heterogeneity across designs. Together, these findings
reveal asymmetries in the moral frameworks guiding evaluations of human and AI agents and point to
psychological factors that may contribute to variability in human-AI moral expectations. No external
funding.

articles.title: Similar exercise adherence and physical fitness outcomes are observed across distinct motivation profiles in older adults participating in a home-based structured exercise programme
articles.authors: Alexandra Munns; Jack Feron; Joan Duda; Sindre Fosstveit; Kelsey Elizabeth Joyce; Foyzul Rahman; Linda Wheeldon; Hilde Lohne Seiler; Sveinung Berntsen Stølevik; Jet Veldhuijzen van Zanten
articles.abstract:
Purpose:

Motivation and self-efficacy are established predictors of engagement and persistence within structured exercise programmes; however, it remains unclear whether these psychological factors predict objective adherence metrics and associated physical fitness outcomes within home-based exercise programmes for older adults. This study adopted a person-centred approach to examine whether distinct psychological profiles predict adherence and fitness outcomes following a structured home-based exercise programme.

Methods:

Eighty-eight older adults (age = 67.2 ± 5.5 years) completed a 26-week home-based exercise programme. Objective adherence was defined as compliance with prescribed exercise volume and quantified using cumulative metabolic equivalent minutes (MET-mins) derived from heart rate–based measures of exercise intensity and duration. Psychological profiles were identified using latent profile analysis based on autonomous motivation, controlled motivation, and exercise self-efficacy. Cardiorespiratory fitness, muscular strength, flexibility, and mobility were assessed pre- and post-intervention.

Results:

Two psychological profiles were identified: an autonomous and highly confident profile and a moderately autonomous and confident profile. Controlled motivation was comparable between profiles, and no differences were observed in baseline demographic or physiological characteristics. Adherence was high and similar across profiles (e.g., cumulative MET-mins: 129 ± 66% vs. 116 ± 38%). Both profiles demonstrated significant and comparable improvements in cardiorespiratory fitness (V̇O₂peak), muscular strength, flexibility, and mobility.

Conclusions:

Baseline differences in self-efficacy and autonomous motivation did not predict objective adherence or fitness outcomes within a structured, well-supported home-based exercise programme for older adults. Consistent objective monitoring and regular behavioural support may therefore reduce the impact of psychological profile membership on adherence and outcomes.

Keywords: ageing; exercise adherence; home-based exercise; latent profile analysis; autonomous motivation; self-efficacy

articles.title: Designing Minds: Toward Systematic Integration of Psychological Science in Design for Augmented Humanity
articles.authors: Nejra van Zalk
articles.abstract:
Design for augmented humanity increasingly operates at the level of human cognition, emotion, and behaviour. As technologies extend and reconfigure human capabilities, design decisions play a constitutive role in shaping how individuals perceive, think, and act. Despite this, the integration of psychological science into design practice remains fragmented and unsystematic. While psychological concepts are widely referenced, they are often applied in selective and decontextualised ways, limiting both explanatory depth and practical effectiveness.
This paper advances the position that the central challenge is not the absence of psychological science in design, but the lack of its systematic integration. Building on this critique, it introduces a framework of levels of psychological grounding, describing progressive forms of integration from informal awareness and ad hoc application to structured incorporation in design processes, evaluation, and interdisciplinary collaboration. The framework provides a conceptual basis for analysing current practice and guiding more coherent integration.
The paper argues that such integration is essential for designing in the context of augmented humanity, where interventions increasingly influence cognition and behaviour at scale. Systematic psychological integration is therefore positioned as a necessary condition for developing design practices that are not only effective, but also transparent, accountable, and aligned with human well-being.

articles.title: A Tutorial for Navigating Ethical Dilemmas in Research: The Case of Early Language Interventions
articles.authors: Sho Tsuji; Kai Jia Tey; Camila Scaff; Alicia Horton-Mastin; Manel Khentout; Lucas Gautheron; Joseph Coffey; May Abdou; Alejandrina Cristia
articles.abstract: This tutorial paper aims to provide readers with tools to reflect on ethical dilemmas that illustrate how complex decisions can become when scientific goals meet real-world consequences. Through the fictional story of the Unicorn Research Team, we focus on a group of scientists, who find themselves in disagreement with each other regarding whether (or how) to be involved in an early childhood intervention aiming to change caregivers' behaviors. We introduce reflective strategies to help researchers make sense of their own intuitions and understand those of others. First, we show that individuals' views often reflect moral frameworks that incorporate consideration of duty, values and outcomes. Second, we invite readers to consider that moral intuitions emerge from human cognition. Third, we introduce Theory of Change, a method that forces us to clarify what our potential actions realistically can be, what the most important outcomes are, and what is the strength of evidence on the underlying causal chain. Rather than offering answers, this paper encourages researchers to pause, reexamine their instincts, and remain open to others' perspectives, using tools to help oneself, and each other, understand the basis for one's position.

articles.title: Time for Consensus: Open Challenges and Recommendations for Research on Individual Differences in Statistical Learning
articles.authors: Louisa Bogaerts; Davide Crepaldi; Haoyu Zhou; Ana Bautista; Kevin Brown; Li-Yun Wendy Chang; Ruth de Diego-Balaguer; Julia Hofweber; Erin Isbilen; Marc F Joanisse
articles.abstract: Statistical learning (SL), the ability to extract regularities from sensory input, is hypothesized to play a major role in many aspects of higher-level cognition. While earlier investigations centered on group-level effects, a growing body of research now focuses on individual differences in this ability. This consensus paper presents the collective insights of 27 researchers worldwide who are active in the field of individual differences in SL, with the goal of reflecting on the field’s progress so far while outlining key challenges going forward. Our discussion covers possible theoretical and operational definitions of SL, the trade-offs between controlled laboratory tasks and ecologically valid paradigms, considerations in interpreting correlations between SL performance and other cognitive abilities, issues of measurement reliability and statistical power, and rationales for adopting individual-differences versus group-level approaches. Based on these discussions, we outline nine actionable recommendations for the field to move forward, which are meant to lead to a new body of evidence that is more theoretically informed and more methodologically robust.

articles.title: Revealing the Mystery of Emotions in Sounds The Theory of Musical Equilibration Explains the Impact of Ordered Sounds as the Listener’s Identification with Processes of Will
articles.authors: Bernd Willimek; Daniela Willimek
articles.abstract: Science has long been concerned with the question of how it is possible for music to elicit emotions‒so far, however, with only moderate success. The theory of musical equilibration shines a light on this problem from a new perspective. The theory was first proposed in 1997 by German music theorist, Bernd Willimek, who further developed it with his wife and fellow theorist, Daniela Willimek. Music itself, the theory goes, cannot generate emotions directly. Rather, emotions should be understood as a response to the processes of will that are encoded in music. By identifying with these processes of will, listeners experience music emotionally. This is comparable to the situation of moviegoers who identify with the protagonist on screen and thereby vicariously experience emotions. In this work, the affirmative character of the major triad and the negative character of the minor triad are substantiated. According to the theory of musical equilibration, the volume at which a minor chord is played determines whether it is perceived as sorrow or anger. Furthermore, the authors discuss issues such as why a diminished seventh chord is well suited as the score for film scenes involving fear, or how an augmented chord can convey amazement and astonishment. To show the practical application of the theory of musical equilibration, the authors carried out tests with school (K-12) and university students (basic test part A, basic test part B, Rocky test).

articles.title: From Human to AI: How Delegation to AI Shifts Moral Responsibility Attribution
articles.authors: Philippe Sloksnath
articles.abstract:
As AI systems increasingly make moral decisions on behalf of humans, questions arise
about how this delegation reshapes moral responsibility. Yet little is known about
whether people hold AI systems responsible for delegated moral decisions, and whether
this differs from delegating to human agents. This study examines people’s willingness
to delegate moral decisions to AI systems or human agents, and how delegation relates
to the attribution of moral responsibility. Across two studies and six moral dilemmas (N =
669), the effects of agent type (human vs. AI), domain (business vs. healthcare), and
trade-off severity (low vs. high) on delegation and responsibility attribution were
examined. Results show no evidence that delegation differed between AI systems and
humans. When decision stakes are high, delegation increases. Furthermore, delegation
is associated with a partial transfer of perceived moral responsibility toward the
delegate, to both AI systems and humans, resulting in a mostly shared responsibility
pattern between delegator and delegate. The findings challenge machine aversion
assumptions and reveal a psychological disposition to attribute responsibility to AI
systems.

articles.title: Dimensions of Victimhood
articles.authors: Emily Kubin; Kurt Gray
articles.abstract: Moral and political conflicts revolve around victimhood —who is harmed, how severely, and even whether harm has occurred at all. Although humans have always cared about concrete interpersonal victimhood, the complexity of modern society creates many varieties of victimhood, which we organize along two dimensions: Scale (the number of victims) and Tangibility (the concreteness of victimization). These dimensions exist in both the mind and the world and are grounded in domain-general cognitive processes: epistemic trust and numerical cognition (scale), and ontological assumptions and linguistic scaffolding (tangibility). These dimensions help explain elements of political dynamics from polarization to misinformation, help map moral cognition to cognitive science, and suggest multiple directions for future research.

articles.title: Audiovisual Entertainment Media Narratives and Openness toward Underrepresented Groups: A Systematic Review
articles.authors: Joël Hendrix; Gijsbert Bijlstra; Rebecca de Leeuw; Wieke M. Knol; Marieke Fransen
articles.abstract: Research examining the relationship between exposure to audiovisual entertainment media narratives and openness toward underrepresented groups has been conducted across multiple disciplines, resulting in high heterogeneity in methods and fragmented findings. This systematic review synthesizes empirical studies (N = 104) across disciplines to examine this relationship and its underlying mechanisms. A comprehensive search yielded studies examining affective, cognitive, and behavioral dimensions of openness. Our synthesis revealed largely inconclusive findings across these dimensions. However, we found most evidence for associations with or effects on reduced prejudice and aversion outcomes. Additionally, we found the most conclusive evidence for outgroup identification as an underlying mechanism. The underrepresented groups for whom we found most evidence of relationships and effects were people with disabilities, disorders, and mental illness. While audiovisual entertainment media narratives can positively influence openness toward underrepresented groups, the mechanisms and conditions under which these effects occur require further investigation. Recommendations for research practices are discussed to advance understanding of entertainment media narratives in promoting openness toward underrepresented groups.

articles.title: The FRIEND Procedure: A Fast-Friends Paradigm With Nonviolent Communication Priming for Dyadic Belonging Research
articles.authors: Vince Jacques de Blauwe; Antonio Fidalgo
articles.abstract: The Fast Friends Procedure (FFP) is a widely used paradigm for generating interpersonal closeness in stranger dyads, but standard versions specify the disclosure prompts far more clearly than they specify how participants are prepared for reciprocal vulnerability (Aron et al., 1997; Sprecher, 2021). This design note documents the FRIEND procedure: a feasibility-stage adaptation of the FFP that introduced a brief Nonviolent Communication (NVC) primer immediately before the dyadic task, paired with the development of a provisional companion measure, the Dyadic Engagement and Safety Index (DESI-14) (Rosenberg, 2003). The study ran at the University of East London with 30 participants forming 15 stranger dyads, randomised to either a 15-minute NVC primer or a duration-matched body-scan mindfulness control delivered immediately before the FFP. This note documents the FRIEND protocol in enough detail for methodological reuse. It also traces how DESI-14 emerged from an earlier DESI-12 pilot rather than presenting the instrument as a finished product without lineage. The procedure was deliverable within a single laboratory session and generated a medium between-group Inclusion of Other in the Self (IOS) signal favouring the NVC condition (U = 160.5, p = .041, rank-biserial r = .43), though a complete-case analysis reduced this to p = .058, rendering it statistically fragile and the feasibility sample was not designed for confirmatory efficacy inference (Aron et al., 1992). Pilot evidence from DESI-12 suggested ceiling-prone items and under-specified coverage of inclusion, reciprocal attunement, and non-verbal safety signalling. Three items were removed and five new items added, yielding DESI-14 as a trial-stage instrument under active development rather than a completed psychometric product. The article contributes a replicable dyadic protocol and a transparent developmental record for a still-emerging emotional-safety measure.

articles.title: Measuring Artificial Intelligence Literacy: A Systematic Review of Instrument Development, Conceptual Foundations, and Psychometric Quality
articles.authors: Yueqiao Jin; Dragan Gašević; Roberto Martinez-Maldonado; Lixiang Yan
articles.abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) literacy is rapidly emerging as a defining competence of contemporary digital life, yet the ways in which it is measured remain fragmented and methodologically uneven. This study presents a systematic review of AI literacy measurement instruments, synthesising their conceptual foundations, development procedures, and psychometric evidence. Following the PRISMA protocol and guided by the COSMIN framework, we analysed 58 studies covering 47 unique AI literacy instruments published between 2014 and December 2025. The review mapped the measurement landscape, examined how AI literacy is conceptualised and operationalised, and evaluated evidence for content validity and other measurement properties. The results show that instrument development has accelerated sharply since 2023, coinciding with the widespread diffusion of generative AI technologies across educational and professional contexts. Conceptually, most instruments converge on a multidimensional core encompassing AI knowledge, ethical awareness, and practical engagement with AI systems. Methodologically, however, the evidence base remains uneven: while structural validity and internal consistency are frequently reported, evidence for content validity, criterion validity, and cross-cultural measurement invariance remains limited. Moreover, the field is dominated by self-report questionnaires, with relatively few performance-based or process-oriented assessments. These findings indicate that AI literacy measurement has expanded faster than its methodological consolidation. The review highlights the need for theoretically grounded, context-sensitive, and process-oriented approaches, potentially integrating learning analytics and longitudinal designs, to capture how individuals actually develop and enact AI literacy in increasingly AI-mediated learning environments.

articles.title: Predicting Multilevel Growth Trajectories Using a Random-Effect Diagnostic Classification Model
articles.authors: Kazuhiro Yamaguchi; Haruhiko Mitsunaga; Shun Saso; Yuri Uesaka
articles.abstract: Learning diagnosis is essential for effective education, with formative assessments shown to significantly enhance academic performance. Diagnostic classification models have been developed to assess students' learning status and provide remedial instruction. However, the impact of mastery or non-mastery of specific attributes on long-term learning development remains uncertain. If certain non-mastered attributes hinder the growth of mathematical ability, early intervention becomes essential. In this study, we developed a random-effects diagnostic classification model for multilevel growth curves (RDC–MGC) model to identify the specific effects of attribute mastery on individual-level mathematics ability growth. The simulation studies showed that the Bayesian estimation procedure provided appropriate parameter recovery and coverage probabilities, whereas ignoring the multilevel structure resulted in biased parameter estimates. The model was applied to arithmetic test data from second- to sixth-grade elementary school students. Diagnosis was conducted in the second grade, and the effects of mastery on mathematics ability growth from the third to sixth grades were assessed. The results showed that attribute mastery in second grade was associated with both the intercept and slope of individual ability growth, suggesting the potential importance of early-stage diagnostic information for understanding later mathematical development. Potential extensions of the proposed RDC–MGC model are also discussed.

articles.title: Linking behavioral and neural measures of visual search
articles.authors: Zoe (Jing) Xu; Kristin M. Woodard; Brenda Qiu; Bridget Leonard; Scott O. Murray
articles.abstract: Visual search is a ubiquitous behavior, yet its neural mechanisms remain incompletely understood. Here, we combine behavioral modeling and fMRI to identify neural signatures of core visual search computations. Participants performed visual search tasks in which targets differed from distractors along color, shape, or both dimensions, while attentional focus was experimentally manipulated. We show that behavioral search slopes – quantifying how response time scales with set size – are mirrored by corresponding fMRI-derived slopes, such that neural responses increase systematically with set size and scale with target-distractor similarity. This correspondence identifies a neural index of search difficulty and implicates regions in mid- and higher-level visual cortex in target-template matching processes. In addition, we replicate behavioral evidence for flexible attentional weighting of feature dimensions and demonstrate that this prioritization is reflected in modulation of neural activity in a subset of higher-level visual regions. Finally, we show that fMRI responses in bidimensional search can be predicted from activity measured during unidimensional search, demonstrating that computational models of behavior generalize to neural data. Together, these findings establish a quantitative link between behavioral and neural measures of visual search and provide a framework for studying attentional guidance and feature integration in the human brain.

articles.title: The Psychological, Emotional, and Relational Impact of Non-Suicidal Self-Injury on Parents: A Meta-Analysis Protocol
articles.authors: Kassandra Hon; Penelope Hasking; Stephen P Lewis; Katrina Hon; Mark Edward Boyes; Jennifer J Muehlenkamp
articles.abstract: Objective: This meta-analysis aims to systematically synthesise the evidence on the psychological, emotional, and relational experiences of parents of individuals who engage in non-suicidal self-injury (NSSI). Introduction: NSSI is a significant public health concern associated with adverse outcomes for both individuals and their families. While a substantial body of research has examined NSSI among young people, less attention has been given to its impact on parents and caregivers. Existing studies reveal that parents often experience considerable psychological distress, emotional burden, and disruption to family functioning following awareness of their child’s NSSI. However, a quantitative synthesis is needed to clarify the magnitude and direction of these parental outcomes and to identify gaps in the current evidence base to inform future research and the development of targeted supports for parents. Inclusion criteria: The review will include empirical studies examining parents or caregivers of individuals who engage in NSSI. Eligible studies must report at least one outcome related to parental psychological, emotional, or relational experiences. Methods: The review will follow the PRISMA guideline for meta-analyses. Electronic searches will be conducted across Medline, Scopus, ProQuest, and Web of Science. Two reviewers will independently screen studies, extract data, and assess risk of bias using standardised tools. Pooled effect sizes and random effect models will be calculated using meta-analysis. Results and Conclusion: This meta-analysis will provide a synthesis of the psychological, emotional, and relational experiences of parents of individuals who engage in NSSI and identify gaps in the literature. Findings will inform future research and support the development of evidence-informed interventions and supports for parents and caregivers of individuals who self-injure.

articles.title: Age and Gender Differences in Body Talk Frequency and Longitudinal Associations Between Body Talk and Body Image Variables
articles.authors: Nahori Ito; Akira Hasegawa
articles.abstract: The first purpose of this study was to investigate age and gender differences in three types of body talk: negative fat talk, negative muscle talk, and positive body talk. The second purpose was to examine longitudinal associations between body talk and body image variables at the within-person level, while distinguishing them from between-person associations. In this three-wave online study, a total of 5,707 women and men aged 15–94 years participated in the Wave 1 survey, and 3,597 completed two follow-up surveys administered at two-month intervals. Participants completed measures of body talk, body fat dissatisfaction, drive for muscularity, body appreciation, and the internalization of thin and athletic body ideals at each wave. Analyses of Wave 1 data indicated that negative fat talk was characteristic of women in their teens and twenties, whereas negative muscle talk was characteristic of men in the same age groups. Random-intercept cross-lagged panel model analyses of the three-wave data revealed that most longitudinal associations between body talk and body image variables at the within-person level were not statistically significant across age and gender groups. In contrast, significant between-person associations were observed across age groups in both women and men, including positive correlations between negative fat talk and body fat dissatisfaction and between positive body talk and body appreciation. These longitudinal findings suggest that previously reported associations between body talk and body image variables may largely reflect relatively stable individual differences, with less clear evidence for a mutually reinforcing relationship between body talk and body image variables.

articles.title: Learning in context: Socioemotional stimuli enhance cognitive learning processes but not psychophysiological engagement in early adolescence
articles.authors: Meriah Lee DeJoseph; Kathleen M. Thomas; Willem Frankenhuis; Daniel Berry
articles.abstract: Throughout development, we learn through trial and error in the face of uncertainty. This ‘reinforcement learning’ supports youth’s ability to adapt to environmental demands. Theory suggests that early experiences optimize learning to prioritize socially relevant cues across multiple levels of analysis. Yet, experimental paradigms often use abstract stimuli and limit measures of learning to behavioral summaries such as task accuracy. This study uses computational models grounded in reinforcement learning and dynamic systems frameworks to examine how socioemotional context modulates cognitive and psychophysiological learning processes. In a sample of 56 youth aged 12-15 years, a within-person experimental design quantified continuous behavior and heart rate (~700 observations of behavior and heart rate, per person) during an adapted reinforcement learning task with stimuli that varied in socioemotional relevance. A series of Bayesian multilevel models revealed that, compared to traditionally-used non-emotional stimuli, learning from stimuli high in socioemotional arousal enhanced behavioral performance. Specifically, computational models suggested that socioemotional salience may elicit faster value-updating processes and a qualitative shift to more exploitative decision-making. Underlying psychophysiological engagement was modulated by heightened sensitivity to learning from rewards, showing faster value-updating in the context of rewards aligned with more optimal psychophysiological flexibility and organization. Together, these findings clarify the contexts and modulatory processes that shape developmental learning mechanisms.

articles.title: “Unbrake my rejected heart”: Cardiac slowing when maltreated youth reconnect with peers who resemble caregivers
articles.authors: Dorukhan Açıl; Jan Keil; Angelika Bracher; Pascal Vrticka; Tobias Stalder; Nina Alexander; Michael Crowley; Kai von Klitzing; Lars O. White
articles.abstract: Adverse caregiving experiences are thought to bias internal representations of self and others, giving rise to negative expectations that generalize to new encounters. However, few studies examine whether cues signaling resemblance to caregivers selectively increase the odds of generalization. In this preregistered study, 133 participants were assessed for maltreatment at two waves (n = 65 maltreated; ages 4–16 and 11–24 years). At the second wave, participants played Cyberball. Following social exclusion, participants re-engaged with two novel co-players, who ostensibly introduced themselves via self-descriptive blurbs: The Target implicitly resembled the participant’s caregiver, whereas the Control resembled someone else’s caregiver. The co-players threw the ball to participants (re-inclusions) or not (re-exclusions) at equal rates. We assessed event-related cardiac slowing, a correlate of expectancy violation. Cardiac slowing to Target was most pronounced in younger participants and declined with age, suggesting that caregiver cues exert a stronger influence on peer interactions at younger ages. Supporting our pre-registered hypotheses, during re-exclusions, maltreated participants showed greater cardiac slowing to Control vs. Target, whereas nonmaltreated participants exhibited the opposite pattern. We conclude that, for maltreated individuals, re-exclusion violates expectancies less when stemming from caregiver-like targets, implying that resemblance cues trigger selective generalization during early psychophysiological processing.
