In the with-loss condition, mean-level web–lab differences were small, but participant-level correspondence was accompanied by substantial uncertainty and possible scale differences. Comparability varied across task conditions, with weaker correspondence in conditions less suited to discriminating individual differences. An auxiliary N-back task showed no clear evidence of general performance disruption under web-based administration. Overall, browser-based SCT administration on participants’ own smartphones was practically feasible, but mean-level similarity alone was insufficient to establish methodological comparability. Evaluations of web-based repeated-choice tasks should distinguish average performance from preservation of individual differences, systematic bias, scale differences, and implementation robustness.

articles.title: ANDrate toolbox: A Tutorial for collecting behavioral data in naturalistic stimuli in a python based toolbox
articles.authors: Eric Rui Wen Wang; Rui Jing; Haiyan Wu
articles.abstract: Naturalistic stimuli, such as movies, can provide ecological validity and examine human perception and cognition in real-world scenarios. As a result, it is drawing more and more attention from the psychological and cognitive neuroscience community. However, one drawback is that these types of studies usually have no explicit behaviour measures and require raters to provide group-level consensus annotations for the stimuli, such as consensus boundaries and emotional ratings. Here, we introduce ANDrate. ANDrate is an open-source annotation toolbox; it implements boundary segmentation, event annotation, and continuous two-dimensional measurement reporting. The toolbox also supports webcam recording capabilities to ensure data quality. Finally, ANDrate implements algorithms for calculating consensus boundaries and ratings, and enables visualization and the export of standard data formats. It is particularly well-suited for applications in naturalistic experiments, such as research providing continuous emotional states and event cognition for films.

articles.title: Cognitive Modeling of Autobiographical Memory Test: Theoretical Approach to the Mechanisms of Overgeneral Memory
articles.authors: Noboru Matsumoto; Michael David Lee; Keisuke Takano; Tom Joseph Barry; David John Hallford
articles.abstract: Overgeneral autobiographical memory is a well-known transdiagnostic feature of psychopathology, commonly assessed using the Autobiographical Memory Test (AMT), a cued recall method. AMT performance has been scored using heterogeneous outcome measures across studies, including differences in whether specific or general memories are treated as the primary outcome and in how omission responses are handled, raising concerns about replicability. In the present study, we propose a model–based scoring framework for the AMT, termed the accessibility model, which decomposes memory retrieval into three latent parameters: accessibility of specific memories (ρ), accessibility of general memories (γ), and the probability of initiating additional retrieval following goal-incongruent memory retrieval (φ). We firstly conducted simulation studies to examine model behavior and estimation stability, demonstrating that reliable parameter estimation requires approximately 15 trials (cue words) per condition. Next, we tested theoretically derived predictions using three empirical datasets. Results showed that concrete cues yielded higher ρ values, whereas abstract cues yielded higher γ values, consistent with theories of cue-driven accessibility. Instructional manipulations showed that traditional instructions emphasizing specific retrieval goals (AMT-TI), relative to minimal instructions that allowed reporting of any memory (AMT-MI), increased φ. Retrieval instructions also modulated memory accessibility, yielding higher ρ and lower γ in AMT-TI than AMT-MI. Furthermore, depressive symptom severity was associated with higher γ and lower φ for negative cues, indicating heightened accessibility of negative overgeneral memories and reduced likelihood of initiating further retrieval. We highlight several avenues for future refinement of the model and its broader theoretical implications.

articles.title: Development and Validation of the Preparedness to Disclose Non-Suicidal Self-Injury Scale
articles.authors: Kassandra Hon; Penelope Hasking; Stephen P Lewis; Mark Edward Boyes
articles.abstract: The disclosure of nonsuicidal self-injury (NSSI) can be a critical step in recovery by enabling access to social support, clinical intervention, and emotional validation. However, little is known about the psychological and contextual factors that shape individuals’ decisions to disclose NSSI. The present study developed and provided initial validation for the Preparedness to Disclose Nonsuicidal Self-Injury Scale (PDNS), a novel measure designed to assess individuals’ psychological readiness to disclose their experiences of NSSI. Seventy-five preliminary items were administered to 675 university students (77% female; M = 22.20 years, SD = 4.83), 64% of whom had previously disclosed their NSSI. Principal axis factoring revealed a three-factor structure (Self-Efficacy to Disclose, Interpersonal Motives, and Situational Readiness). The five highest loading items on each factor were retained, resulting in a 15-item scale that reliably differentiated individuals who had disclosed from those who had not. Evidence of measurement invariance across individuals who had or had not disclosed their NSSI supported the robustness and generalisability of the PDNS. Correlations with measures of theoretically related constructs provided support for the scale’s convergent and discriminant validity. These findings indicate that preparedness to disclose NSSI is a dynamic, multifaceted process shaped by social-cognitive factors. The PDNS provides a psychometrically sound tool for research and clinical practice, offering a framework to support safe and adaptive disclosure experiences among individuals who self-injure.

articles.title: AI Image Generation Exhibits East Asian Prototype Bias
articles.authors: Ishika Ray; Yuichi Shoda
articles.abstract: AI image generation systems are increasingly used to create visual content, including depictions of people. The present study investigated whether images of “Asian” individuals generated by such systems are representative of the diversity in Asian populations. Two text-to-image [IR1.1]AI models (ChatGPT’s Dall-E, and Gemini’s Imagen) were prompted to generate images of ‘an Asian person,’ yielding a total of eight images. One hundred and ninety-one U.S.-based raters were then asked to identify the likely country of origin for the individual depicted in each image. For any given image, an average of 66.9% raters (95% CI [54.1%, 79.5%]) selected an East Asian country as the most likely origin. This observed proportion is substantially higher than would be expected based on real-world distributions, exceeding both the approximately 38% of Asian Americans who identify as East Asian (e.g., Chinese, Japanese, Korean, Mongolian) and the approximately 34% of the global population residing in countries commonly considered to be in Asia. These findings suggest AI training data may reflect “Asian = East Asian” association, systematically underrepresenting South Asian and Southeast Asian populations. Such biases have important implications for representational equity in AI-generated content.

articles.title: Multidimensional Assessment of Smartphone and Social Media Use: Links with Sleep and Mental Health
articles.authors: Emma Caitlin Sullivan; Umar Toseeb; Lisa Henderson
articles.abstract: There is widespread concern that adolescent mental health is at risk in our “always connected” world. For example, smartphone and social media (S/SM) use around bedtime may displace sleep, putting mental health at risk. However, most research examining these relationships relies on undifferentiated measures of S/SM behaviours, with a need to move beyond oversimplistic models to advance mechanistic understanding. We surveyed 2,333 11-19-year-olds on an array of S/SM behaviours, using exploratory factor analysis to reveal three latent dimensions: (i) S/SM time (i.e., time spent on S/SM), (ii) problematic/negative S/SM use and (iii) SM platform use. Preregistered hypotheses tested whether these dimensions predicted self-reported sleep quality, and whether sleep quality would mediate any associations with depression and anxiety symptoms. Higher S/SM time and problematic/negative S/SM use predicted poorer sleep quality and higher symptoms of anxiety and depression. In contrast, SM platform use was associated with lower anxiety symptoms. As well as direct links between S/SM time and problematic/negative S/SM and depressive and anxiety symptoms, there were also indirect associations via sleep quality. The present study highlights the need for future longitudinal research to incorporate rich and multidimensional measures of S/SM alongside objective sleep assessments to robustly examine the role of sleep as a critical mediator in causal pathways to mental health.

articles.title: Spontaneous Perception of Visuospatial Ensembles
articles.authors: Gabriel Waterhouse; Sami Ryan Yousif
articles.abstract: Look out at a forest. What do you see? You see visual masses of green; you see large numbers of leaves; you see the various sizes of the many different trees. Is one of those features more salient than the others? According to a popular view in cognitive science, number is perceptually prioritized over other visuospatial ensemble properties like density and size, such that when you look out at a forest, what you see first and foremost is the number of trees (or trunks, or leaves) rather than their size or their density, etc. The problem with these arguments is that they mostly hinge on comparisons to only one other dimension: cumulative area. Here, we ask a very simple question: Might there be some other dimension that is prioritized over number? Across five unique paradigms and thirteen experiments with over 2,000 participants, we show that human adults prioritize average size over number, such that they are more likely to (a) categorize, (b) explicitly match, (c) notice changes in, and (d) identify extreme instances of that dimension. We additionally show that children behave the same way. For this reason, we argue against the universal salience of number, noting that these ‘competitive’ approaches (i.e., in which dimensions are compared against each other) are often context dependent.

articles.title: Does perceived chronic stress really impair decision-making capacity? Evidence from decisions made under varying computational demands
articles.authors: Karlo Doroc; Nitin Yadav; Juan Pablo Franco; Carsten Murawski
articles.abstract: Chronic stress is a modern health epidemic, yet whether its impairing effects on basic cognitive capacities generalise to higher-order decision-making remains underexplored. To address this, we conducted a large (N=355), preregistered online experiment measuring perceived chronic stress (PCS), five cognitive capacities, and decision-making capacity (via the knapsack optimisation task). This paradigm provides an objective and risk preference-free measure of decision-making capacity. Importantly, it also allows us to systematically modulate the computational demands of each decision using objective, task-independent features. Surprisingly, in a broadly representative US sample, higher PCS within a normal range was largely unrelated to decision-making capacity or cognitive performance, even on computationally demanding decisions. PCS could not credibly predict decision quality, decision time, click-based exploration of the search space, metacognitive judgements or the types of errors made. Our results challenge a generalised view of chronic stress' negative impact and we discuss potential explanations that may reconcile differential effects observed across the literature.

articles.title: Minimum Cost Psychological Defense: Theoretical Construction and Anti-Fraud Application | 最低成本心理防御理论构建与反诈应用
articles.authors: Lijie Zhou; Yifan Zhou
articles.abstract:
针对传统反诈教育中高认知负荷导致的知行分离困境，本研究提出最低成本心理防御模型，旨在开发并验证不依赖高强度认知加工的自动化防御干预工具。本研究采用关键事件法提取防骗微技能，编制《民众诈骗预防技能量表》，并将其转化为《民众反诈应急指南》，通过双盲并发随机对照实验检验微技能的干预效力。本研究确立了情绪缓冲、主动防御和分布式求助三个维度的量表结构。实验结果示，接受微技能指南干预的个体，其高压情境下的顺从冲动显著降低，作答耗时显著缩短，有效阻断了高拟真欺诈引发的极端情绪唤起。研究表明，最低成本心理防御策略能有效规避急性应激下的执行功能受限。将陈述性防骗知识转化为自动化程序性微技能，是阻断欺诈操纵链条、提升群体防诈效能的可靠路径。
Public anti-fraud efforts face a "knowing-doing gap" as declarative knowledge fails under acute stress. This study proposed the Minimum Cost Psychological Defense (M.C.P.D.) model, which shifts the intervention paradigm from high-cognitive-load rational analysis to automated behavioral blocking. Through a mixed-methods approach , we developed and validated the Public Anti-Fraud Skill Scale, identifying three core dimensions: emotional buffering, proactive defense, and distributed help-seeking. A double-blind, randomized controlled trial demonstrated that participants utilizing the M.C.P.D.-based emergency guide exhibited significantly lower compliance impulses and shorter decision-making response times under high-pressure simulated fraud than the control group. These findings confirm that transforming declarative anti-fraud knowledge into automated procedural micro-skills effectively bypasses stress-induced executive function limitations. This M.C.P.D. model offers a scalable, low-cognitive-load pathway to disrupt manipulative fraud chains and provides a reliable behavioral immune barrier in digital environments.

articles.title: Sonority-driven stress: codas in Brazilian Portuguese
articles.authors: Guilherme D. Garcia; Kevin Ryan
articles.abstract: This study provides experimental evidence for sonority-driven stress in Brazilian Portuguese (BP). We show that speakers systematically prefer final stress when the ultima contains a sonorant coda {N, l, r} rather than the obstruent coda {s}. The effect is specific to the ultima, the position where weight is otherwise most relevant to BP stress. A lexical analysis of monomorphemic words corroborates the experimental pattern: /s/-final words receive final stress less often than sonorant-final words. We further show that this asymmetry cannot be reduced to morphological factors (the plural suffix /-s/ is invisible to stress), nor to a sonority-free account on which final /s/ is a prosodic appendix. These findings bear on a contested question in metrical phonology (whether, and how, segmental sonority conditions stress placement), on which experimental evidence has so far been scarce.

articles.title: Clinicians’ Practices and Attitudes Toward Depression Assessment: A Cross-Sectional Survey Study
articles.authors: NA; Veerle Celine Eijsbroek; Clara Wiebel; Aaron Marker; Katarina Kjell; Marc Molendijk; Elizabeth Cameron Stade; Oscar Nils Erik Kjell
articles.abstract: Accurate psychological evaluation is crucial for early detection, intervention and evaluation of mental health disorders. The validity and reliability of psychological evaluation methods have been systematically researched for many decades. However, assessment methods used by mental health professionals in clinical settings have not consistently been  empirically documented. This study examines the extent to which standardized assessment methods are used in clinical settings, and clinicians’ attitudes toward these practices. Clinicians (N = 585) from the U.S. (25%), the U.K. (28%), the Netherlands (18%), Sweden (22%), and other countries (7%) completed an online survey reporting their depression assessment methods. Clinicians reported using many different data collection methods (e.g., unstructured clinical interviews [83%], rating scales [83%]) in various combinations. A majority (80%) primarily employed clinical data combination methods, as opposed to statistical data combination methods (11%). Although they were confident in their assessments, the time to carry out assessments varied widely (M = 87 [SD = 108] minutes). Seventy-seven percent agreed on the importance of standardized, valid, and reliable assessments, and 43% reported interest in receiving AI decision-support. We did not find many significant cross-cultural differences. A majority of clinicians have not fully adopted standardized assessment methods. Predominant reliance on clinical judgment suggests a disconnect between best practices supported by research and everyday clinical practice.

articles.title: Predicting Memberships from Memory.
articles.authors: Luigi Grisoni
articles.abstract: Neural representations are commonly described as mappings between target variables and patterns of neural activity. A central challenge is to specify not only what the brain represents, but how representational content is organised and constrained across contexts. Here, the concept of Representation of Membership (RoM) is proposed as a perspective on representational organisation. RoM assumes that cognitive representations are shaped by the elements they contain and by the membership structures linking those elements to broader semantic, functional, and contextual domains. The same element may belong to multiple structures and play different roles within them, with particular memberships becoming relevant according to context, task demands, or prior knowledge. Within RoM, prediction reflects the dynamic activation, weighting, and refinement of learned membership structures, constraining which continuations, features, interpretations, or responses become more likely. RoM thus links neural representation, memory, flexibility, and prediction through the context-sensitive organisation and updating of membership structures.

articles.title: Influence of food- vs. activity-based episodic future thinking on snack intake: two pre-registered experimental studies
articles.authors: Jennifer Gatzemeier; Laura L. Wilkinson; Menna Price; Michelle LEE
articles.abstract:
Objective: In our current food environment, psychosocial strategies to reduce intake and related overweight/obesity are needed. Thinking vividly about one’s personal health goal or future event (episodic future thinking; EFT) can reduce intake in the present moment. However, little research has investigated food-related EFT, and if the duration between the current moment and the future event is relevant; which the present study aims to identify. The moderating role of dietary restraint tendencies is assessed. 
Methods: Across two pre-registered experimental studies, participants were randomly allocated to either food-related EFT or control (activity-based EFT). Study 1 focused on food-related EFT for the present day, while study 2 also included food-related EFT for the next day. Intake of six different sweet and savoury snacks, and dietary restraint were assessed. 
Results: For study 1, 124 (Mage=29.7±13.2, MBMI=24.8±4.9) and for study 2, 187 participants (Mage=21.6±5.8, MBMI=24.0±5.1) attended the lab. There was no difference between food intake following either a food- or activity-based EFT task (irrespective of duration between the present moment and the future event). Overall, restraint did not moderate this relationship. 
Conclusion: While health EFT has been shown to reduce intake in the literature, this effect has not been extended to food-related EFT here.

articles.title: Measuring Depression Literacy: Development and Psychometric Validation of the Multifaceted Depression Literacy Scale (M-DL)
articles.authors: Anna-Lena Nadine Jasmin Leimberger; Andreas Czaplicki; Jil Zippelius; Ulrich Hegerl; Hanna Reich
articles.abstract: Depression Literacy is a multifaceted construct encompassing the knowledge needed to recognize and effectively respond to symptoms of depression. This study aimed to develop and validate a self-report questionnaire measuring depression literacy and to evaluate its factorial structure, as well as its criterion, convergent, and discriminant validity. Using a 56-item pool from existing studies, we conducted secondary analyses of two representative German adult surveys (ages 18–69; N = 5,178 and 5,285) to develop the Multifaceted Depression Literacy Scale  (M-DL). Development proceeded in five steps: expert rating of the item pool, exploratory factor analysis and item selection (first survey), calculation of psychometric properties, confirmatory factor analysis, and evaluation of construct and discriminant validity (second survey). To assess validity, scores were compared across subgroups with different depression contact (none, indirect, or personal) and correlated with a self-rated depression knowledge item and the Depression Stigma Scale. Confirmatory factor analysis supported a 14-factor, multidimensional M-DL structure. Criterion validity was shown by significant score differences between professionals or relatives and individuals with no depression contact (both p < .05). Convergent validity with the self-assessment item was low but significant (p < .05), and discriminant validity was supported by negative correlations with Personal and Perceived Stigma (p < .001). We developed a brief self-assessment instrument for depression literacy covering all literature-based facets except prevention, and demonstrated its factorial, criterion, and discriminant validity. The instrument showed good psychometric properties and may help identify individuals or groups with low depression literacy. Future research should test its applicability in more diverse populations and further examine convergent validity as well as sensitivity to change over time, including responsiveness to intervention effects.

articles.title: Maladaptive AI Use for Learning: A Regulatory-Failure Framework, Scale Development, Nomological Network, and Links to Reduced Learning Outcomes
articles.authors: Gabriel Rongyang Lau; Caresse Jia Min Liow; Dragan Gasevic; Andree Hartanto
articles.abstract: Generative AI can support learning, but it may become counterproductive when learners use it to bypass regulatory processes needed for durable understanding. We conceptualise this pattern as maladaptive AI use for learning, a learning-specific regulatory failure within self-regulated learning comprising Uncritical Reliance, Shallow Processing, and Illusory Competence. Across six studies (N = 909), we advanced a regulatory-failure framework, developed and validated a 12-item measure, mapped its nomological network, and examined links with learning outcomes. Study 1 generated and content-validated the initial item pool. Study 2 identified a three-factor structure through EFA. Studies 3 and 4 confirmed this structure, with robust factor loadings and good internal consistency across validation studies (αs = .86–.90). Study 4 supported sex invariance, convergent and discriminant validity. In Study 4, maladaptive AI use for learning was associated with higher AI attachment, social influence surrounding AI adoption, positive attitudes toward AI, academic procrastination, controlled academic motivation, trait anxiety, and maladaptive coping, as well as lower critical thinking in AI use, open-mindedness, and conscientiousness. Study 5 showed good one-week test–retest reliability (ICC(2,1) = .77). Study 6 supported criterion validity, with greater maladaptive AI use for learning predicting lower GPA, poorer independent writing-task performance after AI-supported learning, and greater overconfidence in perceived understanding relative to actual performance. These findings clarify maladaptive AI use for learning as a distinct learning-specific construct and provide a foundation for future research and instructional interventions that help learners use AI to support rather than replace the regulatory processes required for durable learning.

articles.title: Longitudinal event‑sampling dataset of football fans across five countries during the 2024 European championship
articles.authors: Hannes M. Petrowsky; Yannik A. Escher; Lea Boecker; Kathi Diel; NA; Meikel Neumann; Danna Oomen; Leonie Schwarz; Marie-Annik Strauch; Marcel Weber
articles.abstract: This dataset was collected during the UEFA EURO football championship in 2024. It provides match-contingent experience sampling data, including (a) individual-level variables such as life satisfaction, (b) interpersonal variables such as prosocial intent, and (c) broader societal variables such as political trust. The data points are nested in a sample of N = 1,012 individuals from five countries (England, Germany, Italy, the Netherlands, Poland). Using an event-sampling method with four to eight measurement waves (depending on how far the national team progressed in the tournament), we captured time-sensitive fluctuations in a total of m = 30 variables in response to the national team’s objective (i.e., win, draw, loss) and perceived success. In addition, the dataset contains moderating variables including Big-5 personality traits, conspiracy and free-will beliefs, or alcohol consumption and betting behaviors, and detailed match-specific data (e.g., shots on target, yellow cards, attendance). Our data descriptor portrays the methodological approach, data collection procedures, and available variables, and outlines how the data may be used for future research.

articles.title: Large Language Models (LLMs) for Evidence Synthesis: An Exploratory Evaluation and A New Approach for Automated Data Extraction and Validation
articles.authors: Yuchen Zhang; Nanyu Luo; Hajung Kim; Linxin Li; Linfeng Gao; Jiayi Han; Shiting Chen; Xiaoya Zhang; Jinbo He; Feng Ji
articles.abstract: Data extraction and coding are among one of the most labor-intensive and error-prone stages of evidence synthesis (ES), yet they directly affect the validity of meta-analytic conclusions. Automating this step could alleviate these constraints, but must first demonstrate accurate and reliable extraction. This study investigates whether generative large language models (LLMs) can meet this standard by improving the accuracy, robustness, and traceability of automated extraction for ES in the social and behavioral sciences. We propose the Divide, Conquer, then Recheck (DCR) pipeline, which segments articles into section-level units, applies multimodal multi-pass extraction with JSON-constrained prompting, and resolves cross-section discrepancies through evidence-based aggregation. In a preliminary exploration (Study 1), standard chatbot interfaces demonstrated feasible but uneven extraction, with GPT-4o achieving the highest accuracy (0.80). Using the same model, the DCR pipeline (Study 2) raised accuracy to 0.95 and F1 to 0.97, with gains driven by increased precision. Cross-model evaluation (Study 3) of Gemini 2.5 Flash, GPT-5-mini, and Claude Haiku 4.5 confirmed strong performance across models (F1 above 0.93), with Gemini 2.5 Flash performing best (F1 = 0.99) at low temperature settings (temperature = 0). External validation (Study 4) on three independent meta-analysis datasets maintained F1 above 0.96. An user-friendly interactive comparison dashboard enables researchers to audit extraction reasoning and trace values to source sections. Dashboard review also revealed that some apparent errors reflected limitations in the human-coded reference rather than pipeline failures. These findings suggest that the DCR pipeline improves not only extraction accuracy but also transparency and verifiability, supporting more rigorous ES workflows.

articles.title: Assessing chronic pain processes longitudinally: Validation of the German “Assessing Chronic Pain Processes” (ProCEss) item set
articles.authors: Verena Eunike Hofmann; Julia Glombiewski; Marlene F. Prasch; Saskia Scholten
articles.abstract:
Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) has become increasingly feasible with technological advances, capturing dynamic fluctuations in symptoms and processes. EMA is ecologically valid and can reduce recall biases. However, there is a lack of thoroughly validated items. 
Therefore, the “Prozesse Chronischer Schmerzen ErfaSSen” (ProCEss; engl. „Assessing Chronic Pain Processes”) item set was developed for an EMA coverage model, i.e. a broad range of the day shall be captured instead of single momentary experiences. In multiple studies, practitioners (n = 18 + n = 12) and affected persons (n = 12 + n = 12) contributed to establishing content validity through an adapted Discriminant Content Validity Method and Cognitive Interviews. Findings from these studies informed the development of written and audio-visual comprehensibility aids, designed and evaluated in a healthy sample (n = 128) to replace extensive participant instruction procedures.
Across studies, both affected persons and practitioners rated the items positively with regard to comprehensibility and relevance. Feedback of affected persons contributed to item refinement, and the item set was reduced from 29 to 25 items. The evaluation of the comprehensibility aids further supported the comprehensibility of the item set in general.
The ProCEss item set is among the first validated instruments for assessing chronic pain processes using EMA in German. The current study supports its relevance and comprehensibility. Other forms of validity have to be established in future research, and comprehensiveness might be enhanced by future extensions of the item set.

articles.title: Narrative identity themes in Mexican American couples’ COVID-19 stories predict social support and loneliness
articles.authors: Hollen N. Reischer; Hayley Fivecoat; Chrishane N. Cunningham; Lena Blum; Stacey Ho; Erika Lawrence
articles.abstract: A narrative identity approach was used to examine associations between narrative theme predictors and loneliness and perceived spousal social support outcomes. Participants (n = 135; 50% women; 73.3% Mexican American) enrolled in a longitudinal study of marital functioning wrote pandemic-related relationship challenge narratives and completed measures of loneliness and social support. Eight themes (established: affect, agency, communion, meaning-making; novel: challenge resolution, couple stress response, familismo, overriding positive regard) were quantitatively coded and used in analyses. Social support was associated with most themes for women, but few for men. Loneliness was associated with different themes by gender. This study extends the narrative identity literature by including a majority Mexican American sample, novel interpersonal themes, and loneliness and social support as outcomes.

articles.title: What Is a “Blank” Mind? Testing the Meaning and Construct Validity of Mind-Blanking Reports to Thought Probes
articles.authors: Chandni Lal; Matt Welhaf; Michael J. Kane
articles.abstract: Four studies evaluated probed mind-blanking reports, an off-task conscious state ostensibly experienced as an absence of thought and subjective experience. We examined whether people make mind-blanking reports to indicate uncertainty about their preceding thoughts, rather than to communicate an experienced “blank;” we also assessed whether subjects used “blank” to refer to the absence of experience, or rather an unclear, diffuse form of experience. U.S.-based Prolific participants were randomly assigned to one of three conditions that differed in thought-probe response options, which included or did not include “I-don’t-know” and “mind-blanking” options in Study 1a, 1b, and 2 (Ns = 463–790). Within an online go/no-go task, we tested whether different response options yielded different rates of mind-blanking and I-don’t-know reports. That is, would mind-blanking rates be higher when an “I don’t know” response option was not available, suggesting that mind-blanking reports indicate uncertainty? Would rates of I-don’t-know reports be lower when the “mind-blanking” option was unavailable? Neither mind-blanking nor I-don’t-know report rates differed significantly between probe-type conditions. Subjects were also confident and quick in their mind-blanking reports, with confidence ratings and report latencies matching other off-task thought categories. Subjects don’t use mind-blanking reports to reflect uncertainty. Study 3 (N = 415) extended our construct-validity investigation to the phenomenology of mind-blanking by separating “mind-blanking” response options into “unclear or foggy thoughts” versus “complete absence of experience.” Unclear thoughts accounted for 3% of thought reports, versus 1% reflecting no experience, suggesting that “blanking” more often reflects diffuse than absent subjective states.

articles.title: Dissociating mentalizing and automaticity in altercentric interference.
articles.authors: LI CHEN; Hiroyuki Muto
articles.abstract: When people judge what they themselves can see, the judgments are often slower or less accurate if another person in the scene has a different view—a phenomenon known as altercentric interference. Two questions about this effect have remained unresolved for over a decade. First, does it arise from genuine perspective taking or from domain-general spatial processes? Second, is it triggered involuntarily, or does it depend on voluntary task demands? Resolving these questions has been difficult because existing paradigms confound mechanism and automaticity with directional orienting, task-switching demands, and agent presence. We addressed both questions within a single framework using a modified occlusion paradigm in which the avatar's orientation was held constant across consistency conditions. This modification severed the directional-orienting confound that pervades the canonical dot-perspective task. Three preregistered online experiments (each N = 100) then progressively removed factors previously entangled with the interference effect: explicit perspective cueing and its attendant task-switching demands (Experiment 1), trial-level variation in agent presence (Experiment 2), and any within-session agent presence whatsoever (Experiment 3). In every condition tested, participants were slower to verify their own view when the scene's spatial structure implied a discrepant perspective—including a condition in which no avatar appeared on any trial and no directional cue was present. Together, these findings place altercentric interference squarely in the involuntary-submentalizing cell of the framework, while require that this cell be broadened beyond directional orienting to encompass stimulus salience and spatial-geometric structure. What has been interpreted as a signature of spontaneous VPT is better understood as a domain-general response to structured visual scenes.

articles.title: Calibration of retrospective and prospective life satisfaction judgments around the world
articles.authors: David R. Mandel; Ethan Andrew Meyers; Phil Omorogbe
articles.abstract: It is a physical fact that humans have access to records of the past but not of the future and that this creates an epistemic asymmetry in time. We explore the epistemic asymmetry, hypothesizing that retrospection about past psychological states is less biased than prospection about future states. Leveraging data from the Pew Research Center’s Global Attitudes Project, involving 61,271 participants across 34 countries, we examined the calibration and bias of individuals' assessments of past and future life satisfaction relative to present assessments. A multi-method analysis contrasted individual and national-level evaluations of life satisfaction across two waves, five years apart, with numerous control variables to account for potential confounding factors. Results confirmed miscalibration in prospective judgments of future life satisfaction, whereas retrospective judgments of past life satisfaction showed no meaningful miscalibration. This supports a psychological effect of the physical arrow of time on human judgment, robust across diverse samples worldwide.

articles.title: Cosmetocracy: Beauty, Social Media, and the Psychosocial Governance of Appearance
articles.authors: Alberto Stefana
articles.abstract:
Beauty has long shaped social perception, desirability, status, and opportunity. However, contemporary digital and commercial environments have intensified the social power of appearance by making bodies, faces, and skin more visible, comparable, modifiable, and measurable. This article proposes the concept of cosmetocracy to describe a sociocultural order in which personal, relational, and social value is increasingly mediated by aesthetic appearance, the capacity to modify appearance, and the public visibility of such modification. The proposed framework integrates research on physical attractiveness, body image, social comparison, objectification, social media affordances, influencer marketing, dating apps, aesthetic labour, cosmetic procedures, body dysmorphic disorder, cosmeticorexia, and prevention.
The Cosmetocracy Model identifies several interacting domains: sociocultural beauty ideals, digital and commercial infrastructures, internalization and appearance comparison, embodied self-experience, appearance-management practices, relational feedback, clinical and psychodermatological outcomes, and prevention. The model suggests that cosmetocratic influence operates through recursive pathways in which appearance ideals are transmitted and amplified, internalized as self-evaluative standards, enacted through self-surveillance and cosmetic labour, reinforced by social and digital feedback, and, in some individuals, escalated into clinically significant distress or impairment. These processes are shaped by moderators such as age, gender, race, skin tone, body size, socioeconomic position, sexuality, disability, cultural context, and platform use.
Special attention is given to adolescence, social media, pediatric skincare, dating apps, body dysmorphic disorder, and cosmeticorexia. The article argues that appearance-related distress should not be reduced to individual vulnerability or vanity. Rather, it emerges within a broader ecology of visibility, comparison, consumption, and social reward. At the same time, the framework avoids pathologizing beauty practices per se, recognizing that appearance management may also express identity, pleasure, creativity, culture, and agency.
The article concludes by outlining implications for prevention, clinical care, digital policy, and future research. Interventions should aim not to eliminate beauty from human life, but to weaken the link between appearance, self-worth, and social value. This requires promoting positive body image, embodiment, media and social media literacy, clinical screening, interdisciplinary psychodermatological care, and structural changes in platform design and beauty marketing. Cosmetocracy offers a conceptual vocabulary for understanding how beauty becomes a form of psychosocial governance in contemporary life.

articles.title: Long-term memory contributions to working memory: a contralateral delay activity study.
articles.authors: Ana Rodríguez; Peter Shepherdson; Lea Bartsch
articles.abstract:
How does long-term memory (LTM) support working memory (WM) performance? Proposed mechanisms include boosting, where LTM enhances WM representations through additional activation, but does not reduce WM load; chunking, where WM holds references to LTM, reducing WM load; and offloading, where LTM substitutes active maintenance in WM. To distinguish between these mechanisms, we measured the contralateral delay activity (CDA) during an alternative forced-choice WM task, in which some trials (varying in memory array set size) included a pre-learnt color-object binding. The CDA is a well-established neural correlate that indexes the amount of information actively stored in WM, allowing us to test between the three accounts by examining whether including pre-learnt bindings in a memory set of a given size reduced its amplitude compared to maintaining entirely new bindings.
Overall, our findings supported the boosting account. Pre-learnt bindings improved performance for matching items in WM, but the benefit did not extend to newly encoded items, indicating no reduction in WM load. CDA amplitude increased with set size irrespective of whether the items were pre-learnt or new, reflecting active maintenance of all the to-be-remembered items. Overall, these results show that pre-learnt associations in LTM can boost WM performance without substituting for WM storage, providing evidence against offloading or chunking accounts in this context.

articles.title: Pupil-linked arousal: more than noradrenaline
articles.authors: Simon van Gaal; Lola Beerendonk; Tobias H. Donner; Jan Willem de Gee
articles.abstract: The literature on pupil-linked arousal shows a pervasive bias: it often attributes findings to the noradrenergic locus coeruleus while overlooking other neuromodulatory systems. Here, we quantify the extent of this bias and emphasize that correcting it is essential for refining theoretical models and improving interpretability in this rapidly evolving field.

articles.title: More Imagery, Less Vividness? Maladaptive Daydreaming Dissociates Imagery Use from Imagery Vividness
articles.authors: Mohammad Atashrooz; Fatemeh Mirzai; Maede Amin Roaya; Hannaneh Fayyaz Rouhi; Arash Ghadir; Hoda Doosalivand; Reza Khosrowabadi; Amir Sam Kianimoghadam
articles.abstract: Maladaptive daydreaming (MD) is characterized by excessive immersive imagination. Despite assumptions that MD is linked to heightened vividness of mental imagery, empirical evidence is lacking. This study examines whether individuals with MD differ from non-MD participants in visual imagery vividness and spontaneous imagery usage. In a study with 786 participants, visual imagery vividness was assessed using the Persian VVIQ-Pr2, while spontaneous imagery use was evaluated with the SUIS. MD was analyzed as group status and continuous severity. Welch’s t-tests and multiple regression were employed, adjusting for age and gender. Participants with MD exhibited lower visual imagery vividness than normal individuals, with a small effect. Despite modest differences in imagery use, MD severity negatively impacted vividness. The final regression model accounted for ~26% of vividness variance, showing SUIS as a strong positive predictor. Maladaptive daydreaming contradicts the belief of high vividness in visual imagery, showing instead a low vividness despite higher imagery use. This suggests that MD may relate to dysfunctions in imagery use or control, impacting theoretical models of imagery and reality monitoring.

articles.title: Developing an open multi-domain assessment battery using laboratory tasks for children in low- and middle-income countries
articles.authors: Joseph Coffey; Elizabeth Spelke
articles.abstract: Recent years have seen growing interest in field research using randomized designs to evaluate the impacts of interventions on early child development in low- and middle-income countries. Measuring these impacts requires assessments that can track development prior to school entry in settings that are often noisy and outdoors. Drawing from research in developmental cognitive science, we created an open multi-domain assessment battery for testing children in low- and middle-income countries consisting of tasks based on experimental paradigms used in laboratory settings. These tasks were designed to target abilities that emerge across cultures early in life and predict later performance on related skills in adulthood. This battery was administered to 1435 Ghanaian children whose parent previously took part in a randomized controlled field experiment on the impacts of parent secondary education on their children, measuring performance on tasks at ages 1.5, 3.5, 5.5, and 7.5 years. Starting at age 3.5, children’s responses to the tasks were internally consistent and above chance, with overall test results predictive of later performance. There was strong evidence of validity both for tasks in numeracy and spatial reasoning, which were designed to tap into culturally invariant cognitive capacities, as well as those in language and executive functioning, which required more context-specific adaptation. In contrast, there was little evidence of validity in infant tasks or tasks focused on intuitive physics or mental state reasoning. Nevertheless, our results suggest that our approach to assessment design is effective at producing tasks that are internally consistent and sensitive to individual differences within cohort. By making our testing materials and data freely accessible, we hope to provide a foundation for the development of tools for studying cognitive development in field settings.

articles.title: AI Mindset – An Empirically Tested Theoretical Framework on the Psychological Factors Shaping AI Competence and AI Use
articles.authors: Shuyan Feng; Martin J. Koch; Astrid Carolus
articles.abstract:
Artificial intelligence (AI) has emerged as a powerful tool in the educational sector. Research
examining the factors that facilitate its practical application by teachers has increased
substantially. Although empirical studies highlight the importance of psychological factors in
shaping engagement with AI technologies, the systematic integration of cognitive, emotional,
and motivational variables into AI use research remains fragmented. To advance the
psychological foundation, the present study proposed and empirically tested a model for AI
Mindset, conceptualised based on prior psychological and social-cognitive theories. The AI
Mindset framework posits a triadic structure, comprising dispositional factors, contextual and
situational characteristics, and technology-related appraisals, to explain teachers’ use of AI.
Structural equation modelling of 224 teachers revealed that AI Mindset variables significantly
predict pedagogical AI competence and AI use. Specifically, dispositional factors of openness
and need for relatedness associated with AI use indirectly through pedagogical AI
competence. Contextual and social facilitations of habit demonstrated the strongest direct
effect on AI use and indirect effect via competence. In terms of technology-related appraisals,
fear showed a complex pattern, with a positive direct effect on AI use but a negative indirect
effect through AI competence. The effect of effort expectancy was fully mediated by
competence, whereas hedonic motivation exerted a direct positive effect on AI use. In sum, by
introducing and empirically testing the AI Mindset framework, the present study integrates a
psychological perspective into the AI use research, provides a foundation for understanding
AI-related use patterns, and informs professional development and AI technology design.

articles.title: Myths About Neurodiversity: A Community Perspective
articles.authors: Emily Friedel; Elif Bastan; Biljana Gjoneska; Caitlin Nicole Hughes; Adrien Mathy; Nathaniel D Porter; Christopher James Graham; Anna Hollis; Sara Lil Middleton; Robert M Ross
articles.abstract: Myths and misinformation about neurodivergent people are widespread, persistent, and potentially harmful, affecting access to diagnosis, accommodations, and support. In this community perspective, we identify and address a carefully considered selection of harmful myths about neurodivergence, drawn from an international collaboration of researchers, practitioners, and community members with lived experience. We collected 56 myths via open submission, then reviewed and organized key myths in four themes: 1) myths about causation, which commonly frame neurodivergence as preventable and assign blame to individual behaviors, most often of parents/caregivers; 2) myths about legitimacy, which question who counts as genuinely neurodivergent and deserves support; 3) myths about ability, which cast neurodivergent people as either fundamentally impaired or exceptionally gifted; and 4) myths about identity and diversity, which prescribe who can be neurodivergent based on demographic characteristics and often erase diversity within neurodivergence. Across these themes, a common thread emerges: false beliefs about neurodivergent people can affect whose experiences are validated and who receives support. We argue that effective responses require not only debunking but also prebunking strategies, media literacy and education, and the active co-production of research with neurodivergent communities.

articles.title: Interbrain synchrony analysis for fNIRS –  a signal processing primer
articles.authors: Katarina Lodrant; Dimitri Van De Ville; Frank Scharnowski; Kathrin Kostorz
articles.abstract: Interbrain synchrony (IBS), also referred to as interpersonal neural synchrony (INS), has become a central construct in social neuroscience and beyond. It describes the temporal alignment of neural activity across individuals and has been studied in a range of paradigms, including sequential interactions, shared-stimulus settings, and hyperscanning, i.e., the simultaneous neuroimaging of two or more individuals during real-time interaction. Functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS) is increasingly used for such studies because it supports portable, naturalistic, face-to-face paradigms. A central goal in these experiments is to quantify IBS between interacting individuals. Wavelet transform coherence (WTC) has become the most widely used method for estimating IBS in fNIRS studies. However, its application involves several non-trivial signal-processing assumptions and time–frequency trade-offs that are often not explicitly addressed, making it difficult to apply and interpret the method consistently. Reliable estimation of IBS is crucial not only for experimental design but also for the interpretation and comparison of results within and across studies. Here, we aim to support this endeavor by providing a principled overview of the relevant signal-processing foundations. To enable researchers to make informed methodological choices, the paper first introduces key concepts in time–frequency analysis and clarifies their relation to WTC. These principles are then connected to the analysis of IBS in fNIRS datasets, with particular emphasis on the time–frequency constraints inherent to wavelet-based methods. Building on these foundations, we provide practical recommendations for experiment design, preprocessing, and frequency targeting. By making resolution limits and analysis assumptions explicit, this primer aims to support improved study planning and more consistent interpretation of IBS in the rapidly growing field of fNIRS-based investigations and beyond. Establishing a shared methodological foundation for IBS analysis is essential for advancing toward more rigorous, transparent, and reproducible measurement of interpersonal brain dynamics in naturalistic neuroscience.

articles.title: Action Simulation as an Intervention to Improve Balance, Mobility and Gait in Healthy Older Adults: A Scoping Review
articles.authors: Neza Grilc; Adam Cocks; Elmar Kal; Toby J Ellmers; Ashika Chembila Valappil; Adam Mark Bruton
articles.abstract: Falls in older adults are a significant public health concern. Action simulation interventions involving action observation and/or motor imagery have been proposed as alternative or adjunct strategies to physical exercise for reducing fall risk. These approaches may minimize barriers to physical practice and may specifically target deficits in motor planning that are linked to falls. The purpose of this scoping review is to examine how action simulation interventions have been applied and evaluated in healthy older adults, and assess their effectiveness in improving balance, mobility, and gait. A systematic search of six databases and repositories was conducted. Studies were included if they used action simulation interventions in healthy, community-dwelling adults aged 60+ years and examined movement outcomes related to balance, gait or functional mobility. Nineteen studies with 587 older adults were included. Motor imagery was the most common action simulation intervention (n = 11 [58%]), and most studies were randomized controlled trials (n = 14 [74%]). Only one study examined long-term effects on balance and gait performance (i.e., 1-month post-intervention). The examined outcomes varied widely, including static and dynamic balance, gait, and mobility. Most interventions (n = 17 [71%]) reported significant improvement in at least one domain, but findings were inconsistent across studies, and none assessed intervention implementability. Overall, this review highlights some promising effects of action simulation on balance, mobility and gait in older adults. However, if action simulation techniques are to be adopted as a fall prevention strategy, more studies examining acceptability are needed.

articles.title: Grammatical generalizations in statistical learning are shaped by the learner’s vocabulary knowledge, but not by the trained vocabulary size.
articles.authors: Amanda Jayne Hickey; Marianna E. Hayiou-Thomas; Jelena Mirković
articles.abstract: The learning and generalization of grammatical regularities is fundamental to successful language acquisition and use. Statistical learning studies have considered how this process occurs through implicit learning primarily of distributional and phonological regularities in the input. The current study focuses on how adults and children generalize grammatical regularities by exploring the role of vocabulary in this process, conceptualised in two complementary ways: as the size of the vocabulary in the input, and the size of the vocabulary acquired by the learner. Across two experiments, adults and children learnt an artificial language comprising phonological, distributional, and semantic regularities, modelling grammatical gender systems found in natural language. In Experiment 1, we found no evidence that an increase in vocabulary size in the input improves generalization across any of the regularities. In Experiment 2, we reduced adults’ vocabulary knowledge to that of children in Experiment 1: this resulted in reduced generalization of the grammatical regularities relative to adults in Experiment 1, but similar to that of children. We additionally assessed emergent explicit knowledge of the regularities in both groups across experiments. We found consistent contributions of explicit knowledge to generalizations in adults but not in children.  The findings are discussed in the context of lexicalist theories of language development and multi-componential views of statistical learning.

articles.title: Emergence and Aftermath of Proactive Transnational Politics in Turkey: A Case Study on Ethnoreligious Communities in Germany
articles.authors: Aydın Bayad; Elif Sandal Önal; N. Ekrem Düzen
articles.abstract: In recent years, the social integration of migrants in Europe faced challenges due to the rise of populism. Migrants are often seen as living in ‘parallel societies’ supposedly due to ‘failed’ integration. Turkish communities in Europe are torn between cultural assimilation in their host countries and the Turkish nationalism promoted by Turkey. This dilemma is heightened by Turkey's diaspora policies, which aim to use Turkish communities abroad as political tools, reinforcing a sense of Turkish identity through an idealized portrayal of the Ottoman past. This study investigates the impact of these policies on the identity expressions of Turkish migrants, particularly within ethnoreligious communities. Through interviews and ethnographic observations in mid-sized city in  Germany, we found that Turkish migrants express diverse identities shaped by their social and political resources in Germany. While some groups align more with an inclusive Muslim identity, other communities aggregated around state-led organisations are more exposed to Turkey's diaspora policies tend to merge Islam and Turkishness, often emphasizing a national pride tied closely to Turkey and its political ambitions. Despite these variations, all participants maintained strong symbolic or real connections to Turkey. This research provides new insights into how Turkey’s diaspora policies affect identity expressions among Turkish postmigrants.

articles.title: Structural priming as a model for testing language change in bilingualism‎
articles.authors: Giuseppe Dario Benigno; Pedro Guijarro Fuentes; Estela García Alcáraz; Marta Rivera
articles.abstract: This study examined the interplay between cross-linguistic influence, structural priming, and language variation. Specifically, it investigated whether Spanish–Majorcan Catalan bilinguals process expressed and unexpressed object constructions similarly, and whether they show evidence of cross-linguistic influence and synchronic change arising from sustained contact between the two languages. While previous research has examined null-object representations in monolinguals, much less is known about how bilinguals of languages that (dis)allow object omission, process such constructions. To address this gap, forty-three Spanish–Majorcan Catalan bilinguals with varying dominance profiles completed a comprehension-based structural priming task in Spanish. The task included both grammatical and ungrammatical Spanish sentences, the latter incorporating constructions modeled on the prescriptive Catalan partitive clitic en to introduce Catalan-influenced syntax into Spanish. Results revealed differential priming effects for expressed vs. unexpressed objects. Priming effects with ungrammatical constructions further supported an implicit learning account of structural priming. Finally, although not mediated by priming, cross-linguistic influence from Catalan to Spanish indicated ongoing synchronic change in the bilingual grammar of this population. This study underscores the role of individual linguistic experience in shaping bilingual syntactic processing and highlights the need for more experience-sensitive approaches to understanding how contact-induced grammatical convergence develops in bilingual speakers.

articles.title: The Art of Making Problems Simple: A Theory of Intelligence
articles.authors: Angela J. Yu
articles.abstract: Human intelligence is remarkably flexible, capable of solving complex , novel problems and generalizing across domains with little training or data. We propose that intelligence depends on the ability to construct and revise representational normal forms: internal coordinate systems in which prediction, comparison, and inference become simple. This capacity rests on four components: (1) discovering lawful generators of variations in the world, (2) quotienting task-irrelevant distinctions, (3) constructing and selecting measurable structure for comparison, evaluation, and control, and (4) reorganizing representations so that task-relevant computations become simple and low-cost. The framework explains how these components arise during human development, from early object perception to abstract reasoning; why broad measures of fluid intelligence such as Raven’s matrices, analogical reasoning, and mental rotation generalize across domains; and how systematic individual cognitive differences can arise from trade-offs between representational precision and flexibility. Together, these results identify structural constraints on intelligence not captured by existing cognitive models or artificial intelligence systems.

articles.title: Active Engagement with Graphs Improves the Long-Term Efficacy of Fact-Checks but Not Belief Regression
articles.authors: Emily Rebecca Spearing; Briony Swire-Thompson; Eryn Newman; Ullrich K. H. Ecker
articles.abstract: Fact-checks can reduce—but scarcely eliminate—misinformation impacts, and their effects are often short-lived. Across two experiments (total N = 471), we examined whether graphs could bolster the effectiveness and longevity of fact-checks, through increasing their memorability. Participants rated their belief in accurate and misleading headlines, then received corresponding fact-checks, some of which included a graph. Participants then rated their belief in each headline again as well as their memory of each fact-check, both immediately and after a 1-week (Experiment 1) or 4-week (Experiment 2) delay. In Experiment 2, graphs were also sometimes accompanied by a multiple-choice question to encourage deeper engagement. Graphs alone improved memory for fact-checks but did not consistently improve discernment between accurate and misleading headlines; graphs presented with a multiple-choice question, however, improved discernment immediately and after a 4-week delay. Findings demonstrate the potential of interventions combining visual content with active engagement for combatting misinformation impacts.

articles.title: Treatment access and barriers among university students experiencing mental health disorder and suicidal thoughts and behaviours in Aotearoa New Zealand
articles.authors: Andre Mason; Alannah Corson Keogh; Damian Scarf; Taylor Winter; Mele Taumoepeau; Hitaua Arahanga-Doyle; Amanda E. Clifford; Sarah Fortune; Jesse Kokaua; Charlene Rapsey
articles.abstract:
Background: Despite the high prevalence of mental disorders and suicidal thoughts and behaviours in university students, relatively few students receive mental health treatment. We aimed to first, document inequities in treatment access to understand systemic barriers and second, describe self-reported barriers to formal help-seeking and treatment access. 
Methods: As part of the World Mental Health International College Surveys (WMH-ICS) initiative, 3,702 first year students completed online self-report surveys (mean response rate: 24.6%). The survey assessed: presence of common DSM-5 mental disorders, suicidal thoughts and behaviours; whether respondents had received treatment for their distress in the past 12 months; and perceived barriers to accessing treatment. Weighted prevalence estimates were calculated and data were analysed using multivariable statistical methods. 
Results: Among students who met DSM-5 criteria for a mental disorder, only 30.9% had received treatment. Among all students, those who were older, transgender, or worked part-time were more likely to have received treatment. Asian students were less likely to receive treatment than their European peers and men were less likely than woman. When considering self-reported barriers to seeking help, older students were more likely to report all barrier types and males were more likely to endorse a desire to talk to friends/family, handle the problem alone, or report uncertainty of treatment effectiveness. 
Conclusion: There is a large unmet treatment need for psychological distress among university students in Aotearoa New Zealand. Student support services need to consider targeted initiatives that challenge the perceived barriers to help-seeking and address inequities in treatment delivery.

articles.title: Trusting the Machine: How Anthropomorphism Impacts Epistemic Trust in Generative AI
articles.authors: Caroline Simpson; Clara Colombatto; Jonathan Albert Fugelsang
articles.abstract: As Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) rapidly becomes more sophisticated and more widespread, understanding the impact of its perceived humanness on trust is increasingly important. Previous work has shown that anthropomorphism increases the perceived trustworthiness of an agent as an advisor, but here we asked whether it might also impact the trustworthiness of informational communications it produces in a non-advisory role. In two experiments, participants read a science communication blog post attributed to a GenAI with varying levels of anthropomorphic cues. They were asked to report the trustworthiness of both the agent and the content it produced. In Experiment 1 (N = 270), anthropomorphic cues in a description of the author directly increased author trust but not content trust. However, anthropomorphism appears positively correlated with both author and content trust due to the positive correlation between author and content trust. Experiment 2 (N = 144) replicated these findings using a more immersive design: participants chatted with a GenAI chatbot presented as the author of the blog. Partial correlations revealed that the effect of anthropomorphism on author trust explains much of the correlation between anthropomorphism and content trust in Experiment 1 but less so in Experiment 2. These effects reveal a dissociation between the perceived trustworthiness of the author and the content, suggesting that anthropomorphic cues may directly impact the former, but not the latter. Additionally, the salience of the GenAI author may impact the cognitive mechanisms involved in these trustworthiness judgments.

articles.title: Predicting visual working memory errors with a priori measures of representational geometry from human and artificial systems
articles.authors: Timothy F. Brady; Maria Martinovna Robinson
articles.abstract: A longstanding question in cognitive science is whether the structure of mental representations — deriving from perceptual and semantic knowledge — can be used to generate a priori quantitative predictions of the specific errors people make in memory. Using both human similarity judgments and an ensemble of modern pretrained deep neural networks trained for general visual tasks, with no memory-task or face-identification training, we measure the representational geometry of face stimuli and simple visual features (e.g., color, shape, orientation, location) independently of any memory experiment. Combined with a simple single-parameter decision rule, these independently measured geometries predict the full distribution of visual working memory errors for those stimuli, including which specific items people are most likely to confuse, without any fitting to the memory data. Human and deep network-derived geometries agree closely, and together they outperform standard mixture model and tuning-function models of memory while using fewer free parameters. The same pipeline, with a fixed modeling procedure, transferred unchanged to thirty new face sets as well as novel tasks and simple stimuli like orientation, shape, color and location, in each case predicting the specific pattern of errors specifically from that set’s unique network-derived geometry. Because the same geometric measurement underlies predictions across different aspects of behavior, the results suggest that representational geometry, measured with modern tools, provides a direct and falsifiable a priori link between internal representations and the errors people make in memory.

articles.title: Distinct forms of uncertainty guide information seeking and subjective confidence
articles.authors: Georgina Edwards-Lowe; Elisa La Chiusa; Helen Olawole-Scott; Daniel Yon
articles.abstract: Humans and other animals seek information to improve their cognition and behaviour. Theories in cognitive neuroscience, developmental psychology and animal cognition widely assume a strong connection between information seeking behaviour and subjective feelings of confidence. However, it remains unclear whether information seeking and subjective confidence are truly driven by the same underlying mechanisms. Here, across a series of experiments in adults, we show that information seeking and subjective confidence can be dissociated and be influenced differently by distinct forms of uncertainty. In particular, we find that information seeking is strongly driven by uncertainty in sampled sensory evidence (sensory uncertainty) while confidence is strongly driven by uncertainty caused by decision boundaries (decisional uncertainty). This ability to dissociate the two suggests that the computations in the mind and brain that generate confidence and information seeking are not identical, as separate kinds of uncertainty can modulate these behaviours. This dissociation has implications for research using information seeking as a window into subjective confidence and self-awareness in nonverbal animals and preverbal infants.

articles.title: Experimental Phenomenological Analysis: A Step-by-Step Guide for Integrating Phenomenological Data into Neurophenomenology through a CAQDAS–R Workflow
articles.authors: Alejandro Troncoso; Antonia Zepeda; David Martínez-Pernía
articles.abstract: Neurophenomenology has progressively consolidated as a research program aimed at integrating first-person lived experience with third-person neurobiological and behavioral measures. Nevertheless, despite its growing empirical development, neurophenomenology continues to face what Varela described as a “pragmatic and methodological limbo”. Subjective experience is recognized as scientifically indispensable, yet the field still lacks sufficiently integrated, reproducible, and operationally coherent workflows for the empirical investigation of lived experience. In response to these limitations, the present article introduces Experimental Phenomenological Analysis (EPA), a structured and reproducible analytical workflow designed to support the systematic analysis of phenomenological data within neurophenomenology through the integration of Computer-Assisted Qualitative Data Analysis Software (CAQDAS) and the R statistical environment. EPA is organized into two interconnected analytic cycles: (i) foundational phenomenological construction and intersubjective triangulation, and (ii) corpus-wide consolidation and computational integration. Across these cycles, the framework progressively articulates lived experience through unified analytic units integrating diachronic and synchronic units, together with the progressive articulation of diachronic phases, diachronic dynamics, higher-order synchronic categories, and experiential structures grounded in participant descriptions. The workflow further incorporates procedures for intersubjective stabilization, intersubjective agreement analysis, computational visualization, and procedures supporting integration with quantitative and neurobiological measures. To illustrate its application, the article presents the complete EPA workflow using real phenomenological data derived from an experimental empathy-for-pain paradigm involving face-to-face interaction with a simulated Alzheimer’s patient. Importantly, EPA is not limited to qualitative phenomenological description; the resulting hierarchically organized and computationally structured corpus enables subsequent qualitative, quantitative, mixed-methods, and neurophenomenological analyses, including integration with psychometric, behavioral, physiological, and neurobiological measures. More broadly, EPA contributes to ongoing efforts to establish phenomenology as a transparent, reproducible, and multimodally integrated scientific practice within contemporary neurophenomenology.

articles.title: Debugging Online Research: A Guide for Detecting Fraudulent Respondents
articles.authors: Ian Matthew Raugh; Megan Davies; Simone D'Amico; Vincent Paquin; John Torous; Manuela Ferrari
articles.abstract: Online research is increasingly popular to reach under-represented populations and expand research samples; however, it is also vulnerable to fraud in the form of “bots” (automated respondents, e.g., Large Language Models) and humans, such as completing a survey multiple times or when they are not eligible. Often, “infiltration” by such fraudulent responses is only noted and addressed after data collection has started. Here we present a classification process consisting of automated and manual detection techniques (“flags”) with practical suggestions for customization and implementation in other research contexts, grounded in the literature and our own experiences in a large online survey of video gaming behaviours. Using this protocol, we screened out 7638 likely fraudulent responses out of 8474 (90.13%). Among the flags implemented, those related to participant address, overall survey speed, and pattern of responses in “batches” provided the best performance. Ultimately, we recommend using multiple routes and layers to detect possibly fraudulent responses rather than reliance on any one or two indicators. By proactively considering how to detect and respond to fraud, researchers can have greater confidence in the integrity of their data, preserve limited resources, and minimize burden from post hoc fraud identification.

articles.title: Autoregressive Marginalized Zero-inflated Poisson Model for Intensive Longitudinal Data
articles.authors: Chendong Li
articles.abstract: Intensive longitudinal studies frequently collect count outcomes that are zero-inflated. Traditional zero-inflated models can account for excess zeros, but their count-process parameters are conditional on a latent count-generating process and therefore may not directly answer research questions about the overall effect. This study proposes an autoregressive marginalized zero-inflated Poisson (AR-mZIP) model for intensive longitudinal count data. The proposed AR-mZIP model directly parameterizes the marginal mean of the observed count outcome, while retaining a separate excess-zero component. This allows researchers to estimate predictor effects on the overall expected count, which is often the primary substantive question in applied ILD research. To address the dynamic nature of ILD, the model further incorporates observed-history autoregression by including a lagged outcome function. Thus, the AR-mZIP model simultaneously addresses excess zeros, within-person temporal dependence, and the need for marginal mean interpretations of overall effects. A Monte Carlo simulation study evaluated the performance of the model with results showing small bias, decreasing RMSE with larger sample size and longer series length, and coverage close to the nominal level for both fixed effects and random-effect parameters. The model was also demonstrated using ecological momentary assessment data on daily alcohol use. In the empirical example, higher-than-usual self-control demands were associated with lower overall expected drinking and higher odds of the excess-zero process. The proposed AR-mZIP model provides an interpretable approach for zero-inflated intensive longitudinal count outcomes when the substantive target is the overall expected observed count, rather than a latent count-process-specific effect.

articles.title: Adaptive Regularization via Extreme Value Distributions for Gaussian Graphical Models
articles.authors: Alexander P. Christensen; Jeongwon Choi
articles.abstract: Edge selection in Gaussian graphical models is fundamentally a variable selection problem where pairwise relationships determine construct validity and variable importance in psychological networks. Network estimation relies predominantly on L1 regularization, whose uniform shrinkage systematically underestimates edge and centrality parameters. Alternative penalties overcome this bias but rely on fixed hyperparameters that do not adapt to the signal in the data. We develop a family of data-adaptive regularization penalties grounded in extreme value theory. Across 290 empirical psychological datasets, we show that absolute partial correlations are well-described by the Weibull distribution. Using this empirical regularity, we derive Weibull, Gumbel, and Exponential penalties that approximate L0 penalization and calibrate to each dataset's noise floor rather than assuming a fixed signal level. A large-scale simulation spanning two network topologies and various sample sizes (N = 100-10,000) demonstrates that the adaptive penalties achieve high specificity at small sample sizes while continuing to accumulate sensitivity as sample size grows with low parameter bias and high rank-order centrality congruence relative to field standards. Empirically, method choice alone determined centrality rankings at sample sizes typical in psychology. The Weibull penalty is recommended as a principled default given the interpretability of its parameters.

articles.title: Cognitive reappraisal changes cognitive evaluations more than affective experiences
articles.authors: Henna Vartiainen; Erik C Nook
articles.abstract: Cognitive reappraisal is an emotion regulation strategy that involves changing one’s interpretation of a situation to change its emotional impact. Cognitive reappraisal studies typically assess changes in valence ratings following reappraisal as the primary dependent variable, but scholars have recently documented that valence ratings can take two forms: affective valence (i.e., the positivity/negativity of one’s hedonic internal feelings) and semantic valence (i.e., the positivity/negativity of one’s cognitive evaluation of the stimulus). Because typical rating instructions do not distinguish between these forms of valence, it remains unknown how strongly cognitive reappraisal shifts either one. We address this gap through two preregistered studies. In Study 1, N=155 participants completed a classic cognitive reappraisal task in which they either responded naturally to or cognitively reappraised negative images. Critically, we manipulated whether participants rated either affective valence, semantic valence, or “default” valence (conventional instructions). Reappraisal significantly improved valence ratings in all conditions, but reappraisal most strongly influenced semantic valence and least strongly affective valence. Interestingly, default valence behaved most similarly to semantic valence, suggesting that existing reappraisal studies largely document changes in semantic evaluations, more than affective experiences. Study 2 (N=105) replicated these effects within-subjects. These results extend research on affective and semantic valence by demonstrating that they vary in their responsiveness to regulation. Additionally, they suggest that existing research likely overestimates the impact of reappraisal on felt emotional experiences, prompting new perspectives on past findings and more focused methods for assessing the hedonic impact of emotion regulation.

articles.title: Who Looks Nice: A Novel Methodology to Visualize Children's Mental Representations
articles.authors: Uliana Solovieva; Daniel N Albohn; Alexander Todorov; Lin Bian
articles.abstract: Humans rapidly form prosocial evaluations from physical appearance, and these impressions profoundly shape social interactions. Yet, little is known about the developmental origins of mental representations of “nice” individuals and if these representations reflect visual stereotypes, or appearance cues that shape first impressions. Computational models of young children’s mental representations are essential for addressing these questions, but existing methods are largely limited to older children and adults because they require many trials to generate stable models. In the present research, we adapted a data-driven Generative Reverse Correlation (GRC) method and introduced a child-friendly version capable of visualizing children’s individual- and group-level mental representations in just 40 trials, with children as young as three. In Study 1, children 3-7 (N = 160) categorized 40 synthetic faces as nice or mean to generate mental models. Ratings revealed that the majority of children—across gender and age groups—associate "niceness" with women and individuals high in perceived trustworthiness, happiness, and femininity, highlighting the early emergence of appearance-based stereotyping. In Study 2, children 5-9 (N = 165) categorized faces as nice or smart to compare non-oppositional traits. Findings replicated associations from Study 1, demonstrating that visual stereotypes of niceness emerge across different comparison traits.
