articles.abstract: As Taiwan is one of the hotspots of Indo-Pacific politics, its governments, political elites and intellectuals are driving the trend to make Taiwan relevant to the Indo-Pacific. The question here is not whether Taiwan should be part of the Indo-Pacific. Taiwan has no choice. Nevertheless, this article illuminates the difficulties that emerge from the interlocking of realpolitik with the production of knowledge, along with the ensuing intersections among Taiwan studies, Indo-Pacific studies, Southeast Asian studies, Austronesian studies, Indigenous studies, and studies of new immigrants as well as relevant imaginaries. These challenges are complex and multi-faceted. This article extends a modest invitation for dialogue and ongoing contemplation regarding this topic. It proposes policy advice with examples to help Taiwanese policymakers, political elites, and educators understand how to incorporate decolonial thinking into their future planning related to the knowledge realm of the Indo-Pacific.

articles.title: When Intercultural Experience Sharpens the Palate: Food Culture, Disgust, and Acceptability Judgements in Chinese Adults
articles.authors: Roberto Filippi; Liang Hu; Olivia Yang; Lam Yuen Sun; Yinuo Tian; Tong Wu; Paola Bonifacci; Li Wei
articles.abstract: Food preferences and acceptability judgements are shaped by cultural experience, yet little is known about how transnational experience influences responses to culturally defined food norm violations. This study examined Chinese adults’ affective and evaluative responses to culturally appropriate and norm-violating food pairings from Chinese and Italian culinary contexts. One hundred and sixty-five Chinese adults completed a Culinary Violation Task, rating each pairing for disgust and appropriateness. Participants also reported whether they had lived abroad and completed a measure of dispositional disgust sensitivity. Trial-level mixed-effects models showed that norm-violating items elicited stronger disgust and lower appropriateness ratings than culturally appropriate items, with the violation effect substantially stronger for Chinese than Italian food items. Overseas experience was associated with heightened disgust responses to norm-violating items, especially Chinese food norm violations, while appropriateness judgements showed broader sensitivity to norm structure across both food cultures. Disgust sensitivity predicted stronger affective responses but did not account for the main overseas-experience effect. These findings challenge a simple exposure-as-openness account and suggest that transnational experience may heighten sensitivity to culturally patterned culinary norms, particularly when violations concern heritage food culture.

articles.title: Economic Impact of Global Warming in India: An Updated Review of Methods, Policies, and Recent Empirical Literature*
articles.authors: Naveen Kumar; Dibyendu Maiti
articles.abstract: This Chapter reviews empirical methodologies studying the impact of global warming on economic indicators inIndia. We begin with an analysis of global climate dynamics, which includes definitional debate of level and growtheffects, using climate econometrics to highlight the methodological issue in temperature-GDP econometrics, andsummarising methodological discussion on differentiation between weather and climate change. The chapter thenevaluates India's vulnerability to climate shocks, detailing macroeconomic impacts on critical sectors such asagriculture, health, and productivity, and reviews the evolution of India's environmental policies. We further reviewthe role of fiscal, regulatory, and monetary policies in facilitating climate adaptation and mitigation. Additionally,we explore diverse econometric approaches for estimating climate change impacts, including cross-sections andpanel methods. This review highlights the urgency of coordinated international action and the need for India toimplement robust climate policies to ensure sustainable economic growth and fulfil its emission reduction targets.

articles.title: Sardonic Vigilance: Emotional Self-Discipline and Politics-Over-Missiles Reactivity in Ukraine's Wartime Online Professional Stratum
articles.authors: Eugene Nayshtetik
articles.abstract: How does an educated audience express emotion through the third year of a full-scale war? I analyze 11,036 comments and 1.14 million reactions from the 2,281-person engaged audience of my own Facebook profile (Kyiv-dominant, 93% aged 35+, education well above national baseline), with every comment scored on reliability-gated LLM emotion dimensions in its original language. The resting register is sardonic vigilance: anger and irony high, hope low, and -- against a 65% national stress prevalence -- expressed distress in only 1.3% of comments, an absence I read as emotional self-discipline rather than absence of feeling. Joining a 34-event war calendar to the mood series, a placebo-permutation design yields one robust effect: hope (+0.61) and author-directed warmth surge only after credible political progress. The wider gradient -- strike waves barely move expressed mood while political-betrayal events coincide with the largest anger deltas -- is directionally stable under a drop-one-event jackknife but not distinguishable from placebo dates, and is offered as a hypothesis (habituation vs. topic-register matching) rather than a finding. A two-channel framework separates effortful, display-managed comments from one-click reactions as affect readouts of different cost; the million-reaction channel corroborates the comment-level picture. All claims carry their evidentiary tier; human validation of the instrument is pre-registered and pending.

articles.title: Exposure Mixes, Conversation Sorts: Tone-Based Rather Than Ideological Homophily in a Wartime Online Public
articles.authors: Eugene Nayshtetik
articles.abstract: Online-segregation research, built largely on anonymous, anglophone, algorithmically curated platforms, reports that interaction sorts by ideology. I test whether that holds in a deliberately different setting: the named-identity, non-anglophone public that formed around my own Facebook posting on defense, technology and politics across 32 wartime months in Ukraine (2,281 engaged accounts, 11,036 comments). Separating exposure (a co-engagement graph) from selection (a reply graph), and attaching 33 LLM-derived expressive, moral, epistemic and political attributes with published instrument reliability, I find: (1) psychographic partitions are orthogonal to interaction communities (max adjusted Rand index 0.031, robust across co-engagement, reply and multiplex layers), and the communities themselves are unstable across content splits -- arenas, not tribes; (2) co-engagement is non-assortative and strongly role-disassortative (z = -15.7): exposure mixes; (3) reply-partner choice sorts on tone and epistemic style -- civility, irony, contempt, anger, conspiracism -- with bootstrap CIs excluding zero; (4) every measured ideological dimension is null in both graphs. A direct shared-resample bootstrap puts the tone-minus-ideology block difference at 0.082 (95% CI [0.035, 0.131]), surviving disattenuation and FDR control. In this public, conversation sorts on manners, not beliefs -- a candidate boundary condition on the ideological-homophily pattern, offered with explicit range-restriction caveats and pending human validation of the instrument.

articles.title: Under what conditions do social media users perceive borderline content moderation as more legitimate?
articles.authors: Diyi Liu
articles.abstract: Content moderation navigates tensions between efficiency, consistency, and contextual understanding, yet theoretical foundations explaining user perceptions of moderation practices remain underdeveloped, particularly across diverse cultural contexts. Through a pre-registered survey experiment with TikTok users in Indonesia (n=304) and Pakistan (n=303), this study examines how moderation source, procedural contestability, and outcome alignment with user preferences individually and jointly influence perceived legitimacy of content moderation for morally, emotionally, and politically charged content. Results reveal that outcome alignment with users’ moderation preferences most consistently and significantly enhances perceived legitimacy across both countries. Moderation source (algorithmic systems, human moderators, governmental agencies, or civil society organizations) and procedural contestability showed limited direct effects, with only marginal benefits observed in Indonesia. Critically, when algorithmic moderation decisions are contestable and align with user preferences, perceived legitimacy peaks; however, when contestable algorithmic decisions contradict user preferences, legitimacy perceptions drop significantly. Individual differences in support for moral restrictions on free speech also influence user perceptions of content moderation. As algorithmic content moderation systems continue expanding globally, understanding these legitimation dynamics, especially in these understudied morally conservative contexts, proves essential for designing approaches that effectively govern online speech while maintaining user trust across diverse cultural communities.

articles.title: The Road Violence Toll of Urban Sprawl:  A Registered Report of Collisions with Cyclists and Pedestrians in England and Wales
articles.authors: Jose Pina-Sánchez; Caroline Tait; Roger Beecham; Juan Fonseca Zamora
articles.abstract: The risk of road collision for cyclists and pedestrians is higher in sprawls than in city centres. As a result of commuting, we also suspect that the higher risk of collision caused by residents from car-dependent sprawls is not confined to their own areas but disproportionately imposed on city dwellers. However, due to the absence of direct comparisons we do not know the specific road violence imposed by urban sprawls. This registered report will combine collision data from the Department for Transport and the Urban Grammar Spatial Signatures dataset that characterises geography by form and function to estimate the share of road violence induced by sprawls’ residents within and outside their localities, and relative to levels of road violence generated by city dwellers in England and Wales. Furthermore, using the subset of collisions data including contributory factors, we will provide a lower bound estimate regarding the share of collisions that could be classified as motoring offences. Drawing on these findings we will reassess the widely held beliefs of: i) sprawls as safer than city centres; ii) residents from urban sprawls are more law-abiding than city dwellers; and iii) crime prevention strategies that promote car-dependent developments as effective.

articles.title: From Free Access to Winner-Takes-All - A Reactive Sequence Perspective on Digital Platform Capitalism
articles.authors: Jürgen Beyer
articles.abstract: This paper reinterprets the emergence of the digital winner-takes-all economy through Mahoney’s concept of the reactive sequence. Rather than treating digital concentration as the natural result of network effects, scale economies, or technological lock-in, it argues that these mechanisms became decisive only within a historically specific institutional trajectory. The early internet was constituted as a non-commercial sphere shaped by gift exchange, hacker ethics, open sharing, and community-oriented practices. As these norms diffused into mass usage, they were simplified into low willingness to pay and expectations of free access. Digital firms reacted by monetizing other market sides, especially advertisers, sellers, and platform-dependent businesses. This adaptation intensified network effects, weakened price differentiation, and privileged market leaders. Over time, observed concentration became generalized into a winner-takes-all expectation and institutionalized through venture-capital strategies, rapid scaling norms, and founder self-understandings. The essay extends this sequence to contemporary AI firms and their emerging wealth elites.

articles.title: Ecological Sustainability and Indigenous Knowledge Systems in Textile Dyeing: The Dabu Mud-Resist Architecture of SA Fab in Bagru
articles.authors: Saloni Agrawal
articles.abstract: The transition toward sustainable manufacturing in the global textile industry necessitates a re-evaluation of indigenous, zero-chemical production frameworks. This paper analyzes the ancient "Dabu" mud-resist block printing process utilized by the Chhipa artisan community of Bagru, Rajasthan. Acting as a primary case study, the preservation efforts and operational architecture of SA Fab demonstrate how 450-year-old botanical and geological methodologies offer a scalable alternative to ecologically damaging synthetic textile dyeing. By deconstructing the chemical composition of the mud-resist paste (kali mitti, chuna, gond, and beedhan) and the natural indigo vat dyeing protocol, this paper formalizes the Dabu technique into the academic record as a vital framework for contemporary sustainable fashion.

articles.title: One Gradient, Not Two Poles: The Asymmetric Structure of Violence Tolerance in the Brazilian Electorate
articles.authors: Joao Feres
articles.abstract: Does the ideological cleavage that organizes Brazilian politics also structure citizens’ tolerance of violence, and does it do so symmetrically? Drawing on the August 2023 wave of the national survey A Cara da Democracia (N = 2,558), this article treats tolerance of violence as a multidimensional construct — lethal punitivism, tolerance of authoritarian rupture, and approval of insurrectionary violence — and tests how three operationalizations of the political cleavage (the 2022 second-round vote, left–right self-placement, and partisan identity) predict each. Dimensionality diagnostics show that the coup-justification items form a strong unidimensional scale while the punitivism items do not cohere, a substantive result that disciplines the modeling. Weighted logistic models yield three findings. First, the cleavage is large and graded, concentrated on tolerance of authoritarian rupture: a Bolsonaro voter is roughly five times as likely as a Lula voter to justify a coup, net of controls. Second, the cleavage is asymmetric rather than a symmetric polarization: entering antipetismo and antibolsonarismo jointly, only rejection of the Workers’ Party predicts greater tolerance of violence, while rejection of Bolsonaro’s party is mildly protective; a formal test rejects their equality on every endpoint. Third, the gap widens with education and income — a pattern of the comfortable rather than the deprived. A multiple correspondence analysis recovers a single violence-endorsement gradient onto which the cleavage maps asymmetrically. Crime salience neither predicts punitivism nor amplifies the vote effect. The Brazilian case suggests that the mass-attitudinal substrate of democratic backsliding is organized by political-identity alignment, not by fear of crime, and that it is asymmetric by construction.

articles.title: Open K-Anonymized Aggregates from a Free Personal-Finance Calculator Platform: Methodology, Dataset, and Public Mirror
articles.authors: Jere Salmisto
articles.abstract: We present CalcFi, an open infrastructure for citation-backed consumer-finance calculators built on US primary economic and tax sources. Every calculator on calcfi.app derives its inputs from a closed whitelist of primary sources (IRS, BLS, BEA, FRED, U.S. Treasury, CFPB, HUD, U.S. Census Bureau, SSA, Freddie Mac PMMS) with provenance enforced via a SourcedValue type contract, retrievedAt timestamps, and stale-data floors. We document the methodology, the data layer's exposure via open REST APIs, an MCP server distributed on npm and PyPI, llms.txt for LLM-discovery, and the archival flow into Zenodo with citable per-snapshot DOIs. We argue consumer-finance calculators should ship audit trails at parity with academic data citations, and demonstrate a reproducibility framework that enables third-party replication of every numeric output. CC-BY 4.0.

articles.title: Germany as a ‘social state’: post-migration geographical imaginaries as visions of the good society
articles.authors: Margherita Cusmano
articles.abstract: Migration research increasingly examines geographical imaginaries but typically treats them as pre-migration constructs that collide with post-migration realities and subsequently dissolve. This article challenges this assumption by showing how migrants actively reconstitute the imaginaries of their destination after arrival. Drawing on interviews and group discussions with 47 migrants in Germany, it identifies a geographical imaginary of Germany as a ‘social state’ marked by low inequality and extensive welfare provisions. I argue that geographical imaginaries are not solely a pre-migration factor but also evolve post-arrival through migrants’ experiences within specific institutional settings. Furthermore, this study reconceptualizes geographical imaginaries as multi-layered: alongside descriptive understandings of place, they encompass normative visions of the good society. Through the lens of geographical imaginaries, migrants therefore evaluate not only their own prospects but also the broader social order.

articles.title: Die historisch-soziologische Erforschung der Abel-Biogramme: Methodologische Notizen, Anmerkungen zur Verwendung von AI in der qualitativen Sozialforschung und ein Ergebnis der Fallanalysen
articles.authors: Lehrstuhl Sozialstrukturanalyse; Robert Schäfer
articles.abstract: Dieser Beitrag versammelt Forschungsnotizen, die sich durch die Analysen der Nazi-Biogramme, die im Kontext des Preissauschreibens von T. F. Abel 1934 entstanden sind, ergeben haben. Das Ziel ist es, zukünftigen Forschungen die Arbeit zu erleichtern und einige neue Informationen zum Gegenstand beizusteuern. Der erste Teil behandelt Herausforderungen im Umgang mit dem Datenkorpus und Ansätze dafür, AI-Tools für die qualitative Forschung zu nutzen. Im zweiten Teil werden forschungsstrategische und methodologische Fragen besprochen. Im dritten Teil wird als inhaltliches Ergebnis der Fallanalysen erläutert, dass eine eigentümliche Dialektik von Agency und Fatalismus in der nationalsozialistischen Weltanschauung eine zentrale Rolle spielen.

articles.title: Scrolling Through the Plate: Motivations Behind Why Young Adults Post Food Pictures on Social Media - A Qualitative Study from New Zealand
articles.authors: Saswata Ray
articles.abstract:
Background: Food photography has become a defining behaviour of young adults on social media, yet the motivations underlying this practice remain incompletely understood. Existing literature has examined photo-sharing broadly, but few studies have explored the specific intentions behind food picture posting through qualitative inquiry in the Oceania context.
Objective: To investigate the intentions and psychosocial motivations behind why young adults post food-related pictures on social media platforms.
Methods: A qualitative approach using semi-structured interviews was employed. Fifteen young adults aged 18 to 25 years, residing in Auckland, New Zealand, participated. Purposive and opportunistic sampling strategies were used. Participants shared five food pictures they had personally posted on social media, and interviews explored their motivations. Thematic analysis following the Braun and Clarke (2006) framework was applied to the data.
Results: Four principal themes emerged to explain why young adults post food pictures: (1) Cooked food - sharing home-prepared food to express pride and seek social validation; (2) Pretty food - sharing aesthetically appealing food driven by visual appeal and associated social reasons; (3) The highlight of the day - capturing food as a memory of significant moments, meetings, cultural occasions, or as miscellaneous social expression; and (4) Exotic food - sharing food that is rare or unavailable in one's country of origin. Additional behavioural dimensions included the immediacy of posting and platform preference, with Instagram favoured over Facebook among this cohort.
Conclusion: Young adults post food pictures on social media for a range of psychosocial reasons extending beyond aesthetics. Attribution theory, social gratification, and collective or we-intention frameworks offer explanatory lenses. These findings contribute to the growing body of knowledge on food-related social media behaviour among young adults and have implications for health communication and digital food literacy.

articles.title: Divergence of Economic Opportunity in Average Places: Pathways of Stagnation
articles.authors: Howard Hao Wang; Harald Bathelt; Maximilian Buchholz
articles.abstract: Widening interregional inequality and growing political divides have led to a growing body of work investigating extreme cases of economic growth and decline. In contrast, our research of the U.S. urban system analyzes ‘average places’, defined as urban regions with less than 250,000 people and average income levels in 1993. This analysis focuses on places with low growth between 1993 and 2017 and complements an earlier paper that investigates high-growth average places. Using fuzzy-set qualitative comparative analysis (fsQCA), we identify three trajectories of stagnation with different absent or present socio-economic and institutional conditions. These are (i) isolated manufacturing/nonspecialized towns, (ii) satellite manufacturing/non-specialized towns, and (iii) disconnecting publicsector/non-specialized towns. Our results suggest that low-growth average places are more concentrated in a limited number of trajectories than their high-growth counterparts, and that they are largely characterized by the absence of conditions, as opposed to their presence. This is not simply a consequence of deindustrialization but must be understood in combination with other context conditions.

articles.title: IranWar.ai: An Open-Source Event-Level Dataset of the 2026 US–Iran Conflict
articles.authors: Jacob E. Thomas; Aigerim Alpysbekova; Eugene Osei Mensah; Nigel Masara; Prakhar Sharma
articles.abstract: We present the IranWar.ai Event-Level Research Dataset, a structured, openly available record of the first 30 days of the 2026 US–Iran conflict (Operation Epic Fury, February 28–March 29, 2026). The dataset comprises 1,653 event-level observations across 48 variables, derived from 16 curated JSON data files that power the IranWar.ai public intelligence dashboard. Each observation represents a discrete, verifiable event: an airstrike on a named target, a retaliatory attack, a financial market data point, a daily casualty estimate by faction, a naval deployment, or a diplomatic development. Nine analytical domains are covered (military strikes, retaliations, financial markets, humanitarian impacts, diplomatic events, naval operations, cyber activity, general military, and contextual data) integrating open-source intelligence (OSINT) from more than 60 specific source streams, including CENTCOM, the IDF, ACLED, the Iranian Red Crescent, the IAEA, Bloomberg, and the IMO. Beyond its function as a research resource, this paper examines the dataset as an epistemic object. Assembled in part by AI research agents and carrying known imperfections, the dataset is released with full transparency about its own limitations, including interpolated values, unverified records, and probabilistic confidence ratings. We argue that this transparency constitutes a deliberate counter-technology: a structural intervention against the conditions of the information flood, which we frame as a dual-layer epistemic crisis comprising both a structural component (the volume, velocity, and synthetic origin of available data overwhelming the infrastructure available to evaluate it) and a strategic component (the deliberate production of disinformation by state and non-state actors). Radical transparency is necessary for both layers but, by itself, is sufficient only against the structural layer; the strategic layer additionally requires interpretive expertise that situates discrepancies as political facts rather than data-quality defects. We further examine the dataset's relationship to AI training pipelines, model collapse dynamics, and the degradation of provenance metadata across summarization and citation chains. To address the limits of any single research team's expertise, we set out a tentative, non-exhaustive research agenda and invite collaborators across political science, public health, economics, military studies, media studies, and information science. The live dashboard, codebook, source data, prompts, manifests, reproducible extraction script, and versioned research releases are publicly available at github.com/jethomasphd/WarTheater; scheduled research releases are monthly, with interim correction releases when warranted.

articles.title: Going Gunless
articles.authors: Dru Stevenson
articles.abstract: Firearm policy in the United States is subject to longstanding political gridlock. Up to now, most of the legal academic literature has focused on the constitutionality of various—or any—regulations regarding firearm possession, sales, or usage. This article inverts the problem and proposes a system for voluntary registration and certification of nonowners, those who want to waive or renounce their Second Amendment rights as a matter of personal conviction. The proposed system is analogous to both the registration of conscientious objectors during wartime conscriptions, and the newer suicide prevention laws whereby individuals can add their names to a do-not-sell list for firearm dealers—though the proposal made here is broader and more permanent. Voluntary registration, with official certification, would serve three important purposes. First, this would help create social identification markers for the gunless-by-choice movement, something that historically has been missing; formal signals and labels of identification with a movement are necessary for a movement’s success, especially with prohibition or abstinence movements. Second, registration and certification as gunless would be a personal moral commitment marker; all societies provide ways for solemnizing one’s vows and solidifying one’s resolve on serious, lifelong moral decisions. Third, certification allows for a market signaling effect, providing useful information that can trigger a beneficial response from the private sector; market responses, in turn, provide useful information about otherwise hotly-debated beliefs, such as whether guns in fact enhance or reduce safety for individuals and public places. This article develops each of these points and offers a unique and simple regulatory and statutory alternative for a new system of registration and certification.

articles.title: What We Talk about When We Talk about Guns: Four Decades of Firearms Coverage in the New York Times
articles.authors: Brett C Burkhardt; Aimee Dinnin Huff
articles.abstract: Guns are potent cultural objects in the United States, a fact that has spurred much recent research. Most of that work centers the beliefs, discourses, and actions of gun enthusiasts. Less understood are the cultural dimensions of guns in the wider population, where gun owners are in the minority. This paper considers the cultural life of guns by studying the language used to depict them in a prominent US mass media outlet over four decades. We use structural topic modelling to describe the New York Times’ coverage of guns from 1980 to 2019. The analysis reveals that the coverage centers danger and the societal responses to it, albeit in different ways over time. Whereas local street violence and criminal punishment dominated coverage during much of the time period, high-profile mass shootings and efforts at federal legislation have become more salient in recent years. In examining depictions of guns in mass media, the paper contributes to a growing body of literature that seeks to understand the cultural life of guns in US society.

articles.title: Village Productivity Index (VPI) / Indeks Produktivitas Desa (IPD): A New Framework for Measuring Rural Economic Contribution Beyond Administrative Performance
articles.authors: Sahroni Darmawan
articles.abstract: Indonesia channels more than IDR 70 trillion annually through village fiscal transfers to 75,265 village governments without a nationally standardized instrument for measuring the economic productivity of recipient units. Existing performance indices (the Village Development Index (IDM), SDGs Desa, and Indeks Desa) evaluate administrative compliance and service delivery; none measures village economic output. This paper introduces the Village Productivity Index (VPI/IPD), a composite index operationalizing the productive territorial unit (PTU) framework to assess rural economic productivity across five weighted dimensions: primary sector output (25%), economic diversification (20%), labor productivity (20%), Dana Desa fiscal return (20%), and market integration (15%). Theoretically, the VPI integrates Pritchett, Woolcock, and Andrews’s (2013) isomorphic mimicry framework with North’s (1990) institutional economics and OECD Rural Policy 3.0. Methodologically, the index employs Winsorized normalization at national percentiles and a three-year backward-moving average for the fiscal return dimension to address outlier sensitivity and capital circulation lag. A stratified pilot empirical analysis on 1,200 villages across six provinces demonstrates meaningful inter-village discrimination (mean VPI = 51.3, SD = 14.7; range 18.4–82.6) and preliminary predictive validity with district PDRB per capita. Monte Carlo sensitivity analysis confirms classification stability for 78.4% of villages across plausible weight variations. The comparative analysis positions the VPI within the landscape of rural development indicators, addressing a key gap in existing administrative frameworks. Integration of VPI into Dana Desa allocation formulae under UU HKPD No. 1 Tahun 2022 is simulated to assess potential resource redirection and aggregate economic outcomes. Keywords: Village Productivity Index; Indeks Produktivitas Desa; rural economic productivity; productive territorial unit; Dana Desa; isomorphic mimicry; fiscal transfer; Indonesia village policy; rural value added; composite index methodology

articles.title: Mapping Australia’s Regulatory Architecture: A Seven-Layer Census of Regulatory Authority
articles.authors: Michael McGuinness
articles.abstract: Australia’s regulatory system is large, fragmented, and structurally opaque. No jurisdiction maintains a complete inventory of its regulators, and no national body tracks the full extent of regulatory authority across the federation. This preprint presents a cross‑jurisdictional census of regulatory authority in Australia using a seven‑layer architecture model. The method identifies and classifies regulators across statutory agencies, embedded units, national schemes, tribunals, and forms of regulatory dark matter. The findings indicate that Australia operates between 1,244 and 1,404 regulatory bodies and loci of authority, with a central estimate of approximately 1,360. Every jurisdiction, regardless of population size, operates a triple‑digit regulatory system. The study contributes a replicable census method, a seven‑layer model of regulatory architecture, and an account of how regulatory authority is distributed across jurisdictions and institutional layers. These findings have important implications for regulatory governance, visibility, coordination, and system stewardship.

articles.title: Patient Perceptions of Artificial Intelligence in Suicide Risk Assessment Models: Insights from Three Tribal Communities
articles.authors: Valentín Quiroz de la Sierra; Rohan Dayal; Paul Rebman; Novalene Goklish; Hannah Metwally; Pua Yang; Kristin Mitchell; Timothy Werwie; Renae Begay; Roy Adams
articles.abstract: Suicide rates in American Indian and Alaska Native (AIAN) communities are nearly double the US national average. Artificial Intelligence (AI)-assisted suicide risk identification tools offer potential for early detection, but their cultural acceptability and ethical implications in healthcare settings that serve AIAN populations remain underexplored. This study examines patient perspectives on existing suicide screening practices, stigma, trust, patient-provider communication, and AI-assisted suicide prevention in Tribal-serving healthcare settings. A qualitative study was conducted in three Tribal communities in the Southwestern US involving 25 AIAN adults who had received care at a Tribal-serving healthcare facility in the past two years. Semi-structured interviews explored experiences with existing suicide screening, barriers to care-seeking, and perceptions of AI-assisted suicide risk identification tools. Inductive content analysis identified key barriers and facilitators. Stigma remains a primary barrier to seeking care as exacerbated by distrust in the healthcare system. Existing suicide screening practices were perceived as impersonal, which discouraged disclosure. Participants emphasized the need for culturally responsive, strengths-based communication to foster trust. AI-assisted risk identification elicited mixed reactions; while some saw potential benefits, others expressed concerns about privacy, risk of misdiagnosis, and the potential for long-term stigma. Culturally grounded suicide prevention efforts must prioritize trust-building, Tribal governance over AI-assisted tools, and healthcare provider training in patient-centered communication. AI technology holds promise but must be developed under Tribal governance and with community leadership to ensure ethical implementation. With community guidance, AI-assisted tools can complement existing prevention efforts and expand access to culturally responsive care.

articles.title: Comparing Campaign Rhetoric and Legislative Behavior Through Issue-Specific Polarization in the US House of Representatives, 2021–2025
articles.authors: Jillian Pizza; Klint Kanopka
articles.abstract: Voters make decisions on who to elect based, in part, upon candidate messaging and party platforms. Here we examine the relationship between campaign rhetoric and legislative behavior among members of the 117th and 118th United States House of Representatives. Using campaign text and roll-call voting data, the paper compares polarization across policy domains through an IssueIRT model of legislative behavior and a Wordscores model of campaign rhetoric. We find that polarization varies substantially across issue areas and that the relationship between rhetoric and behavior is not uniform. Civil Rights exhibits highly polarized and morally distinct partisan rhetoric, while Government Operations rhetoric displays a more continuous ideological spectrum despite strongly polarized voting behavior. International Affairs appears comparatively less polarized in both rhetoric and legislative behavior, with greater thematic overlap between parties surrounding national security and international engagement. We also observe a second latent dimension in congressional voting associated with institutionalism and intra-party factionalism. Republican legislators exhibit substantially greater dispersion along this dimension, while Democrats remain comparatively cohesive in both rhetoric and voting behavior.

articles.title: Network analysis in advertising research, reimagined: A methodological catalyst for structural and institutional insight
articles.authors: Alvin Zhou; harsh taneja; Ewa Maslowska
articles.abstract: As digital systems increasingly mediate advertising, the field must move beyond message effects to engage with the structural and institutional dynamics that define the advertising ecosystem. This article argues that network analysis, when reimagined beyond its typical application to social media interactions, offers powerful tools for understanding the relationships among brands, consumers, media, influencers, and other intermediaries and stakeholders. Through two case demonstrations, one on influencer marketing and the other on media planning, we illustrate how bipartite projections and inferential network analysis uncover latent relational patterns such as brand competition and influencer heterophily. We further explore alternative strategies for constructing networks from advertising data, including sequence-based, co-occurrence-based, and influence-based approaches. These models enable a shift from descriptive to explanatory analyses and open new pathways for theorizing advertising as a relational, institutional, and dynamic system. Ultimately, we position network analysis as a methodological and theoretical catalyst for structural thinking in advertising research, facilitating deeper engagement with ecosystem-level complexity and interdisciplinary integration with computational social science.

articles.title: Distinguishing Sustainability from Durability: Hidden Convergences and the Logic of Redirection
articles.authors: Alexandre Monnin
articles.abstract:
Sustainability and durability are often treated as overlapping terms in environmental debates. This article argues that distinguishing them reveals hidden convergences between positions usually presented as divergent. Sustainability evaluates trajectories under ecological limits, material dependence, limited substitutability, and systemic risk. Durability refers to the organized capacity of material and institutional arrangements to maintain a function through time, through maintenance, repair, support chains, and the governance of obsolescence.
The argument is conceptually driven and empirically informed by an eight-month advisory study conducted by the author for a public development finance institution, involving twenty interviews, a 100-page internal report, and a 25-page synthesis. Drawing on Halloy’s contrast between living and zombie technologies, the article develops a sustainability–durability matrix distinguishing sustainable continuity, durable lock-in, sustainable fragility, and negative inheritances. It then uses redirection, organized withdrawal, and Deron’s framework of ruins, ghosts, seeds, and visions to rethink how inheritances may be maintained, supported, redirected, or allowed to decline.

articles.title: Sensing the Research Field: A Somatic Methodology for Awareness-Based Collaborative Inquiry
articles.authors: Louise Massacrier; Zoe Lina Wehmeyer; Marc Santolini
articles.abstract:
The complexity of contemporary challenges demands collaborative, interdisciplinary
forms of knowledge production capable of integrating multiple ways of knowing. Yet the
dominant research model brackets the conditions that make genuine collective inquiry
possible — the quality of presence, the relational ground, the somatic attunement that
builds trust and collective intelligence. This paper presents a methodological prototype
that both implements and measures these conditions, drawing on two Social Presencing
Theater practices: the Stuck practice as a daily somatic check-in building
inter-researcher attunement, and 3D mapping as a collaborative material practice for
project development and field sensing. Two researchers integrated both practices into
an ongoing project on aesthetic sustainability and embodied learning in design. Results
show statistically traceable change: posture shifts significantly toward openness within
each session (p = 0.012), entry posture orientation shifts progressively inward across
the research arc (p = 0.02–0.03), trust-coded language increases significantly after each
practice (p = 0.023), and the affective valence of entry postures shifts from negative to
positive over time. A compensatory within-session dynamic is also identified: the
practice orients toward the affective quality latent in the entry posture rather than
amplifying what is already present. Situated within the science of the social field and
awareness-based design, we propose this prototype as a contribution to a knowledge
production that values the quality of the process — the relational ground, the somatic
attunement, the container built between researchers — as much as the outcomes it
generates.

articles.title: The Uncounted: A Case for Federal Enumeration of the Bereaved Parent Demographic in the United States
articles.authors: David Wittenburg
articles.abstract: This research presents the legislative precedents and statutory arguments required to authorize the formal enumeration of the bereaved parent population within active federal surveys. It outlines the specific administrative instruments across federal agencies capable of restoring demographic tracking systems discontinued in 1885. This document is cross-indexed with the Social Science Research Network (SSRN) under registry metadata.

articles.title: How Young Adults Prioritize Public Values When Choosing Mental Wellness Apps: A Mixed-Methods Study
articles.authors: Tonka Milošević; Marilisa Boffo; Julia van Weert; Esther Rozendaal
articles.abstract:
Background: Mental wellness apps (MWAs) are increasingly used by young adults as demand for mental health support grows. The rapid adoption of AI in MWAs creates opportunities for more personalized care but also raises risks, including reduced user autonomy and sensitive data privacy concerns. Since MWAs are classified as “general wellness” tools, they fall outside the EU's Medical Devices Regulation, potentially leaving key public values unprotected. However, little is known about how a vulnerable subgroup of young adults, university students, assess or adopt MWAs, or how they weigh different public values in their decisions.
Methods: A mixed-methods approach was employed combining semi-structured interviews and a hierarchical card sorting task. University students at a Dutch university (N=27) were interviewed on the role of 10 public values (autonomy, authenticity, agency, fairness, integrity, solidarity, privacy, security, trustworthiness, and transparency) in the decision-making process behind choosing to adopt an MWA. Additionally, they ranked these 10 values on importance in this process. 
Results: Reflexive thematic analysis yielded five themes: (1) motives for private MWA use, (2) tensions between digital resignation and privacy concerns, (3) how severity and urgency of mental health needs shape value trade-offs, (4) perceived personal benefits or drawbacks of specific values, and (5) sources of trust when adopting an MWA. By-person factor analysis of value rankings revealed four prioritization patterns centered on: (1) data protection, (2) effectiveness, (3) secure and reliable outcomes, or (4) fairness. No consensus in value priorities emerged.
Discussion and conclusions: Although MWAs are unregulated, students are expected to make informed choices and bear the responsibility of consequences. Yet, their value priorities differ depending on personal circumstances, making some more exposed to risks than others. Many feel resigned and unable to influence how MWAs handle the values they care about, leading them to downplay those values, assuming apps will not realize them anyway. The findings show concrete mechanisms through which harm can emerge, especially considering most students turn to MWAs mainly in times of urgent need. This further emphasizes the need for regulation to ensure MWA safety, quality, and protection of public values, particularly as AI features continue to expand.
Keywords: mental wellness apps, mHealth, mental health, university students, value trade-offs, public values

articles.title: Environmental Citizen Science in India: Objectives, Challenges, and Impacts
articles.authors: Anjali Bharati; VV Binoy
articles.abstract: Environmental Citizen Science (ECS) - involving community members in the research addressing environmental and conservation challenges - is widely promoted for targeted data collection and analysis at broader spatial and temporal scales, and to encourage public engagement with science. Keeping perspectives and experiences of the coordinators of 22 ECS from India in focus this study explored the purpose of the projects, challenges faced during implementation, and their contribution to the science and society. Most of the ECS we studied were contributory in nature and had dual targets: biodiversity data generation and strengthening societal involvement in science. The role of coordinators in ECS in India was a mix of boundary worker and knowledge broker. Minimal availability of resources, reduced participation from the rural regions, and inconsistency in involvement and contribution of the citizen scientists were the major challenges ECS faced. Large-scale collection of biodiversity data, and increased visibility of the taxa and ecosystems were the important outcomes of ECS reported by the coordinators. However, assessment of learning by the participants and evaluation of the social impact of the ECS were not common. The need for developing and implementing an all-inclusive support mechanism and evidence-based policy framework to ensure the efficacy and sustainability of the ECS in India is discussed in the light of the present findings.

articles.title: Формальное ядро цифрового поля Казахстана: контур, концентрация и пределы публичной видимости
articles.authors: Nikolay Sudnikov
articles.abstract:
The article reconstructs the formal core of Kazakhstan’s IT field through a series of empirical studies within the Digital Class research programme. Instead of treating the field as a sector composed of “IT companies plus IT workers”, it is analysed as the overlap of three maps: a formal OKED-based contour, an upper layer concentrated by taxpayer-level payment weight, and the regimes through which technical competence becomes publicly and professionally legible.
The main findings are as follows. The formal contour includes approximately 32.5 thousand active entities selected by a fixed set of IT-related OKED codes. Almost half of the contour consists of individual entrepreneurs, while software development as a single OKED code accounts for about 40 percent of the contour. Payment weight is highly concentrated: a narrow upper layer - less than 1 percent of the contour - accounts for roughly two thirds of the aggregate payment trace, whereas about 44 percent of entities show no significant trace in the latest annual slice. Substantive classification of the upper layer shows that software production is only part of it, alongside enterprise implementation, embedded IT in non-software organizations, state digital infrastructure, and educational or symbolic layers. The article therefore argues that economic weight and public visibility are substantially decoupled. The data support claims about formal structure, payment-weight concentration and public readability; they do not support claims about revenue, profit, turnover, workforce size, influence, control or actual social mobility outcomes.

articles.title: Will a Rising Middle Class in the Global South Reshape Politics?
articles.authors: Joel Stillerman
articles.abstract: Discussions of the “global middle class” in the early 21st Century argue that this group expanded in the Global South from 1990-2010 as a product of economic growth. This discussion echoes the 1960s debate regarding the relationship between economic development and democracy. This entry focuses on the economic structure of middle classes in the Global South, their role in housing and consumption markets as it influences middle class urban politics, and the role of middle classes in political change in the Global South. Middle classes have grown in the Global South, though their economic characteristics are diverse and their members are at risk of downward mobility due to precarious employment patterns and periodic economic shocks. The expansion of gated communities and shopping malls in cities of the Global South has generated patterns of cross-class segregation that leads members of the middle classes to engage in exclusionary practices toward the poor. However, some members of the middle classes engage in progressive urban social movements. While scholars in the 1960s were optimistic that a growing middle class would encourage democratization, subsequent scholarship has shown that middle classes display inconsistent support for democracy and engage in diverse forms of political expression. These expressions include both participation in formal politics and engagement with social movements. Additionally, middle classes are internally divided based on religious affiliation, racial and ethnic identities, birth cohort membership, and occupation, leading to competing political identities across middle class subgroups. This diversity suggests that efforts to predict how the middle classes may reshape politics should attend to national historical and political legacies to understand the distinctive pathways of middle-class politics in the Global South.

articles.title: Crime and Symbolic Boundaries in Unsettled Times: An Analysis of Middle-Class Responses to Fear in Chile
articles.authors: Joel Stillerman
articles.abstract:
Students of crime in the U.S. examine drug economies in poor communities (Bourgois 1995; Contreras 2013), poor residents’ adaptive behaviors (Kirk and Matsuda 2011; Sampson, Raudenbush, and Earls 1997) and punitive crime policies (Garland 2023; Picket 2019; Wacquant 2010). Research on Latin America, the most violent region in the world (Vilalta 2020), focuses on criminal governance (Feldmann and Luna 2022), violence in poor communities (Auyero and Sobering 2019), and the popularity of punitive policies (Chouhy, Singer, and Lehmann 2022). However, few scholars have examined middle class residents’ experiences of crime. This article builds on work on middle classes (Caldeira 2000; Villareal 2025), and utilizes interviews and observations in three Santiago, Chile communities. Findings focus on middle-class Chileans’ interpretations of crime, adaptive behaviors, and analyses of crime policies. In unsettled times (Swidler 1986), individuals construct symbolic boundaries (Lamont 1992, 2000, 2018) with criminals and their peers. Direct experiences with crime as well as cultural and ideological beliefs shape middle class individuals’ contrasting interpretations of crime and construction of boundaries with others. These divergent attitudes reflect enduring fissures within Chilean society over its dictatorship (1973-1990) and recent social upheaval (protests, immigration, and rising crime), complicating universalizing narratives about a punitive Chilean citizenry.
Keywords: crime,

articles.title: The Formal Core of Kazakhstan's Digital Field: Contour, Concentration, and the Limits of Public Visibility
articles.authors: Nikolay Sudnikov
articles.abstract: This article reconstructs the formal core of Kazakhstan's IT field from a cumulative series of empirical pilots conducted within the Digital Class research programme. Instead of treating the field as an ordinary sector made up of IT firms plus IT workers, the article reconstructs it through three partially independent maps: the formal contour defined by official economic activity codes (OKED), the upper layer identified by taxpayer-level aggregate payment trace, and the regimes through which technical production becomes publicly and professionally legible. The analysis uses taxpayer-level aggregate payment traces as a proxy for relative payment weight. These traces are used only as shares and concentration indicators; no absolute payment amounts or entity-level payment tables are published. The article's main findings are as follows. First, the formal contour contains approximately 32,500 active entities selected through a fixed set of IT-related OKED codes. By count, the contour is more than half individual entrepreneurs, while software development as a single code accounts for roughly 40 percent of the contour. Second, payment weight is highly concentrated: a narrow upper layer of fewer than one percent of entities accounts for roughly two thirds of the aggregate payment trace, while about 44 percent of entities show no significant annual trace in the latest yearly slice. Third, manual classification of the upper layer shows that software production is only one component of the formal upper contour, alongside enterprise implementation, embedded IT functions in non-software organizations, state digital infrastructure and educational-symbolic actors. Finally, economic weight and public visibility are substantially decoupled. The article therefore treats the digital class not as a directly measurable population, but as a field object that becomes observable only through disciplined layering of formal, payment, production-boundary and visibility evidence. The data support claims about formal structure, payment concentration and public legibility. They do not support claims about revenue, profit, turnover, employment, influence, control, hidden networks or actual social mobility.

articles.title: Digital Platform Work Beyond the Workplace: Household Risk and Welfare Inaccessibility among Delivery Rider Families in China
articles.authors: Chen Zhang; Wenqiao Jin
articles.abstract: Digital platform work is usually analysed through algorithmic control, flexible contracting and risk transfer between platforms and workers. This study extends this debate beyond the workplace by examining how platform-mediated labour insecurity becomes household precarity and welfare inaccessibility. Drawing on a qualitative multiple-case study of 22 delivery-rider households in Xiamen, China, together with anonymised service case records from 10 community-based support cases, the analysis shows how app-based coordination, data-mediated income visibility, platform-account use and ambiguous employment relations shape family decisions about rent, debt, illness, schooling and care. Spouses, children and older parents absorb platform-related risks by adjusting employment, care routines and settlement plans, while fragmented welfare rules often turn work insecurity into administrative burden. The study contributes to scholarship on digital labour, employment relations and social protection by showing how technology-mediated labour insecurity is reproduced through household relations and institutional boundaries.

articles.title: Das „politische Vorfeld“ als sekundäre Agitator:innen. Wie kann politische Online-Mobilisierung anhand einer zeitgemäßen Erweiterung des autoritären Sozialcharakters erklärt werden?
articles.authors: Melina Lenzner
articles.abstract: Diese Bachelorarbeit untersucht, wie politische Online-Mobilisierung durch Influencer:innen des sogenannten „politischen Vorfelds“ mithilfe einer zeitgemäßen Erweiterung der Sozialcharaktertheorie der Frankfurter Schule erklärt werden kann. Im Zentrum steht die Anfälligkeit von Individuen für faschistische Agitation im Zusammenspiel von Sozialcharakterstrukturen und agitatorischen Kommunikationsstrategien. Sozialcharakterstrukturen werden dabei als psychische Dispositionen bzw. Bedürfnisse gefasst, die durch sozioökonomische Bedingungen geprägt sind. Theoretisch rekonstruiert die Arbeit die Transformation vom autoritären hin zu einem narzisstisch-libertären Sozialcharakter, das gesteigerte Kränkungspotenzial spätmoderner Individuen sowie entsprechende Kompensationsmechanismen und daran anknüpfende Agitationsmethoden. Empirisch folgt die Studie einem qualitativen Forschungsdesign, das Netnography und strukturierende qualitative Inhaltsanalyse kombiniert. Am Fallbeispiel des Gesundheits-Influencers Fabian Kowallik wurden über einen Zeitraum von zwei Wochen Inhalte verschiedener Social-Media-Profile erhoben, dokumentiert und zusammen mit Nutzer:innen-Kommentaren analysiert. Letztere wurden zusätzlich mithilfe eines deduktiv-induktiv entwickelten Kategoriensystems auf Basis der Sozialcharaktertheorie mit MAXQDA ausgewertet. Die Analyse zeigt ein komplexes Zusammenspiel autoritärer und narzisstisch-libertärer Charakterstrukturen, wobei Mobilisierung im digitalen Raum insbesondere über affektive Bindung, Identifikation mit der Community sowie die Kompensation individueller Kränkungserfahrungen erfolgt. Influencer:innen fungieren dabei als sekundäre Agitator:innen, indem sie parasozial an narzisstische Kränkungspotenziale anschließen und durch emotionalisierende Agitationsmethoden – etwa Opfer-Märtyrer-Narrative, inszenierte Skandalisierungen sowie misstrauensbasierte und verschwörungsideologische Deutungsmuster – kollektiv-narzisstische Dynamiken fördern. Der innerhalb der jeweiligen Community ausgeprägte kollektive Narzissmus erweist sich im Kontext strategischer Netzwerkarbeit und algorithmischer Sichtbarkeitslogiken als anschlussfähig für neue Formen politischer Ein-flussnahme. Die Arbeit leistet damit einen Beitrag zur Weiterentwicklung der Autoritarismusforschung im Kontext sozialer Medien.

articles.title: Ananta
articles.authors: David Wittenburg
articles.abstract: This research establishes a forensic architecture to map the 140-year administrative gap left in American data structures following the discontinuation of the Federal Mortality Schedule. It introduces the technical baseline and structural metrics required for the federal enumeration and data classification of the uncounted Vilomah demographic. This document is cross-indexed with the Social Science Research Network (SSRN) under registry metadata.

articles.title: Public support for different CO2 pricing policies: A factorial survey experiment in the German Longitudinal Environmental Study (GLEN)
articles.authors: Felix Wolter; Katrin Auspurg; Andreas Diekmann
articles.abstract: This technical report presents the design and technical setup of a (multi-)factorial survey experiment (FSE) on support for CO2 pricing policies, which was implement-ed in an online Inter-wave survey of the German Longitudinal Environmental Study (GLEN) in 2025.

articles.title: The Monopoly and Monetization of Academic Publication Rights: Toward Fairness in Scholarly Publishing Rights
articles.authors: Jincheng Zhang
articles.abstract: With the highly commercialized development of the global academic publishing system, the right to publish papers has gradually evolved from a "knowledge dissemination mechanism" into a scarce resource controlled by a few publishing groups and platforms. In the current system, authors not only face high rejection rates and lengthy review processes during the submission stage, but also bear the dual pressure of high subscription fees and open access (OA) publication fees during the reading and dissemination stages. This structural asymmetry has led to the de facto "quasi-monopolization" and "monetization" of the right to publish papers. This paper analyzes the formation mechanism and economic logic of the monopoly of the right to publish papers, starting from the institutional structure of academic publishing, and further explores the practical dilemmas faced by preprint platforms, the open access movement, and new academic dissemination technologies. Finally, this paper proposes a reform path towards fairness, including decentralized review mechanisms, the reconstruction of the publicly funded publishing system and the academic evaluation system, in order to promote the evolution of the academic dissemination system towards a more open and equitable direction.

articles.title: Is Openness in Preprint Platforms Truly Open? A Critical Reflection on the Fairness of Academic Publication Rights
articles.authors: Jincheng Zhang
articles.abstract: Preprint platforms are widely regarded as an important mechanism for breaking down traditional academic publishing barriers and promoting the rapid dissemination of research results. However, beneath the surface of "open access," preprint systems still suffer from implicit screening mechanisms, differences in platform rules, and structural barriers caused by inequalities in resources and reputation. This paper analyzes the true boundaries of the "openness" of preprint platforms from the perspectives of their access mechanisms, review logic, academic power structures, and the allocation of publication rights, and further discusses the fairness of paper publication rights in the current academic dissemination system. The study argues that while preprint platforms reduce the time cost of publication in traditional journals, they have not completely eliminated publication barriers; on the contrary, they have, to some extent, created a new "soft censorship structure." Therefore, it is necessary to re-examine the governance mechanisms and academic evaluation systems of preprint platforms to promote a more equitable and transparent academic publication environment.

articles.title: Hidden Vulnerability and Heat-Triggered Decompensation: Rethinking Urban Overheating Beyond Clinical Risk
articles.authors: Mingyu Zhu; Qunshan Zhao; Jiayi Jin
articles.abstract: Urban overheating is increasingly recognised as a major public health challenge under climate change, yet existing frameworks remain heavily focused on acute medical risk and clinically vulnerable populations. This paper introduces \textit{hidden vulnerability} to describe a broader population who remain socially and economically active under ordinary conditions but experience progressive destabilisation during sustained indoor heat exposure. Drawing on a sensor-enhanced longitudinal housing survey in Southwark, London during the summer of 2023, combining indoor environmental monitoring with weekly health and wellbeing questionnaires, the study examines how overheating affects everyday urban life. The findings reveal that impacts extended well beyond thermal discomfort. Participants described cascading disruptions across physical, functional, and psychological domains, sleep loss, exhaustion, impaired self-care, reduced concentration, emotional strain, and increased caring burdens, that interacted and reinforced one another, progressively eroding the capacity to maintain ordinary routines. Critically, these processes were observed not only among participants with chronic conditions but also among those without formally recognised health vulnerabilities, suggesting that the threshold for destabilisation is lower, and more widely distributed, than existing frameworks assume. The paper argues that hidden vulnerability is an urban condition, produced by the intersection of housing quality, social infrastructure, and accumulated disadvantage. The response calls for a conceptual framework for redefining heat vulnerability beyond demographic and clinical categories, and an empirical foundation for a social cure approach to urban heat governance.

articles.title: Bucks for Bumps: How Paid Parental Leave Pays Off for Firms
articles.authors: Hillary Cookler
articles.abstract: Firms use benefits such as paid parental leave (PPL) to attract and retain employees. In the U.S., which lacks broad public PPL benefits, firms increasingly provide this benefit. Systematic evidence of its benefits remains limited, due in part to the absence of comprehensive data on firm policies. Using a novel, hand-collected database of firm policy changes, this paper estimates the causal effect of PPL expansion on employee retention using a differences-in-differences approach. PPL expansions reduce departure rates for both men and women by 3.5%. Retention gains for women are concentrated among the lowest paid and those in the most junior roles, while retention gains for men are broad-based. At the firm level, PPL expansions reduce departures most in firms with moderately flexible internal labor markets. These expansions also have stronger effects when bundled with other family-friendly benefits. Firm PPL expansions do not appear to yield differential retention effects in jurisdictions with public PPL benefits. Combining fertility estimates from the American Community Survey (ACS) with worker and firm data, the paper calculates the expected costs of PPL provision and compares them to retention gains: at realistic utilization rates, PPL expansions generate positive net returns to firms.

articles.title: Does social media explain young people’s support for Palestine?
articles.authors: Noah Carl
articles.abstract: Young people in Britain are much more supportive of Palestine than older people. Here I examine whether this difference can be explained by young people’s greater use of social media. Analysing data from the British Election Study collected in the summer of 2024, I find that the direct effect of social media use explains less than 30% of the gap between people aged 18–25 and those aged 66+. So while social media does seem to play a role, other factors or mechanisms are needed to explain most of the gap.

articles.title: Whose Knowledge Governs? Incumbent Actors, Transformative Networks, and the Politics of Knowledge in Indonesia's Sustainable Palm Oil Policy
articles.authors: Faris Salman
articles.abstract: Indonesia's Sustainable Palm Oil (ISPO) certification scheme has evolved across three regulatory iterations since 2011, yet certification coverage sits at only 39.8\% of 16.38 million hectares as of 2025, despite legal mandates in force across the study period. Existing research explains the structural features of ISPO's path dependency but leaves the actor-knowledge mechanism that drove selective reform underspecified. This paper asks how knowledge origins shaped the content and trajectory of ISPO policy formation across two morphogenetic junctures (2013--2016 and 2018--2020). We trace ISPO consultation records, policy documents, NGO position papers, and interviews with key actors, identifying two competing knowledge architectures. An incumbent architecture, controlled by the Ministry of Agriculture (MoA), GAPKI, and state research bodies, encoded path-dependent lock-in in the 2011 baseline. A transformative knowledge challenge, led by WWF Indonesia, KEHATI Foundation, Sawit Watch, TFT/Earthworm Foundation, and WRI Indonesia, introduced alternative evidence through international credibility channels. We apply the morphogenetic cycle to ISPO's three-iteration trajectory, identifying two temporal windows through which transformative knowledge was selectively incorporated. Each window required the same combination: external pressure that the government could not answer with its own knowledge resources, accumulated transformative knowledge credibility, and a government judgment that market costs of inaction exceeded the political costs of partial reform. The findings develop a mid-range theory of knowledge politics for sustainability governance. Knowledge origin shapes whose interests are encoded in a governance outcome. The network position of transformative actors conditions whether their knowledge reaches those critical moments when structural change becomes possible. A core limitations of this research is that interview evidence derives from civil society actors, government-side perspectives are inferred from documentary and accountability sources, and smallholder and community knowledge is analyzed as mediated through NGO representation, not from direct community interviews.

articles.title: Model Diversity Over Model Size: Unanimous LLM Ensembles Correct Over-Classification in Survey Coding
articles.authors: Chris Soria
articles.abstract: Large language models are increasingly used to classify open-ended survey responses, but they systematically over-classify, assigning categories too liberally on ambiguous cases and producing high sensitivity but low precision. This problem is most severe on subjectively ambiguous categories where models default to "yes" when uncertain. Drawing on the established principle that aggregating multiple noisy annotators outperforms any single annotator, we test whether ensembles of LLMs can correct this problem. Using four open-ended survey questions with human-coded ground truth (3,208 responses, 6 categories per question), we evaluate ensemble configurations across 16 models spanning three cost tiers and six providers. Unanimous voting (requiring all models to agree before assigning a category) directly corrects over-classification by dramatically improving specificity: on the most ambiguous categories, the false positive rate drops from 50% to 3%, and precision triples. This advantage concentrates precisely where over-classification is worst, on subjectively ambiguous categories with fuzzy boundaries, while categories with clear criteria show no benefit. This pattern replicates across three independent datasets. Cross-provider model diversity is the key ingredient: models from different providers make different errors on ambiguous cases, and consensus filters the idiosyncratic false positives. Temperature variation and within-family size scaling contribute nothing. As few as three diverse lower-tier models suffice to reliably exceed GPT-5. For the ambiguous classification problems common in open-ended survey research, the well-established annotation principle of multi-coder agreement transfers directly to LLMs: investing in diverse perspectives is more effective than investing in a single expensive model.

articles.title: Algorithmic Reality as a Psychological Ecology: Epistemic Destabilization, Significance Threat, and Regressive Meaning Making in Digital Environments
articles.authors: Mitchell Sanders
articles.abstract:
Human belief formation serves not only epistemic functions but also regulatory ones, helping individuals manage uncertainty, identity coherence, and affective distress (Conway & Pleydell-Pearce, 2000; Kunda, 1990). Contemporary digital environments increasingly mediate these processes through algorithmically curated information streams optimized for engagement rather than epistemic coherence (Praiser, 2011; Gillespie, 2018; Zuboff, 2019). This paper argues that such environments function as psychological ecologies that systematically destabilize epistemic conditions while amplifying motivational and affective pressures toward belief rigidity. Within these ecologies, epistemic destabilization is converted to motivational urgency, and rigid belief adoption functions as a regulatory solution rather than as an epistemic failure.
Drawing on cognitive psychology, moral psychology, and clinical science, the paper advances an amplification model in which chronic uncertainty, narrative saturation, moral salience, and algorithmic reinforcement interact with individual differences in curiosity, intolerance of uncertainty, and identity stability (Kruglanski, 2004; Carleton, 2016; Baird et al., 2012). Under such conditions, belief adoption serves a regulatory function by restoring coherence, significance, and moral orientation, even when epistemic accuracy is compromised. Algorithmic reinforcement dynamics such as repetition, attentional salience, processing fluency, and identity affirming feedback further consolidate these beliefs, increasing resistance to correction and revision (Hasher et al., 1977; Dechene et al., 2010; Pennycook & Rand, 2019).
The framework calls on Significance Quest Theory to explain why epistemic destabilization becomes motivationally urgent for some individuals, highlighting how algorithmic environments can both generate significance threat and supply readily available restoration pathways grounded in moralization, oppositional identity, and narrative certainty (Haidt, 2001; Kruglanski et al., 2014, 2022). These pathways may provide a subjective sense of autonomy while simultaneously constraining reflective agency, creating self reinforcing regulatory loops. Importantly, this work does not claim that digital environments directly cause psychopathology. Instead, it adopts an amplification perspective in which algorithmic contexts intensify latent vulnerabilities ranging from normative cognitive biases to belief rigidity and subclinical disturbance (Bentall et al., 2001; Vorontsova et al., 2013).
By conceptualizing algorithmic media as psychological ecologies, this paper offers a unifying theoretical account linking digital reinforcement structures to belief rigidity, identity fixation, and epistemic closure. The framework delineates scope conditions, specifies underlying psychological mechanisms, and generates testable claims, providing a coherent basis for future empirical research and clinically informed preventative reflection.

articles.title: The Theme-Enablers Method for Identifying an Extremist Movement's Cause
articles.authors: Moorthy Muthuswamy
articles.abstract:
Political science lacks a replicable method for establishing causation in ideology-driven political 
movements because existing frameworks conflate a movement's cause with its dependent 
variables. The reproducible, falsifiable, and portable Theme-Enablers Framework solves this by 
identifying a movement's animating theme, its institutional enablers, and the agendas they 
propagate as separable, measurable variables. Applied to the Islamist movement using potential
outcomes identification, difference-in-differences estimation, and natural experiment 
comparisons, the regression analysis of 20 lands yields R² values of 0.85, 0.86, 0.83, and 0.82. 
The designs converge on a finding: religious leaders are leveraging the Sharia platform to 
radicalize communities and consolidate political power.

articles.title: The salience and focality of normative expectations
articles.authors: Francesco Bogliacino; Diego Aycinena
articles.abstract:
We elicit (incentive-compatible) normative expectations in a vignette study employing the conventional four-item scale to measure the degree of social appropriateness. The participants are randomly assigned to one of four treatments: the coordination game elicitation, an adapted two-step method in which subjects report personal normative beliefs and are incentivized to guess those of others, a simplified version of the second treatment where participants engage only in the belief-guessing task, and a pure coordination game without framing on social norms in the instructions. The experiment informs our understanding of different methods and clarifies the degree of salience of social norms.

In naturally occurring vignettes, we detect no differences across the four treatments, neither for the average nor for the dispersion.

articles.title: TOWARD MORPHOLOGICALLY-AWARE TOKENIZATION FOR INFLECTIONAL LANGUAGES: A STRUCTURAL HYPOTHESIS
articles.authors: Elżbieta Dawidek
articles.abstract:
This article proposes a theoretical hypothesis concerning the structural and temporal architecture of tokenization in inflectional languages. The starting point is an observation documented in prior empirical work (Dawidek, 2026a; 2026b): standard frequency-based tokenization systematically fails to preserve morphologically significant boundaries in Polish, a synthetic inflectional language. The present article argues that this failure has a deeper cause than training data composition or algorithm choice. Standard tokenization has no preconditions — it begins cutting without structural knowledge of the word — and no concept of time: it treats the interval between cuts as empty latency rather than as a potential carrier of morphological information.
I propose two interconnected hypotheses. First, that linguistically motivated tokenization requires a morphological skeleton as a precondition — a minimal representation of root, prefix, ending type, case, number, gender, and valency, specified here for Polish and hypothesized to generalize to other inflectional languages. Second, that the cutting order itself is morphologically motivated and temporally structured: prefix first, inflectional ending second, root exposed last — where exposure is not a cut but a revelation of what was present throughout.
I further propose the concept of morphological resolution time — the interval between cuts understood as a site of grammatical resolution rather than computational waste. This proposal has architectural consequences: the temporal dimension required by the hypothesis is structurally incompatible with standard transformer parallelism. A hybrid architecture combining sequential morphological pre-processing with parallel downstream processing is identified as a research direction.
This article presents a hypothesis, not demonstrated results. It is offered as a theoretical framework for empirical work that has not yet been done — and as a named set of variables that must exist before measurement can begin.

articles.title: Audience and Proximity in News Coverage of Black Protests About Police
articles.authors: Pamela Elaine Oliver; Chaeyoon Lim; Anna Milewski; Erin Gaede
articles.abstract: Studies of protest often rely on news sources but less often consider variations between sources or the selection or description biases in sources. We use data about 1,059 Black protests about police in the U.S. between 1994 and 2010 identified in three newswire services and 33 Black newspapers to assess variation in event selection and descriptions along two dimensions: primary intended audience (“mainstream” versus Black Americans) and proximity (local versus nonlocal coverage). The largest difference found is nonlocal mainstream emphasis on protester violence in both event selection and description. Other differences are mainstream selection for larger events and bigger cities and mainstream emphasis on police and official perspectives versus Black news emphasis on systemic bias and collective action. We discuss the implications for both racialized collective memories and social movement research.

articles.title: Sexual behaviour, health, and health service accessibility during the first wave of the COVID-19 pandemic: A scoping review
articles.authors: Cassandra Freitas; Joseph Friedman-Burley; Alexa Yakubovich; Dionne Gesink
articles.abstract: Background: Despite an upsurge in COVID-19 research and commentary early in the pandemic, the impact of restrictions on sexual health services, sexual behaviour, and sexual health outcomes was largely unknown. Objective: We reviewed emerging topics and research gaps surrounding sexual health during early stages of the pandemic and compared and integrated preliminary empirical evidence with nonempirical commentary. Methods: We searched MEDLINE, PsycInfo, and Scopus for English-language articles that measured or discussed the impacts of COVID-19 on sexual behaviour, sexual health, and sexual health services during the first four months of the pandemic (March-June 2020). We qualitatively analyzed extracted data. Categories were used to frame a narrative synthesis and comparative interpretation of empirical research and nonempirical commentary. Results: Of the 3,647 unique articles retrieved from the literature search, 97 were included. Only 32 articles (33%) presented empirical data. Most empirical articles were assigned ‘very poor’ or ‘poor’ quality. We found considerable gaps in the quantity and quality of evidence on sexual health reported during early COVID-19, with non-empirical conjecture dominating the literature. We also identified gaps between empirical and nonempirical articles, suggesting that the issues and concerns being discussed at the start of the pandemic were not being sufficiently or rigorously studied.
