Conclusions: The field should consider establishing infrastructure for researchers to communicate, coordinate, collaborate, and carry out high-priority and high-quality research during rapidly d/evolving public health emergencies. Equally important are systems to evaluate and synthesize emerging evidence for use by public health policymakers and practitioners.

articles.title: Orchestrating the Twin Transition in Multinational Corporations: Technology Roadmapping for Green and Digital Global Business Services
articles.authors: Han-Teng Liao; Karen Ang
articles.abstract: Global Business Services (GBS) have emerged as a "living laboratory" for the Twin Transition of Green and Digital Transformation, as multinational corporations (MNCs) face increasing pressure to harmonize digital efficiency with environmental stewardship. Aiming to derive a socio-technical framework, this paper synthesizes Technology Roadmapping (TRM) with the International Telecommunication Union (ITU) ICT-centric innovation ecosystem toolkit. A bibliometric analysis of research clusters reveals an evolutionary shift from basic process automation toward "Sustainable Intelligence," identifying the GBS unit as a central "operational airlock" that mediates between landscape pressures—such as the EU’s dual mandate and Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanisms—and niche innovations in AI-native workflows. The study further maps these clusters onto a stakeholder engagement canvas, highlighting how resilient "Middle Power" hubs in Poland, Portugal, and Malaysia are bypassing the middle-income trap to provide a "third way" for global value chains amidst a bifurcated geopolitical cloud. The results offer a data-driven design approach for leaders and entrepreneurial support networks to orchestrate talent and supply chain flows, thereby enriching the conceptual understanding of Industry 5.0 and the role of GBS as a primary mechanism for navigating a volatile, multipolar digital economy.

articles.title: Generative AI and Knowledge Gains Among Older Adults and People of Color: Between Opportunity and Inequality
articles.authors: Jihye Lee; Ryan Christopher Moore
articles.abstract: As large language models (LLMs) increasingly influence how people access information and acquire knowledge, it is crucial to understand whether they amplify or mitigate existing social inequalities. Building on the digital inequality and knowledge gap literatures, this study examines how older adults and people of color – groups historically underserved in information landscapes – gain knowledge from interacting with LLMs across diverse topics. Analyzing naturalistic dialogue logs of 925 U.S. adults’ interactions with ChatGPT about a daily consumer topic (organic foods) and contentious social issues (climate change, gun control), we show that LLM interactions substantially increase knowledge, but gains are uneven: older adults and Black and Hispanic participants exhibit smaller improvements than younger and White participants. While gaps remain, prompt elaboration – the degree to which participants’ prompts contain substantive informational content – attenuates these disparities. The findings offer novel evidence that extant structural inequalities shape how individuals use and benefit from AI-mediated information.

articles.title: Political News Personalization in Conversational AI: Experimental Evidence from Three Large Language Models
articles.authors: Zachary P Dickson; Miriam Sorace; Thomas Samuel Robinson
articles.abstract: As AI is increasingly integrated into everyday digital life, a growing share of users turn to AI chatbots for information about current events. We run a series of experiments to test whether three large language models (LLMs)---GPT-4o-mini, Grok, and DeepSeek Chat---tailor political content to implicit user signals embedded in conversation. Two computational experiments each employ a demographic arm---in which cues about gender, life stage, and occupation are established in conversation before a political request---and an ideological arm in which cues spanning left-wing to right-wing orientations precede the same request. Across 2,000+ trials per arm, demographic cues systematically predict variation in political news provision across all three models, with occupation and life stage as the strongest predictors. Ideological cues show little effect on historical event framing for GPT-4o-mini but produce substantial framing effects for Grok and DeepSeek. Ideological cues also strongly predict news provision across all three models. Search query analysis reveals that GPT-4o-mini and Grok additionally vary their retrieval queries based on user signals, adding an upstream layer of tailoring.

articles.title: Detecting Social Categories in Multilingual Newspaper Corpora
articles.authors: Selma Šarenkapa; Isabelle Guinaudeau; Elisa Deiss-Helbig; Mathieu Dubied; Raphael Heiberger; Theres Matthieß
articles.abstract: Social constructions of groups shape policy and representation, but measuring them at scale remains difficult. Existing approaches typically face a trade-off between the number of social categories and the number of time points they cover. This article introduces SOCCAT, a multilingual pipeline for detecting and classifying social categorizations in large text corpora. SOCCAT first filters large corpora for sentences containing social category mentions and then uses Natural Language Inference to classify these mentions into 57 specific categories across eight broader classes related to socio-economic position, labor market position, age and family status, identities, professions, social roles, deviance, and real estate ownership. We train and validate the pipeline on manually annotated French and German newspaper data from 1995 to 2023. The pipeline performs strongly across countries, outlets, and time periods. Applying SOCCAT to more than 9 million newspaper articles, we show that it produces substantively plausible patterns of category salience and temporal variation around major focusing events, including migration debates and the Covid-19 pandemic. By releasing the manually annotated data and trained models, SOCCAT provides ready-to-use infrastructure for constructing comparable category-specific corpora and for downstream analyses of framing, deservingness, representation, and feedback effects between public discourse and policy.

articles.title: Wasta as Informal Governance: A Componential and Institutional Theory for Emerging Market Economies
articles.authors: Yousif Abdelrahim
articles.abstract:
Wasta is a social network based on family, lineage, tribe, and extended family. It is used to secure jobs, contracts, promotions, and access to services that are otherwise difficult to obtain. The authors believe that Wasta is not simply a cultural practice or social network but an ingrained broader cognitive model and institutional framework that shapes how people evaluate justice, responsibility, & moral action. Wasta illustrates a cultural mode of mental reasoning in honor-based, relational, interdependent societies where cognition is holistic, and decision-making prioritizes network-based trust, obligation, & loyalty over impartial rules. Wasta in the Middle East and North Africa (MENA) region and other collectivist societies—in general understood by scholars as the use of personal relationships to secure advantages—remains insufficiently researched, despite its pervasive influence across formal organisational systems and governance in emerging markets. Existing research often treats the Wasta phenomenon as a fragmented concept, often combined with nepotism or favouritism, thereby limiting Wasta measurement, analytical clarity and conceptualization. This research study analysis develops the Componential and Institutional Wasta Theory (CIWT), a multidimensional framework that conceptualizes Wasta as an informal governance mechanism ingrained in institutional and socio-cultural dynamics. Drawing on qualitative evidence from observations, focus groups, interviews, and prior literature, the examination identifies five critical core elements (i.e., factors or roots) that structure Wasta practices and illustrates their differential influence on organisational performance and outcomes, including creativity, innovation, corruption, institutional trust, and employee well-being. The proposed Wasta theoretical framework extends prior studies and advances the literature in five ways: (1) the theoretical framework provides researchers with a unified and agreed-on theoretical definition of Wasta, (2) it explains how institutions create dependence on informal systems (Wasta), and (3) it explains why cognition makes informal systems socially legitimate and self-reinforcing. (4) It presents a measurable componential structure promoting future empirical analysis, and (5) it links informal Wasta practices to formal organisational or institutional performance. By explaining, describing, and clarifying how Wasta functions across formal and informal business and social contexts, the newly developed theory and its theoretical framework offer actionable insights for policymakers, business leaders, and other NGO organizations striving to mitigate bribery, inequality, and corruption and reinforce governance in emerging market economies.
Keywords: Wasta; Informal Governance Mechanisms; Institutional Dynamics; Socio-cultural Dynamics; Wasta Theories; Cognition Theories.

articles.title: TOWARD MORPHOLOGICALLY-AWARE TOKENIZATION FOR INFLECTIONAL LANGUAGES: A STRUCTURAL HYPOTHESIS
articles.authors: Elżbieta Dawidek
articles.abstract:
This article proposes a theoretical hypothesis concerning the structural and temporal architecture of tokenization in inflectional languages. The starting point is an observation documented in prior empirical work (Dawidek, 2026a; 2026b): standard frequency-based tokenization systematically fails to preserve morphologically significant boundaries in Polish, a synthetic inflectional language. The present article argues that this failure has a deeper cause than training data composition or algorithm choice. Standard tokenization has no preconditions — it begins cutting without structural knowledge of the word — and no concept of time: it treats the interval between cuts as empty latency rather than as a potential carrier of morphological information.
I propose two interconnected hypotheses. First, that linguistically motivated tokenization requires a morphological skeleton as a precondition — a minimal representation of root, prefix, ending type, case, number, gender, and valency, specified here for Polish and hypothesized to generalize to other inflectional languages. Second, that the cutting order itself is morphologically motivated and temporally structured: prefix first, inflectional ending second, root exposed last — where exposure is not a cut but a revelation of what was present throughout.
I further propose the concept of morphological resolution time — the interval between cuts understood as a site of grammatical resolution rather than computational waste. This proposal has architectural consequences: the temporal dimension required by the hypothesis is structurally incompatible with standard transformer parallelism. A hybrid architecture combining sequential morphological pre-processing with parallel downstream processing is identified as a research direction.
This article presents a hypothesis, not demonstrated results. It is offered as a theoretical framework for empirical work that has not yet been done — and as a named set of variables that must exist before measurement can begin.

articles.title: Inflectional Paradigms as a Diagnostic Tool for Tokenizers in Morphologically Rich Languages: A Proposal for a Linguistic Benchmark
articles.authors: Elżbieta Dawidek
articles.abstract:
This article addresses the problem of evaluating the morphological quality of tokenizers used in natural language processing models for synthetic languages. The starting point is the observation that the standard measure of fertility ratio — the mean number of tokens per inflected form — captures the problem of fragmentation quantitatively but does not diagnose its morphological structure. A tokenizer achieving a low fertility ratio may nonetheless systematically cut morpheme boundaries at grammatically significant points, destroying information about the lexical stem, inflectional suffixes, or derivational prefixes.
Based on empirical analysis of four tokenizers with different architectures (BPE, WordPiece, Unigram LM) and training corpus compositions (monolingual Polish, monolingual English, multilingual), conducted on 109 Polish inflected forms across full paradigms of 10 lexemes, this article proposes a linguistic benchmark based on three metrics: stem integrity (SI), inflectional suffix separation (ISS), and mean fragmentation per lexeme (MFL). Results show a 3.2x ratio between the monolingual Polish tokenizer (HerBERT BPE-PL, MFL = 1.22) and the monolingual English tokenizer (GPT-2 BPE-EN, MFL = 3.91), and indicate that tokenizer architecture has independent explanatory power: XLM-R Unigram (MFL = 1.66) with a multilingual training corpus approaches the monolingual tokenizer more closely than mBERT WordPiece (MFL = 2.45) with comparable data composition.
The proposed benchmark is designed as minimal yet sufficient: aspect and conditional mood do not require separate diagnostic categories, because their behavior follows structurally from the behavior of the prefix and suffix that the benchmark measures directly. Inferring these categories from a correctly tokenized form is a task for the language model, not the tokenizer. This article continues research published in the preprint Dawidek (2026a) and constitutes a proof of concept for an evaluation tool that fills the gap between performance-based and linguistic assessment of tokenizers in morphologically rich languages.

articles.title: From Free Access to Winner-Takes-All - A Reactive Sequence Perspective on Digital Platform Capitalism
articles.authors: Jürgen Beyer
articles.abstract: This paper reinterprets the emergence of the digital winner-takes-all economy through Mahoney’s concept of the reactive sequence. Rather than treating digital concentration as the natural result of network effects, scale economies, or technological lock-in, it argues that these mechanisms became decisive only within a historically specific institutional trajectory. The early internet was constituted as a non-commercial sphere shaped by gift exchange, hacker ethics, open sharing, and community-oriented practices. As these norms diffused into mass usage, they were simplified into low willingness to pay and expectations of free access. Digital firms reacted by monetizing other market sides, especially advertisers, sellers, and platform-dependent businesses. This adaptation intensified network effects, weakened price differentiation, and privileged market leaders. Over time, observed concentration became generalized into a winner-takes-all expectation and institutionalized through venture-capital strategies, rapid scaling norms, and founder self-understandings. The essay extends this sequence to contemporary AI firms and their emerging wealth elites.

articles.title: The Limits of BPE Tokenization in Polish: Segmentation-Flexional Forms, Grammatical Anchoring, and First-Person Stability in Inflectional Language Models
articles.authors: Elżbieta Dawidek
articles.abstract:
This article analyzes BPE tokenization in Polish as a test case for a broader problem: the limits of statistical segmentation in an inflectional language. The starting point is the question of whether, and to what extent, frequency-based tokenization preserves units that are relevant for the linguistic description of Polish: orthographic form, the segmentation-phonemic level, syllable structure, logotomes, prefixes, derivational bases, inflectional endings, grammatical form, and the position of the speaking subject.
The empirical material includes the tokenization of diagnostic words, a simple children’s text, selected forms from the Preamble to the Constitution of the Republic of Poland, word-family tests, and examples containing Polish diacritics and nasal vowels. The analysis shows that BPE tokenizers may locally produce segments that coincide with syllabic, logotomic, or morphologically interpretable divisions, but they remain fundamentally dependent on the frequency of written forms. They do not systematically move from orthographic representation to the segmentation-phonemic level or to context-dependent phonetic realization.
The results also indicate that BPE stabilizes not grammatical categories themselves, but frequent surface fragments of their exponents. Segments such as -y, -ich, -ni, or -ć may appear as stable tokens or parts of tokens, but this does not amount to preserving the full grammatical form. A form such as ustanawiamy is not merely a sequence ending in -y; it is a verbal form anchored in conjugation, person, number, tense, mood, and aspect.
The article develops the concept of grammatical form anchoring, arguing that in Polish a form may not only encode syntactic relations, but also establish the position of the speaking subject. In forms such as poszłam, zrobiłam, or byłam, the grammatical “I” need not be expressed by a separate pronoun, because it is anchored in inflection. In interaction with AI, this reveals an additional problem: a language model does not possess its own stable grammatical “I”, but reconstructs it contextually, often mirroring the user’s forms or shifting grammatical gender.
I propose treating Rocławski’s segmentation-flexional forms not as a description of how BPE operates, but as a diagnostic framework for assessing the quality of tokenization boundaries. The findings lead to the research hypothesis that more stable modeling of Polish may require several layers: sublexical stabilization inspired by Rocławski’s system, anchoring grammatical form in the inflectional system, representing sentence patterns and verbal valency structure, and maintaining the position of the grammatical “I” in dialogue. From this perspective, the problem of Polish AI is not reducible to reducing the number of tokens. Rather, it concerns maintaining relations between orthographic representation, word form, grammatical function, sentence position, and the position of the speaking subject.

articles.title: Rethinking Development: From Growth to Livelihoodbility
articles.authors: Laud Kpakpo Addo
articles.abstract:
Few would dispute the importance of development, however it is defined or interpreted. It speaks not only to policy or academic inquiry, but to a broader civilisational aspiration: the enduring human effort to improve the material, social, and moral conditions of life across generations. Precisely because of this centrality, development has become one of the most prominent ideas in human thought and practice. Yet it is also among the most contested, functioning variously as aspiration, ideology, analytical framework, policy objective, and moral project. While everyday discourse often assumes a shared understanding, scholarly engagement reveals a concept marked by plurality, tension, and continual reinterpretation.

Drawing on extensive desk research, qualitative survey evidence, and a case study, this volume brings together diverse strands of development thinking into a coherent analytical whole. Building on insights into shifting meanings of development and evolving development narratives, it introduces the concept of “livelihoodbility” (derived from livelihoods and liveability), advancing a working definition of development that seeks to bridge normative aspiration with analytical measurability. It further organises development thinking into three intersecting axes, expressed through the “development cube,” an orthogonal framework that functions both as a conceptual model and a practical tool. This framework allows development to be examined across contexts, disciplines, and epistemic traditions, and offers potential application in the empirical assessment of policies, programmes, and outcomes.

Anchored in a China-focused case study, the work argues against privileging any single theoretical lens. Instead, it calls for the strategic integration of multiple perspectives in designing context-sensitive policies and programmes.

Finally, the study raises difficult but necessary questions about humanity’s broader developmental trajectory and the possibility of a civilisational impasse. These are not posed to suggest inevitability or decline, but to encourage critical reflection on the future of development. 
If development remains a central human aspiration, its future depends not on rhetorical affirmation, but on intellectual clarity, ethical commitment, and collective yet differentiated responsibility.

articles.title: Social Closure in U.S. High Schools? Patterns and Determinants of Socioeconomic Segregation in Adolescent Friendship Networks
articles.authors: Benjamin Rosche
articles.abstract: Adolescent friendship networks exhibit limited interaction across socioeconomic and racial lines. Using Add Health data and a novel exponential random graph model, this study examines socioeconomic segregation in high school friendships and its relationship to racial segregation. Results show that networks are segregated less by socioeconomic status (SES) than by race, yet low-SES students are excluded from high-SES circles to a similar degree. Crucially, unlike racial segregation, which is mutual, socioeconomic segregation is unilateral: many ties from low-SES to high-SES peers go unreciprocated. A decomposition of determinants shows about 60 percent reflects differences in schools’ socioeconomic composition, while 40 percent arises from within-school friendship choices. Within schools, segregation arises less from SES-stratified courses and extracurriculars than from racial homophily, SES-based popularity differences, and triadic closure. Thus, while between-school compositional differences limit who can meet, within schools, segregation is shaped more by students’ preferences and network processes than by meeting opportunities.

articles.title: Bordering on Discontent: The Political Consequences of Border Liberalization
articles.authors: Jose Maycas-Sardi; Josep Serrano-Serrat
articles.abstract: In the aftermath of globalization, Western democracies have experienced rising political disaffection and increased support for radical-right parties. While prior research has extensively examined the political consequences of immigration, the effects of policies that ease cross-border mobility remain comparatively underexplored. Building on political geography and social psychology, this paper theorizes and tests how greater border permeability can generate political discontent even in the absence of heightened immigration or meaningful economic effects. Crucially, the political consequences of such discontent depend on the availability of political supply. In the absence of radical-right entrepreneurs, we expect political disengagement, reflected in lower turnout and declining support for mainstream parties. When a viable radical-right party is present, however, symbolic discontent is more likely to be politically mobilized. We test these arguments in the context of the German--Czech border, exploiting the Czech Republic's accession to the European Union in 2004 --- which eased cross-border mobility without increasing settlement rights --- and the programmatic shift of the AfD in 2015. Using difference-in-differences and event-study designs with German electoral data, we find evidence consistent with the proposed theory. These findings highlight the importance of better understanding how bordering liberalization shapes political reactions.

articles.title: Does social media explain young people’s support for Palestine?
articles.authors: Noah Carl
articles.abstract: Young people in Britain are much more supportive of Palestine than older people. Here I examine whether this difference can be explained by young people’s greater use of social media. Analysing data from the British Election Study collected in the summer of 2024, I find that the direct effect of social media use explains less than 30% of the gap between people aged 18–25 and those aged 66+. So while social media does seem to play a role, other factors or mechanisms are needed to explain most of the gap.

articles.title: The Hiring Value of Digital Micro-Credentials. Evidence from a Discrete Choice Experiment in Germany
articles.authors: Martin Ehlert; Benjamin Schimke
articles.abstract: Micro-credentials such as online certificates or digital badges have been promoted as flexible tools to enhance employability, especially given the growing demand for continuing education and training. Yet, little is known about how employers value such credentials in hiring decisions and whether they function as complements or substitutes for other qualification signals. Drawing on signaling and human capital theory as well as the digital divide literature, this study examines the hiring value of digital and in-person micro-credentials relative to formal qualifications and work experience. The empirical analysis is based on a pre-registered discrete choice experiment conducted with 1,380 human resource professionals in Germany, who evaluated 12,048 applicant profiles across 24 occupations requiring higher education. Results based on conditional logit models show that digital micro-credentials do not increase hiring probabilities and are valued significantly less than equivalent certificates obtained through in-person courses. This difference is independent of recruiters’ prior experience with digital micro-credentials and largely driven by trust in the signal quality of the two credential formats. We also find that in-person micro-credentials issued by universities improve hiring chances compared to other providers. Furthermore, among applicants with weaker field-of-study matches, in-person micro-credentials can enhance employability, indicating a partially substitutional signaling function, while this is not the case for digital micro-credentials. These findings suggest that in strongly institutionalized labor markets such as Germany, employers continue to prioritize established (micro-)qualifications over emerging digital forms. At the same time, this is evidence against a digital divide in terms of outcomes.

articles.title: L’IA dans les élections municipales 2026 : Des usages qui appellent un regard nuancé
articles.authors: Antoine Marie
articles.abstract:
The municipal elections of March 15 and 22, 2026 constitute the first
French local election in which generative artificial intelligence has
massively entered campaign practices. This research brief offers a
balanced analysis of documented uses, distinguishing legitimate
applications (poster generation, programmatic chatbots, content
synthesis) from confirmed abuses (defamatory deepfakes, misleading
images, astroturfing). While these new technological capabilities raise
legitimate concerns for the integrity of democratic debate, we argue that
their impact must be put in perspective in light of the literature on the
limited effects of mass political communication and the actual
determinants of disinformation consumption. Rather than a
catastrophism that risks fueling generalized distrust, we advocate for an
approach grounded in media literacy and the strengthening of
professional journalism.

articles.title: A Machine Learning Method for Detecting Cultural Patterns in Ethnographic Texts: A Demonstration on ‘Offenses Against Life’
articles.authors: Maxine Lelasseux; Marco Spruit; Kristen Syme
articles.abstract:
Despite the increasing availability of large, structured ethnographic datasets such as the Human
Relations Area Files (HRAF), machine learning (ML) methods remain underutilized in crosscultural
research. This paper presents a methodological demonstration of how supervised ML can
be used to detect cultural patterns in ethnographic data coded with the Outline of Cultural Materials
(OCM). Using ’Offenses Against Life’ (OCM 682) as a case study, we applied classification models
to predict OCM code co-occurrences associated with four target variables: 627 (Informal Ingroup
Justice), 578 (Ingroup Antagonisms), 628 (Intercommunity Relations), and 754 (Sorcery).
Although model performance was modest, feature importance and decision tree diagrams revealed
that predictive OCM codes were thematically aligned with the target codes, suggesting that ML
can identify meaningful associative patterns of cultural domains as represented in ethnographic
annotations. This approach enables replicable exploration of coded ethnographic data, and serves
as a computational tool for testing and generating testable hypotheses about the patterning of
institutions, norms, and behaviours across cultures.

articles.title: Transcontinental Diffusion of a Vernacular Team Combat Game: From Japanese S-ken Through Squid Game to Eastern European “Koreyka”
articles.authors: Maxim Osovsky; Aleksei Shirshov; Alexey Ovodov
articles.abstract: "Squid Game" (2021) drew a global audience to "Ojing-eo Nori" (오징어 놀이), a Korean children's street game — yet the wider family of vernacular contact games to which it belongs has remained almost undescribed in scholarship and unexamined as a candidate for intangible cultural heritage. This family is defined by three traits: a maze field drawn in chalk (or scratched on bare ground), two-sided push/pull combat, and instant elimination when a player steps on a line. Despite a spread across at least ten countries, it has never been treated as a single phenomenon in academic literature, and no existing folk-game taxonomy provides a dedicated category for its hybrid morphotype. Using digital ethnography (netnography) and comparative morphological analysis across 95 cross-cultural sources in 8 languages (Japanese, Korean, Russian, Romanian, Bulgarian, Polish, English, French), we present the first reconstruction of its transcultural diffusion route — from the Japanese "S-ken" (Sケン) through the Korean "Ojing-eo Nori" and the Central Asian "Tim" to the Russian "Koreyka", Romanian "Frunza", Bulgarian "Kŭr", and Polish "Manzo". Five independent lines of evidence — phonetic (Manse → Manso → Myaso), zoosemantic (squid → mollusk), safe-zone architecture (islands → pockets), kinetic continuity (one-legged hopping), and capture-ritual identity — together favour a single origin over convergent evolution. We document 21 regional names and reconstruct 7 structural field modifications, establishing this family as a rare case of transcontinental diffusion of a complex contact street practice, with traceable preservation of both a structural and a linguistic genome.

articles.title: Rails, Risks, and Resilience: A Dynamic Spatial Equilibrium Analysis with Catastrophic Natural Disaster Risk
articles.authors: Daisuke Adachi
articles.abstract: We develop a dynamic stochastic spatial model in which catastrophic disasters temporarily reduce regional productivity and disrupt the transportation network, applied to Japan's Tokai Trough Earthquake (TTE) risk and the Chuo Shinkansen (CS) maglev train project. Productivity-recovery and network-disruption are estimated on the 2011 Tohoku Earthquake, and the model qualitatively reproduces the observed slow population decline and wage overshoot in the affected regions. The population-weighted welfare cost of TTE risk is 4.96\% of permanent consumption, of which 97\% flows through the migration option-value channel, representing a spatial-spillover mechanism where even far-epicenter zones lose welfare because their fallback destinations also become riskier. As the CS plays a role as insurance against transport-network disruption, it delivers welfare gains of 0.83\% under TTE risk versus 0.63\% in the risk-free benchmark, resulting in a 0.19 percentage-point redundancy premium. Overall, our framework extends dynamic spatial analysis beyond perfect-foresight counterfactuals to reveal spatial welfare spillovers and a redundancy value of infrastructure under catastrophic risk.

articles.title: From Climate Crisis to Fascism? An Empirical Examination of Ecofascism Susceptibility in the UK
articles.authors: Viktoria Spaiser; Alex Beresford
articles.abstract:
Could the acceleration of the climate crisis create an opening for ecofascist politics? In this study we
refine theoretically our understanding of ecofascism, going beyond the focus on immigration and
ecobordering by acknowledging the inherently offensive, expansionist logic of fascism represented in
the Volk, Kampf, Lebensraum triad. We then use representative YouGov survey experiment data from
1,967 UK residents to examine how susceptible the electorate is to ecofascist ideas and whether
exposure to climate change threat makes them more susceptible. Findings suggest that approximately
one-fifth of the UK population could be susceptible to an ecofascist ideology rooted in Volk, Kampf and
Lebensraum. A sense of climate despair and anomie stemming from climate change threats, combined
with predispositions toward social dominance and authoritarianism, and a conflicted relationship with
nature, increases susceptibility to ecofascism. We conclude by proposing future research avenues to
deepen our understanding of the phenomenon, including beyond the UK.

articles.title: IranWar.ai: An Open-Source Event-Level Dataset of the 2026 US–Iran Conflict
articles.authors: Jacob E. Thomas; Aigerim Alpysbekova; Eugene Osei Mensah; Nigel Masara; Prakhar Sharma
articles.abstract: We present the IranWar.ai Event-Level Research Dataset, a structured, openly available record of the first 30 days of the 2026 US–Iran conflict (Operation Epic Fury, February 28–March 29, 2026). The dataset comprises 1,653 event-level observations across 48 variables, derived from 16 curated JSON data files that power the IranWar.ai public intelligence dashboard. Each observation represents a discrete, verifiable event: an airstrike on a named target, a retaliatory attack, a financial market data point, a daily casualty estimate by faction, a naval deployment, or a diplomatic development. Nine analytical domains are covered (military strikes, retaliations, financial markets, humanitarian impacts, diplomatic events, naval operations, cyber activity, general military, and contextual data) integrating open-source intelligence (OSINT) from more than 60 specific source streams, including CENTCOM, the IDF, ACLED, the Iranian Red Crescent, the IAEA, Bloomberg, and the IMO. Beyond its function as a research resource, this paper examines the dataset as an epistemic object. Assembled in part by AI research agents and carrying known imperfections, the dataset is released with full transparency about its own limitations, including interpolated values, unverified records, and probabilistic confidence ratings. We argue that this transparency constitutes a deliberate counter-technology: a structural intervention against the conditions of the information flood, which we frame as a dual-layer epistemic crisis comprising both a structural component (the volume, velocity, and synthetic origin of available data overwhelming the infrastructure available to evaluate it) and a strategic component (the deliberate production of disinformation by state and non-state actors). Radical transparency is necessary for both layers but, by itself, is sufficient only against the structural layer; the strategic layer additionally requires interpretive expertise that situates discrepancies as political facts rather than data-quality defects. We further examine the dataset's relationship to AI training pipelines, model collapse dynamics, and the degradation of provenance metadata across summarization and citation chains. To address the limits of any single research team's expertise, we set out a tentative, non-exhaustive research agenda and invite collaborators across political science, public health, economics, military studies, media studies, and information science. The live dashboard, codebook, source data, prompts, manifests, reproducible extraction script, and versioned research releases are publicly available at github.com/jethomasphd/WarTheater; scheduled research releases are monthly, with interim correction releases when warranted.

articles.title: Mobilidade educacional intergeracional e raça no Brasil: uma abordagem posicional de longa duração
articles.authors: Carlos Costa Ribeiro
articles.abstract: This article examines racial disparities in intergenerational educational mobility in Brazil among individuals who were part of the educational system over the last 100 years, between 1924 and 2024, and who were born between 1918 and 2002. Using data from the 1982 and 2014 PNAD and the 2016, 2019, and 2024 PNAD Contínua, we adopt a positional approach that measures parents’ and children’s education in terms of cohort-specific percentile ranks rather than absolute years of schooling. To address the discrete nature of educational attainment, we estimate kernel-smoothed joint distributions of parental and offspring education and calculate upward and downward transition probabilities by race. This strategy allows us to compare racial inequalities in educational mobility over a long historical period marked by profound educational expansion, including the diffusion of basic schooling, the massification of secondary and higher education, and recent affirmative action policies. The results show that Whites consistently enjoyed greater chances of upward mobility and lower risks of downward mobility than non-Whites. Racial gaps in upward mobility narrowed across intermediate and recent cohorts, especially among those originating at the bottom of the distribution, but remained far from eliminated. By contrast, racial inequalities in downward mobility proved more persistent and robust over time. Analyses by sex indicate that the recent convergence in downward mobility is explained mainly by the advances made by non-White women. Taken together, educational expansion reduced, but did not overcome, racial inequality in intergenerational mobility in Brazil.

articles.title: Addressing Rural Unemployment Disparities in Oregon: A Spatial Analysis and Multi-Criteria Policy Evaluation
articles.authors: Styles Carnley
articles.abstract: Oregon's unemployment rate of 5.2% in February 2026 exceeds the national average of 4.4% and has diverged from national trends since June 2024. County-level Bureau of Labor Statistics data reveals a 4.5 percentage point spread across Oregon's 36 counties, with the highest rates concentrated in rural eastern and southern counties. This analysis uses ArcGIS Online spatial visualization and a Weighted Sum Model Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis to evaluate three workforce policy alternatives. Remote-friendly small business incentives received the highest weighted score (4.05), supported by SBA Office of Advocacy data showing small businesses employ 54.3% of rural workers nationally. This analysis recommends Oregon establish a Rural Remote-Friendly Small Business Incentive Program targeting residents of high-unemployment rural counties starting or expanding businesses that can operate online or serve clients remotely.

articles.title: Designing for the Threshold: Relational Outcomes and Inclusive Scaffolding in Higher Education Inductions — The FRIEND Feasibility Study
articles.authors: Vince Jacques de Blauwe; Antonio Fidalgo
articles.abstract: UK student loneliness has become a persistent concern in higher education, especially around induction and early transition points. Higher education institutions increasingly use brief relational primers to support connection, yet such activities are rarely compared head-to-head in pre-registered feasibility trials and are even less often evaluated against explicit inclusive-design safeguards for anxious or neurodivergent students. FRIEND is a pre-registered, CONSORT-aligned cluster-feasibility randomised controlled trial comparing a 15-minute Nonviolent Communication primer with a duration-matched mindfulness body-scan control in 30 students organised into 15 stranger dyads. All four a priori progression criteria were met: retention 100%, protocol adherence 100%, adverse events 0, and a directional primary-outcome signal on dyadic closeness. Across the three convergent analytic pathways, the NVC primer showed a consistent directional advantage over the mindfulness control on IOS change scores. The Mann–Whitney test yielded U = 160.5, p = .041, rrb = .43, indicating a medium effect in favour of NVC. The random-intercept mixed model estimated b = 1.06, 95% CI [0.13, 1.99], p = .025, after accounting for dyadic clustering. A BCa bootstrap with R = 10,000 (seed 20260425) returned rrb = .43 with a 95% CI of [−0.74, +0.005]. Taken together, these estimates are consistent with a directional mechanism signal of feasibility-grade magnitude rather than definitive evidence of effectiveness. Descriptively, the scaffold-usage pattern was compatible with a calm–connection divergence, in which procedural calm and relational closeness did not map perfectly onto the same condition. The study provides a transparent template for testing relational primers and perceived-safety scaffolding in induction, peer-support, and buddy-programme settings.

articles.title: A real-effort task with endogenous work environment
articles.authors: Pablo Branas-Garza; Antonio M. Durán-Rosal; Filippos Exadaktylos; ADRIANA GAVIRIA; Cesar Mantilla; Carlos Isaías Ramos
articles.abstract: Experimental economics studies using real-effort offer a better match with the field environment than stated-effort studies, incorporating the psychological aspects affecting effort provision. However, this literature does not analyze how subjects choose the environment in which they work but rather studies effort provision within an exogenously given setting. This paper introduces a new task in which the experimenter offers a menu of costly inputs levels that increase the chance of success, and the subject chooses the levels that define their working environment subject to a budget constraint. We present an application of this task when there is a shock to input prices.  We further release an open-access platform, AREPA (Allocation-based Real Effort Production App), which allows researchers to access and set up the task for teaching and research purposes.

articles.title: Exploring the use of funding acknowledgments for mapping global research funding flows
articles.authors: Rong Ni; Jeroen van Honk; Jonathan Dudek; Gabrielle Ding; Ismael Rafols; Rodrigo Costas
articles.abstract: Funding acknowledgments (FAs) provide a potential but underexplored source for mapping global research funding flows. This study develops a framework for constructing global funding flow measures using FA data from Dimensions and assesses both the opportunities and limitations of this approach. We compare total and inter-regional funding flows based on funding grant information available in FAs of scientific publications. Our analysis identifies a key limitation arising from collaborative publications, where funding is attributed at the publication level rather than to individual authors. This obscures the interpretation of cross-border funding flows. We show that this attribution structure introduces systematic ambiguity and limits the validity of FA-based analyses. We argue that disaggregated, author-level funding attribution is essential for accurately tracing funding flows. This requires coordinated action among funders, publishers, and researchers, as well as the adoption of more standardized, machine-readable FAs formats.

articles.title: In AI We Verify? Literacy, Privacy Concerns, and Task Importance in Verifying LLM- Generated Information
articles.authors: Jihye Lee
articles.abstract: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly integrated into everyday information-seeking and decision-making contexts, concerns have grown regarding their tendency to produce inaccurate or fabricated outputs, such as hallucinations. This study investigates factors associated with users’ intention to verify LLM-generated information, focusing on three domains: user competencies (digital literacy, information literacy, and AI literacy), sociotechnical attitudes (trust in AI and privacy concerns), and usage characteristics (frequency of use and perceived task importance). Analysis of a survey of U.S. adult LLM users shows that AI literacy is the strongest and most consistent predictor of verification intention: individuals with higher AI literacy report greater intention to verify LLM outputs, including cross-checking information, requesting supporting evidence, and reporting inaccurate information to LLM platforms. Digital literacy shows a positive but smaller association with verification intention, while information literacy is not significantly related. In addition, higher privacy concerns and greater perceived task importance are positively associated with verification intention, although these effects are smaller in magnitude compared to AI literacy. These findings provide valuable initial evidence about user-level factors that shape information verification behaviors in human-AI interaction, addressing one of the pressing concerns in increasingly AI-mediated information environments.

articles.title: Provocative Storytelling: Narrative, Interpretation, and the Problem of Meaning in Pedagogy
articles.authors: Randa Elbih
articles.abstract: Storytelling is widely used in education, but it is still most often treated as a tool for engagement, illustration, or representation rather than as a structure for thought. I theorize provocative storytelling as a pedagogical construct that positions narrative as a site of inquiry. Rather than delivering meaning, provocative stories are deliberately designed to generate interpretive tension, inviting learners into processes of questioning, perspective-taking, and dialogic reasoning. Drawing on narrative theory, dialogism, critical literacy, and inquiry-based learning, the article articulates the design principles that distinguish provocative storytelling from adjacent approaches such as case-based learning and dilemma pedagogy. It argues that contemporary pedagogical practices often privilege coherence, clarity, and transmission at the expense of interpretive engagement, limiting opportunities for students to grapple with complexity, ambiguity, and competing perspectives (Biesta, 2010). By framing narrative as an epistemic space rather than a rhetorical device, this article contributes a theoretically grounded approach to pedagogy that foregrounds meaning-making as a social, cultural, and interpretive process. The article concludes with implications for curriculum design, teacher education, and classroom practice, particularly in contexts that seek to engage learners in critical, interdisciplinary, and culturally responsive inquiry.

articles.title: Global research funding flows according to available grant data in Dimensions
articles.authors: Jonathan Dudek; Rong Ni; Jeroen van Honk; Gabrielle Ding; Nicandro Bovenzi; Nicolau Duran-Silva; Ismael Rafols; Rodrigo Costas
articles.abstract: While most research funding is national, international funding plays a relatively important role, particularly for some regions of the Global South. However, the extent of funding flows between world regions remains unclear. This study represents a first attempt to map global research funding flows based on a set of grant metadata from Dimensions, augmented with data from the Gates Foundation and the European Commission (CORDIS). We find major regional gaps in the data outside of the leading scientific regions. With the data currently available, we find that Europe and Sub-Saharan Africa are the biggest recipients of inter-regional funding, with North America as main funder, and that over the last 25 years, total annual research funding has grown to above USD 60 billion, while inter-regional funding flows have stagnated at around USD 1.6 billion per year. These insights are contingent on data availability, raising the urgency for improving funding metadata infrastructures.

articles.title: Criminal Order and Gendered Violence: Gang Control, State Repression, and Violence Against Women in Chicago
articles.authors: Igor Novaes Lins
articles.abstract: Women are increasingly the targets of gunfire in Chicago. Between 2010 and 2016, women made up roughly one in ten shooting victims. By 2022, the share had reached nearly one in six. Chicago's gang order fragmented after the late-1990s federal prosecutions, as super-gangs gave way to block-level cliques and the arrangements that had structured local violence eroded with them. That much is well established. I argue that this fragmentation reshaped who absorbs the resulting violence, and that women bear a rising share of it. Drawing on a panel of 77 community areas (2008–2024), Chicago Police Department crime and shooting records, and gang-territory maps, I show that what predicts violence against women in Chicago is not which gang controls a neighborhood. Folk Nation and People Nation territories record nearly identical female victimization rates. What predicts it is whether any stable territorial order exists at all. Where governance consolidates, reported domestic violence falls. Where multiple gangs compete, it rises. A difference-in-differences design covering the years after November 2015, bracketed by the Laquan McDonald dashcam release and the collapse of proactive policing, shows female shooting victims rising by about half and fatal shootings of women nearly doubling in high-gang community areas relative to low-gang ones. The rise cannot be pinned on the police pullback alone. The paper documents the pattern itself, a sustained and geographically concentrated rise in lethal violence against women that a literature trained on aggregate homicide has missed.

articles.title: Herramientas digitales para la participación del estudiantado universitario
articles.authors: Alejandro D. Hernández-Melián
articles.abstract: Las herramientas digitales interactivas favorecen la participación activa del alumnado y la evaluación compartida en distintos entornos educativos. En este proyecto se trata de promover la capacitación del profesorado universitario en competencias digitales mediante el uso de Mentimeter, así como el intercambio de experiencias docentes. Metodológicamente, la herramienta permite crear presentaciones interactivas con participación en tiempo real, facilitando una comunicación bidireccional y adaptable a diversos objetivos educativos mediante distintos tipos de preguntas. Como resultados, doce docentes elaboraron 150 presentaciones, generando 4033 interacciones y la participación de al menos 1350 estudiantes. Su uso en distintas fases de la docencia evidencia su versatilidad y potencial para mejorar el proceso de enseñanza-aprendizaje.

articles.title: From ideal experiments to ideal research designs: What they are and why they are helpful
articles.authors: Paul C. Bauer; Camille Landesvatter; Florian Keusch
articles.abstract: Ideal experiments (IEs) describe hypothetical studies that we would carry out without practical, ethical, or resource-related constraints. IEs can help us to identify pitfalls and deficiencies in causal research projects (e.g., Angrist and Pischke 2008). We contend that the focus of IEs is too narrow and introduce the more general notion of ideal research designs (IRDs). IRDs go beyond IEs in that they also speak to issues such as measurement errors that are rarely the focus of IEs. After reviewing how researchers define and advocate using IEs, we discuss what components IRDs should encompass drawing on well-known error frameworks. After an applied example that explains how IRDs can be used in research, we explore how empirical researchers are using IRDs. While we find few examples, they attest to the usefulness of IRDs to evaluate research designs that have been realized or are planned for the future.

articles.title: Occupying Autism: Structural Ableism and the Living Infrastructure of Pathology
articles.authors: Ericwilliam Brown
articles.abstract:
The history of autism scholarship is traditionally narrated as a sequence of progressive clinical discoveries. The argument advanced here proposes an alternative genealogical framework, tracing how the pathology paradigm reproduces itself independently of its scientific validity through mechanisms of institutional inheritance. Informed by epistemic injustice theory and the DisCrit framework, the analysis demonstrates how early bidirectional understandings of autistic traits were actively displaced by five historical nodes of clinical authority: Kanner, Bettelheim, Asperger, Lovaas, and Autism Speaks. To explain the durability of this deficit-based orientation across paradigmatic shifts, the analysis introduces a transmission model comprising three recursive structures: interpretive prefiguration, credential monopolization, and administrative sedimentation. Together, these mechanisms transitioned the Autistic community from a state of hermeneutical lacuna into a condition of hermeneutical domination. This theoretical model is subsequently applied to the contemporary United States federal health apparatus, documenting how 2025 and 2026 executive branch funding allocations, rhetorical strategies, and judicial rulings on compliance-oriented interventions function as convergent instruments of epistemic severance. Dismantling this living infrastructure ultimately requires accountable research governance that untethers federal grant eligibility from normative institutional affiliation and centers the intersectional knowledge produced by the Autistic community.


Keywords: critical autism studies, epistemic injustice, hermeneutical domination, DisCrit, federal health policy, structural ableism, Double Empathy Problem, neurodiversity

articles.title: The Limits of BPE Tokenization in Polish: Segmentation-Flexional Forms, Grammatical Anchoring, and First-Person Stability in Inflectional Language Models
articles.authors: Elżbieta Dawidek
articles.abstract:
This article analyzes BPE tokenization in Polish as a test case for a broader problem: the limits of statistical segmentation in an inflectional language. The starting point is the question of whether, and to what extent, frequency-based tokenization preserves units that are relevant for the linguistic description of Polish: orthographic form, the segmentation-phonemic level, syllable structure, logotomes, prefixes, derivational bases, inflectional endings, grammatical form, and the position of the speaking subject.
The empirical material includes the tokenization of diagnostic words, a simple children’s text, selected forms from the Preamble to the Constitution of the Republic of Poland, word-family tests, and examples containing Polish diacritics and nasal vowels. The analysis shows that BPE tokenizers may locally produce segments that coincide with syllabic, logotomic, or morphologically interpretable divisions, but they remain fundamentally dependent on the frequency of written forms. They do not systematically move from orthographic representation to the segmentation-phonemic level or to context-dependent phonetic realization.
The results also indicate that BPE stabilizes not grammatical categories themselves, but frequent surface fragments of their exponents. Segments such as -y, -ich, -ni, or -ć may appear as stable tokens or parts of tokens, but this does not amount to preserving the full grammatical form. A form such as ustanawiamy is not merely a sequence ending in -y; it is a verbal form anchored in conjugation, person, number, tense, mood, and aspect.
The article develops the concept of grammatical form anchoring, arguing that in Polish a form may not only encode syntactic relations, but also establish the position of the speaking subject. In forms such as poszłam, zrobiłam, or byłam, the grammatical “I” need not be expressed by a separate pronoun, because it is anchored in inflection. In interaction with AI, this reveals an additional problem: a language model does not possess its own stable grammatical “I”, but reconstructs it contextually, often mirroring the user’s forms or shifting grammatical gender.
I propose treating Rocławski’s segmentation-flexional forms not as a description of how BPE operates, but as a diagnostic framework for assessing the quality of tokenization boundaries. The findings lead to the research hypothesis that more stable modeling of Polish may require several layers: sublexical stabilization inspired by Rocławski’s system, anchoring grammatical form in the inflectional system, representing sentence patterns and verbal valency structure, and maintaining the position of the grammatical “I” in dialogue. From this perspective, the problem of Polish AI is not reducible to reducing the number of tokens. Rather, it concerns maintaining relations between orthographic representation, word form, grammatical function, sentence position, and the position of the speaking subject.

articles.title: From ideal experiments to ideal research designs: What they are and why they are helpful
articles.authors: Paul C. Bauer; Camille Landesvatter; Florian Keusch
articles.abstract: Ideal experiments (IEs) describe hypothetical studies that we would carry out without practical, ethical, or resource-related constraints. IEs can help us to identify pitfalls and deficiencies in causal research projects (e.g., Angrist and Pischke 2008). We contend that the focus of IEs is too narrow and introduce the more general notion of ideal research designs (IRDs). IRDs go beyond IEs in that they also speak to issues such as measurement errors that are rarely the focus of IEs. After reviewing how researchers define and advocate using IEs, we discuss what components IRDs should encompass drawing on well-known error frameworks. After an applied example that explains how IRDs can be used in research, we explore how empirical researchers are using IRDs. While we find few examples, they attest to the usefulness of IRDs to evaluate research designs that have been realized or are planned for the future.

articles.title: When Climate Inaction Shapes Climate Responsibility: Moral Disengagement, Psychological Distance, and Information Seeking in Malaysia
articles.authors: Zhi Xuan Leong; Shue Ling Chong; Matthew J Ashfold; Haliza Abdul Rahman; Eun Hee Lee
articles.abstract: Public engagement with climate change remains uneven despite growing exposure to climate risks and communication. While climate inaction is often explained as a result of weak concern, less attention has been given to whether inaction itself shapes how people interpret climate responsibility and information. Drawing on cognitive dissonance theory, this study examines climate inaction from a behavior-to-attitude perspective, proposing that limited action may trigger self-justifying processes that distance individuals from climate risks and shape climate-related information engagement. A nationwide online survey of 500 Malaysian adults was analysed using structural equation modelling. Results broadly supported the proposed model: climate inaction was associated with moral disengagement, greater psychological distance, lower climate concern, and higher climate skepticism, while climate information seeking played a complex role in these attitudinal outcomes. The findings suggest that climate inaction may actively sustain future disengagement by reshaping psychological and communicative responses to climate change.

articles.title: Understanding Human-Technology Interaction: Models, Ethics, and the Future of Control
articles.authors: Kristina Sabrina Weißmüller; Pascal König
articles.abstract: Drawing on the latest interdisciplinary insights on human-technology interaction, this conceptual chapter explains key perspectives and frameworks of how people adopt, interact with, and experience technology. After introducing core ethical aspects of technology, the chapter takes a critical stance on the promises and pitfalls of disruptive technological advancement—including but not limited to AI. Combining organizational, socio-psychological, and ethical perspectives on human-technology interaction, fundamental challenges of human control over digital technologies—which are not only tools but also agents and infrastructures—are explained. Based on this literature review, the chapter highlights and explains important blind spots within the commonly used models of human-technology interaction and discusses unresolved issues of user-technology value alignment, accountability, and control.

articles.title: The Moral Question: Norms and Anti-System Politics
articles.authors: Daniel A. N. Goldstein; Tore Wig
articles.abstract: Do moral norms influence citizens' tolerance of anti-system behavior by politicians? Although moral norms are often invoked as guardrails, their measurement and causal influence in politics remain understudied. We theorize the link between moral norms, personal judgments, and attitudinal consequences and test our theory in a pre-registered survey experiment with financial incentives. Respondents faced moral trade-offs concerning personal scandals, political violence, corruption, and anti-democratic actions. Using a second-order approach to elicit beliefs about what others consider morally wrong, we find that norms against democratic violations are descriptively weaker than norms against scandal and corruption; both lag behind norms against violence. Causal evidence shows that norms primarily shift judgments when respondents face large discrepancies from their initial perceived norm and have strong conformity motives. However, this largely does not translate into attitudinal shifts. The findings shed light on the concept of moral norms and detail their consequences and limitations for politics.

articles.title: The Disappearance of the Social?  A Conceptual Critique of the Para- and Post-Social Media Debate
articles.authors: Miira Hill
articles.abstract: Recent contributions by danah boyd (2026) and Törnberg & Rogers (2026) have argued that social media platforms have undergone a fundamental transformation: what once enabled reciprocal social interaction has become a landscape of parasocial observation, algorithmic broadcasting, and post-social fragmentation. This essay takes these empirical diagnoses seriously while subjecting their conceptual foundations to critical scrutiny. It argues that both contributions operate with an implicitly normative and analytically underdetermined concept of sociality, one that reduces a theoretically complex category to a historically particular manifestation. Drawing on the sociology of knowledge (Berger & Luckmann), communicative constructivism (Knoblauch, Keller), actor-network theory (Latour), feminist materialism (Haraway), and the concept of object-centred sociality (Knorr Cetina), the essay argues that sociality was never independent of its material and technical mediations, and that the relationship to objects, algorithms, and non-human actors is itself a form of sociality rather than its absence. A further objection is epistemological: ‘social media’ was never a scientifically precise category but an ethnocategory, an industry term and journalistic shorthand whose normative charge has been unreflectively adopted as an analytical framework. The essay concludes that the current debate diagnoses less the end of ‘social media’ than the crisis of a never-sufficiently-defined scientific category, and calls for a more systematic engagement with theoretical resources, available both within and beyond communication research, that may help to specify what the social is, and where it resides in contemporary digital environments.

articles.title: Simplifying Sociology: When Complex Writing Occurs, Where It Matters, and How It Can Be Improved
articles.authors: Carsten Schwemmer; Sebastian Block; Sandra Jaworeck; Manuel Holz; Philipp Spielbauer
articles.abstract: Clarity and precision are vital in science communication, yet sociology is often criticized for using overly complex language. In this study, we examine the complexity of sociological writing using an original dataset of 1,700 full-text articles from three major German journals. We validate a composite measure to investigate complexity over time and across methodological orientations, which past work has shown to follow different research conventions. We find that peak textual complexity in sociological articles increases over time and is systematically related to methodological choices. Next, we examine the relationship between complexity and citation-based impact. In contrast with findings from other disciplines, our results provide no evidence for complex writing to either harm or boost the number of citations per article. Finally, we demonstrate a simple workflow that uses our composite measure alongside generative artificial intelligence. This approach can identify highly complex passages and support targeted revision, thereby contributing to clearer communication and accessibility in academia and beyond.

articles.title: These Four First: A Robust Endogenous Baseline for Relational Event Sequences
articles.authors: C. Ben Gibson
articles.abstract: Which endogenous interaction mechanisms belong in a baseline relational event model? We test for recurring endogenous patterns in 217 empirical interaction sequences spanning ten social domains, evaluating a twelve-term candidate set by forward AIC reduction. Four terms appear in the top-four selection path in over 60% of domains — interaction-order constraints (IOviolation, 94%), prior sender initiation (SndInitiation, 88%), rank-recency of the most recent reverse-dyad event (RecRecencyRank, 65%), and dyadic repetition (Repetition, 61%) — with substantially diminishing returns at step 4. Together they re- cover approximately 97% of the maximum AIC improvement achievable within this candi- date set and represent three distinct behavioral mechanisms: sequential ordering, dyadic persistence, and reverse-dyad recency. Notably absent from this baseline are imitation (preferential attachment driven by others’ targeting behavior) and triadic closure terms, despite their prominent roles in the network literature; both are absorbed by forward-dyad history once SndInitiation and Repetition enter the model. IOviolation’s dominance as the most consistently selected term reflects the near-universal importance of participation- shift norms across interaction settings, even those that are not conversational. The step-2 composition diverges ecologically: in high-visibility settings RecRecencyRank competes closely with IOviolation at step 2 (38% vs. 60%), while in low-visibility settings IOviola- tion dominates step 2 in 79% of domains. These four terms are not a sufficient model — institutional context, status, demographic attributes, and additional endogenous mech- anisms all shape interaction — but they are a consistent baseline that researchers can include with confidence before introducing substantive covariates. The cross-domain sta- bility of this four-term core — spanning private messaging, classroom interaction, and gang violence — hints at a compact set of dyadic mechanisms that may constitute near- universal building blocks of directed social interaction.

articles.title: High Agreement, Different Stories: How LLM Classifiers Reshape Demographic Patterns in Survey Data
articles.authors: Chris Soria
articles.abstract:
What we learn from open-ended survey data depends on who—or what—does the coding. Large Language Models (LLMs) promise to democratize qualitative analysis, but do high agreement rates translate into equivalent thematic findings? This study compares eight LLMs to human annotators on a multilabel coding task using 3,200 responses from the UC Berkeley Social Networks Study, comprising over 19,000 coding decisions. Although LLM-human reliability does not match human-human reliability overall, LLMs approach human performance on simpler tasks and can serve as useful additional coders for generating consensus labels. Compared to a gold-standard human consensus, models achieve 82–97% per-category agreement, but macro F1 is lower and response-level similarity is lower still: even the best model reproduces the full human label set for fewer than 60% of responses. Yet high agreement masks thematic divergence. Models systematically over-identify themes, assigning 67% more categories per response, especially for categories requiring greater interpretive judgment.

Models also show lower agreement for some demographic groups. These gaps are partly explained by response characteristics such as length, clarity, and atypicality, and some persist after controls, with implications for studies of populations whose response styles diverge from the corpus average. At the sample level, models largely preserve the overall thematic narrative: human and model category rankings correlate strongly (pooled Spearman's ρ=0.75), and top-performing models achieve approximately 80% directional agreement on demographic patterns. Concrete behavioral questions, such as reasons for moving or strategies for making friends, show especially strong alignment. Yet systematic over-classification can still shift narratives about how specific groups behave, leading researchers to report patterns that the human gold standard does not support.

articles.title: Social Culture in the Era of Generative AI: Structural Transformation, Cognitive Reshaping, and Future Perspectives
articles.authors: Jincheng Zhang
articles.abstract: The rapid development of generative artificial intelligence (GAI) is profoundly reshaping the fundamental structure of human society and culture. From knowledge production methods to daily communication patterns, from cultural content creation to value formation mechanisms, AI is transforming from a "tool-based technology" into a "cultural force." This paper systematically analyzes the impact mechanism of generative AI on contemporary social culture from four dimensions: social structure, cultural production, identity recognition, and ethical order, and further explores the cognitive reshaping and future development trends it has triggered. The study argues that generative AI not only changes the efficiency of cultural dissemination but also restructures the relationship between "humans, technology, and culture," enabling social culture to enter a new stage of "co-generation."

articles.title: A demographic theory of similarity-biased social learning
articles.authors: Alejandro Pérez Velilla; Paul E. Smaldino
articles.abstract: We develop a demographic theory of similarity-biased social learning that formalizes our understanding of when and why individuals should preferentially copy others that look or act like them. We build an evolutionary model in which individuals can either learn on their own or copy others from a demonstrator pool that contains varying proportions of in-group and out-group members, and where group tags can be more or less informative about local knowledge. We find that where social learning becomes common, selection favors copying biases that track the direction of informational advantage---toward the group that tends to be better adapted to local conditions, including an anti-similarity bias when tags are negatively associated with local correctness (as may be the case for some immigrant communities). We also find conditions in which a similarity bias can stabilize social learning when such learners are already common, but not when they are rare, with implications for the role of group identities in cultural evolution.  We discuss implications for understanding parochialism as risk aversion, majority-minority dynamics, the sociology of immigration, and the lasting impacts of colonialism.

articles.title: Autonomy, Adolescents, and Artificial Intelligence
articles.authors: Michael J. S. Beauvais
articles.abstract:
In this essay, forthcoming 32 B.U. J. Sci. & Tech. L. (2026), I explore the potential implications of adolescent use of AI assistants on the development of adolescents’ capacities for autonomy. Thinking through these issues from the perspective of first principles is, I believe, needed in order to sharpen our understanding of some of the issues AI assistants pose for young people. A clear analysis will suggest which legal or policy prescriptions should follow. 

I first give an overview of adolescent use of AI assistants, noting their popularity and the variety of use contexts. Drawing upon the work of Catriona Mackenzie, I then outline a relational conception of autonomy that conceives of autonomy as having three distinct dimensions: self-determination, self-governance, and self-authorization. The precision of Mackenzie’s conception allows us to more carefully consider how different uses and contexts of technological use can affect individuals’ capacities for autonomy. Using this three-dimensional conception of autonomy, I propose three potential interventions for law and policy: thinking beyond harms, substantive privacy protections, and autonomy impact assessments.

articles.title: Love, Loathing, and Loyalty: Affective Micropublics on Political TikTok
articles.authors: Matthias Revers; Julia Niemann-Lenz; Lasse Rodeck; Hendrik Meyer
articles.abstract: This study examines how affective micropublics emerge in TikTok comment sections during the 2025 German federal election campaign. We conceptualize comment sections as digital micropublics: localized but networked arenas in which users enact publicness through affirmation, opposition, and emotional expression. Empirically, the analysis draws on 12,685 videos from 1,274 political accounts, engagement metrics, and more than two million comments, examined through LLM-based topic classification and automated stance and emotion classification. Methodologically, the study shows how LLM-assisted analysis can move beyond content classification to trace how political messages are taken up, emotionally charged, and redirected in audience interaction. Results show that campaign-related content dominates political TikTok, followed by economic issues, democracy and the political system, social policy, and migration. Yet visibility and audience response vary sharply across parties. The AfD combines high posting frequency with strongly supportive and emotionally aligned comment environments, while centrist and governing parties face predominantly critical or mocking responses. Cross-party hostility analysis further reveals asymmetric patterns of animosity, with negative engagement directed toward specific political opponents rather than distributed randomly. The study shows that TikTok functions simultaneously as a space of participatory visibility and affective polarization. Conceptually, it advances digital micropublics as a framework for analyzing how platformed publics form around political content, emotional alignment, and antagonistic relations.

articles.title: Inside of Structural Economy: An Approach from Informal Angola Economy.
articles.authors: Nerhum Sandambi
articles.abstract: This approach focuses specifically on the behaviour of the economy, that is, its economic structure, with particular emphasis on the formal and informal sectors. Thus, the evidence points specifically to the plausible existence of an informal economy in Angola, the significance of which is in fact closely linked to the very nature of the determining factors that are, by their very nature, relevant. A key feature of the informal economic structure relates, above all, to the fact that there is, for example, a limited capacity on the part of the state to generate wealth, as evidenced by  significant tax losses; for instance, Angola incurs an annual tax loss of 6.9 billion dollars. On the other hand, however, it nevertheless represents a significant opportunity cost in terms of public policy; thus, the analysis suggests  that, to the extent that there is a substantial expansion of the informal economy, on the other hand, there is a particular trend towards the continuous emergence of new informal professions. Informal markets exhibit characteristics that are specific to them; for example, most informal workers do not have fixed monthly incomes, and income levels depend directly on daily informal economic activities. Thus, the greater the flow of informal economic activities, the higher the income levels of economic agents will naturally be. On the other hand, approach show in particular, that, many Underdevelopment Countries, they not give attention to informal economy, where, on majority of this countries see the informal economy as statistics, the politics and institutions not exist in these Countries, in particular.

articles.title: Food Insecurity and the Accumulation of Material  Hardship: Evidence from the CPS, SIPP, and PSID
articles.authors: Thomas O'Rourke
articles.abstract: Food insecurity is widely used to identify vulnerable households, yet the literature remains divided over what exactly it captures. One perspective treats food insecurity as a proxy for economic disadvantage—a view supported by cross-sectional associations but complicated by recent causal evidence. A second perspective contends that food insecurity reflects subjective perceptions of hardship rather than objective economic deprivation, but empirical evidence on this hypothesis is limited. A third perspective emphasizes that food insecurity primarily depends on household experiences of other forms of hardship. Using longitudinal data from three nationally representative surveys, this study adjudicates between these perspectives by examining how food insecurity changes within households as economic resources, subjective wellbeing, and material hardship change over time. I find that food insecurity is highly stable within households over time and is only weakly responsive to changes in economic resources and subjective wellbeing. By contrast, food insecurity increases when households experience other direct forms of hardship, including worsening housing conditions and greater difficulty paying bills. These findings challenge the idea that households substitute one form of hardship for another when navigating scarcity. Instead, food insecurity appears to coincide with other forms of material hardship: households experiencing hardship in one domain are often exposed to hardship in others.

articles.title: Neurodivergent Intelligence Framework: A Dimensional Framework for Understanding Cognition in Neurodivergent Profiles and Psychiatric Risk
articles.authors: Rochelle Bernard
articles.abstract: Emerging evidence in neurodevelopmental research increasingly indicates that individuals diagnosed with Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) and Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) may demonstrate amplified pattern recognition capabilities as a core cognitive feature. Historically, these capacities have been framed as deficits within prevailing diagnostic and clinical traditions rather than as expressions of differentiated cognitive architecture. However, the field has yet to develop a framework that accounts for the distinct types of capabilities across these profiles or the developmental conditions that shape their expression.
